title: Support Multi-Chat in Codex harness
document:
Support Multi-Chat in Codex harness

Summary

Add Multi-Chat support to the Codex harness so it matches the Copilot experience.

Background

- The Multi-Chat feature (previously called Sub-Sessions) is currently available for the Copilot harness and works in AHP sessions for Copilot.
- Team feedback and user studies have shown confusion around the feature and its name; the UX should be consistent across harnesses rather than Copilot-only.
- Codex AHP sessions do not currently show the Multi-Chat UI.

Proposal

- Implement Multi-Chat in the Codex harness with parity to the Copilot implementation and UX.
- Ensure Multi-Chat is available in AHP sessions for Codex as it is for Copilot.
- Minimize agent integration effort (sessions should be transparent to agents where possible), and document any agent-facing changes required.
- Update tests, e2e scenarios, and relevant documentation to reflect Multi-Chat availability in the Codex harness.

Acceptance criteria

- Multi-Chat UI appears and behaves the same in the Codex harness as it does in Copilot.
- Multi-Chat is available and functional in AHP sessions for Codex.
- Agent/handler code for Codex either requires no changes or any required changes are documented and minimal.
- Relevant tests and documentation updated.

Implementation notes

- Verify whether sessions need to be linked or if the agent surface can remain unchanged (transparent sessions).
- Coordinate UX changes with the Copilot harness team to ensure consistency and avoid repeating prior confusion around naming/behavior.
- Add testing steps to validate in manual and automated tests across the Codex harness and AHP sessions.

---

Split from #322776 (Claude). This issue tracks the Codex harness.

[View original Slack conversation](https://vscodeteam.slack.com/archives/C09MVCZKLP4/p1782230045706259?thread_ts=1782230045.706259&cid=C09MVCZKLP4)

title: Increase Settings Sync limit for `keybindings.json` to 200kb
document:
Increase Settings Sync limit for `keybindings.json` to 200kb

## Problem
When using comprehensive keyboard layout extensions or modal editing wrappers like [Master Key](https://haberdashpi.github.io/vscode-master-key/), the `keybindings.json` file size can drastically increase due to the sheer volume of generated shortcuts for various modes and contexts. 

Currently, VS Code strictly enforces a 100kb limit on the keyboard shortcuts file for Settings Sync, throwing the following error and disabling keyboard settings synchronization:
> Disabled syncing keyboard shortcuts because size of the keyboard shortcuts file to sync is larger than 100kb. Please open the file and reduce the size and enable sync

This hard limit penalizes power users who rely on heavy keybinding configurations or ecosystem-altering extensions.

### Describe the solution you'd like
I would like VS Code to increase the maximum allowed file size for `keybindings.json` in Settings Sync from 100kb to 200kb (or higher). This slight overhead would easily accommodate complex, extension-generated keybinding maps without risking server performance.

### Describe alternatives you've considered
* **Manually stripping keybindings:** Not viable as it breaks the functionality provided by key-mapping extensions.
* **Disabling keybindings sync:** A frustrating workaround since keyboard shortcuts are a core part of an engineer's workflow synchronization across multiple machines.

### Additional context
#86178

title: Autopilot/Bypass silently skips user-requested high-risk actions with no opt-out
document:
Autopilot/Bypass silently skips user-requested high-risk actions with no opt-out

## Type
Feature request / behavior gap

## Environment
- VS Code: 1.126
- Copilot Chat: latest
- Permission level: Autopilot (`chat.permissions.default: "autopilot"`, `chat.autopilot.enabled: true`)
- Also set: `chat.tools.global.autoApprove: true`, terminal `/.*/` catch-all auto-approve, `chat.tools.terminal.ignoreDefaultAutoApproveRules: true`

## Summary
Even at the **most permissive** documented configuration (Autopilot), the agent **silently skips** irreversible/destructive tool calls that I *explicitly asked it to perform* (e.g. removing a file, removing a database row). The agent returns:

> Autopilot skipped this tool call because it was automatically assessed as high-risk: ... The action was not performed.

There is **no documented setting** to opt out of this secondary risk gate.

## Why this is confusing (docs vs. behavior)
The [approvals docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agents/approvals) state that Autopilot and Bypass Approvals:

> "...bypass manual approval prompts, **including for potentially destructive actions like file edits, terminal commands, and external tool calls.**"

But in practice a *separate* risk classifier re-blocks destructive actions **after** the permission level is applied. So the documented "approve everything" promise is not honored, and there is no setting (documented or surfaced) to disable this second layer.

## Repro
1. Set permission level to **Autopilot** (or Bypass Approvals).
2. Set `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` to a `/.*/` catch-all and `chat.tools.terminal.ignoreDefaultAutoApproveRules: true`.
3. Ask the agent to remove a file (e.g. "remove scripts/foo.py"), or to remove a row from a database via an MCP tool.
4. Observe: the tool call is auto-skipped as "high-risk" and never executes, despite the explicit request and the most permissive settings.

Notably, the classifier appears to key partly on surface tokens: a command literally containing the removal keyword gets blocked, while a functionally-identical script with a neutral name slips through, so the gate is inconsistent rather than a reliable safety guarantee.

## Expected
One of:
- Autopilot/Bypass honor an **explicit** user request to perform a destructive action (matching the documented behavior), **or**
- Expose a setting (e.g. `chat.tools.allowHighRiskActions` or per-tool `eligibleForAutoApproval`) to opt out of the high-risk gate, **or**
- At minimum, fall back to a **single confirmation prompt** instead of silently skipping with no way to proceed.

## Related
- #313210: "Copilot easily breaking out of sandbox mode" (the inverse concern; shows the team is actively tuning this line)
- #303174: "autopilot mode should allow curl in terminal" (same theme: autopilot more restrictive than expected)

title: Feature request: React-Grab-like customizable element-selection context for the integrated browser
document:
Feature request: React-Grab-like customizable element-selection context for the integrated browser

I’d like to propose a feature for the integrated browser that lets a repo or page provide custom context when a user selects an element and chooses “Add Element to Chat”.

In short, it would enable [react-grab](https://www.react-grab.com/) like functionality through a hook with the code defined in repo. There are a lot of "grab" libraries designed for different web frameworks: [react](https://www.react-grab.com), [angular](https://angular-grab.com/), [svelte](https://meshkore.com/agent/heicg-svelte-grab), [solidjs](https://github.com/omniaura/solid-grab), [vue](https://vue-grab.vercel.app/), etc... This feature would enable these tools to work inside the integrated browser directly. Each framework would have its own npm package to enable the hook on the browser side.

Today, the flow in `browserEditorChatFeatures.ts` builds a standardized snippet from the selected DOM element (HTML, path, dimensions, computed styles, etc.) and sends that into chat. That is useful, but it is not flexible enough for repos with specialized needs.

As a frontend developer, I want to be able to customize the text snippet that gets attached to chat so I can provide richer, repo-specific context to agents. This should make agent-driven edits more accurate and reduce the need for manual follow-up.

Example use case:
- In a React repo, I want the browser selection flow to call custom code that inspects the React virtual DOM / component tree and returns a structured component-context summary.
- I also want to customize CSS output, for example to de-transform CSS module class names before they are attached to chat, or to remove hashed griffel class names.

Proposal:
- When an element is selected in the integrated browser, VS Code should attempt to invoke a page-provided hook that can customize the context payload.
- The hook should be available for both:
 - the manual “Add Element to Chat” flow from the integrated browser UI, and
 - the browser-tool / agent-driven selection path, so the same repo-specific context can be provided regardless of how the selection was triggered.
- The hook could be exposed as an optional global function on the page, for example:
 - `window.__vscodeIntegratedBrowserGetContext?.(elementData)`
- The hook should be able to return either:
 - a string snippet to append to the existing context, or
 - a richer structured payload that VS Code can render into the chat attachment.
- If the hook is not provided, the current built-in behavior remains unchanged.
- For discoverability, we could add a small docs/help entry or info popover near the element-selection button in the integrated browser UI.

Why this is valuable:
- Different repos need different context
- Frontend teams often want framework-specific summaries
- Better context should lead to better agent suggestions and edits

Possible follow-up work:
- Support repo-specific injection via a script already loaded in the page
- Provide a documented API shape and examples
- Add a UI hint/docs link near the selection action
- Add some default formatters for common libraries like react

---

You can contact me directly on Teams at my Microsoft alias

title: Proposed API: opt-in, consent-gated camera/microphone access for extension webviews
document:
Proposed API: opt-in, consent-gated camera/microphone access for extension webviews

### Problem

Extension webviews currently can't use `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia` at all — the webview iframe carries no `allow="camera; microphone"`, and the Electron permission handler denies `media` for `vscode-webview://` origins. That's intentional ("media must not be silently accessible", #113916), but it leaves **no opt-in path** for legitimate, user-initiated scenarios: live device/camera preview, audio/video capture, QR scanning, in-editor calls, voice/accessibility input, hardware diagnostics.

Today the only workaround is to spawn `ffmpeg` in the extension host and stream frames back into the webview — heavy and platform-specific, just to reach hardware the OS already exposes.

### Precedent

There's now an active precedent: consent-gated, per-origin, revocable camera/mic/location permissions for the integrated browser (#299521 / PR #322639), **currently in Insiders**. This proposes extending that same safety model to extension webviews.

### Proposed shape (designed to answer the "no silent access" concern)

- Extension opts in via a proposed API (`webviewMediaDevices`) and declares devices on the webview — `mediaDevices: ['camera', 'microphone']` — visible at install time, off by default.
- The iframe `allow` is injected **only** for declaring webviews (per-device).
- First `getUserMedia` triggers a **one-time consent prompt**; the grant is stored **per extension** and is revocable.
- No declaration → no `allow`; no consent → no access.

### Working PoC (desktop), behind the proposed API

Verified end-to-end (declare → consent prompt → `getUserMedia` → live camera/mic in the webview). Deliberately minimal; happy to rework to your conventions, not asking to merge as-is:
https://github.com/chrisdp/vscode/commit/5b9064c626cbc87554c65f0639fa9062d86b72e6

The net-new piece vs the browser work is mainly the iframe `allow` injection (on both the outer webview iframe and the inner content frame, for Permissions-Policy delegation) plus the per-extension grant; the consent UI/store could be reused.

### The ask

Would the team be open to this direction? If so, we'll productionize (persisted/revocable grants + management UI, renderer-brokered consent, remote/web/WSL hosts).

### Related
- #250568 — Permissions-policy violation report (microphone not allowed in a webview)
- #113916 — earlier decision that webview media should be blocked without a prompt
- #299521 / PR #322639 — consent-gated browser permissions (in Insiders), the model this builds on

title: Copilot CLI agent unusable on GitHub Enterprise — CopilotCLIModels model fetch returns 401 Bad credentials
document:
Copilot CLI agent unusable on GitHub Enterprise — CopilotCLIModels model fetch returns 401 Bad credentials

## Bug Report

**VS Code:** 1.126.0
**Copilot Chat:** bundled built-in
**Copilot CLI:** 1.0.63
**Auth provider:** `github-enterprise` (`*.ghe.com`)
**OS:** macOS arm64

---

### What happens

The "Copilot CLI" agent type in the Agents / Chat Sessions view cannot be used. On every window open, the extension fails to fetch the CLI model list:

The error fires **immediately after** a successful enterprise auth:

This repeats on every window across multiple VS Code sessions.

---

### What works fine

- **Normal Copilot Chat** — succeeds against the enterprise account (e.g. `claude-opus-4.8 | success`)
- **Standalone Copilot CLI** — `copilot -p "..."` works fully, bills AI Credits, with no auth errors
- **GitHub API** — `gh api user` returns the correct enterprise identity; `gh auth status` shows active session

Only `CopilotCLIModels` 401s.

---

### Root cause hypothesis

VS Code is configured as:

The `CopilotCLIModels` fetch appears to route to **github.com**'s Copilot user-info endpoint while presenting the **enterprise token**, which github.com rejects with 401. The main chat and standalone CLI both work because they respect the enterprise host routing; `CopilotCLIModels` does not.

With no model list, the CLI agent type silently fails to initialize and is unusable in the Agents view.

---

### Steps to reproduce

1. Configure VS Code with a GitHub Enterprise host (`github-enterprise.uri` + `authProvider: github-enterprise`)
2. Sign in with an enterprise account that has an active Copilot seat
3. Open the Chat Agents view and attempt to use the Copilot CLI agent type
4. Observe in **Output → GitHub Copilot Chat**:
 

---

### Expected behaviour

`CopilotCLIModels` should route through the configured enterprise host so the Copilot CLI agent type is usable on GitHub Enterprise.

---

### Workaround

Use the standalone `copilot` CLI in an integrated terminal — auth works correctly there.

title: Isolation picker shows only 'Folder' (grayed out) when folder is not git-backed
document:
Isolation picker shows only 'Folder' (grayed out) when folder is not git-backed

- VS Code Version: main
- OS Version: 

1. Create or open a folder that is not a git repository.
2. Open the isolation picker (the UI that lets you choose how to open the workspace/isolation target).
3. Observe that only the "Folder" option is shown and it appears grayed out; the "Worktree" option is not shown and there is no indication why.

Expected behavior:

- The isolation picker should indicate why Worktree is unavailable when the folder is not git-backed. Preferably both options (Folder and Worktree) could be shown, and when the user selects Worktree, VS Code could offer to initialize a git repository in that folder (git init) or provide a clear action to enable worktrees.

Actual behavior:

- Only "Folder" is shown (grayed out) with no explanation, which is confusing for users who expect worktrees to be available.

Proposed fix / suggestion:

- Show both options in the isolation picker. If the folder is not git-backed, either:
 - Disable the Worktree option but show a clear message explaining that a git repository is required and offer an inline action to initialize git in the folder, or
 - Allow selecting Worktree and prompt to initialize a git repository (run `git init`) in that folder so the user can proceed.

Notes:

- This came up during internal testing of a new repository — the folder being new (no git) made the picker behavior confusing. Adding an explanatory message or an init action should improve discoverability and UX.

[View original Slack conversation](https://vscodeteam.slack.com/archives/C09MVCZKLP4/p1782750547562709?thread_ts=1782750547.562709&cid=C09MVCZKLP4)

title: Copilot run_in_terminal sandbox dependency installer is hardcoded to apt, breaks on Arch/Linux non-Debian distros
document:
Copilot run_in_terminal sandbox dependency installer is hardcoded to apt, breaks on Arch/Linux non-Debian distros

### Context
Using Copilot Chat terminal tools on Arch Linux with sandboxed execution enabled.

### Problem
When the terminal tool enters the sandbox dependency install/remediation flow, it attempts to run:

`sudo apt install -y `

On Arch (and other non-Debian distros), this fails because `apt` is not available. The user-facing result is:

`Sandbox dependency installation failed (exit code ...). The command was not executed.`

### Repro
1. On Arch Linux, ensure `bwrap` and `socat` are installed (for example via `pacman`).
2. Use Copilot Chat terminal tool (`run_in_terminal`) with sandboxed execution flow enabled.
3. Trigger a path that invokes missing dependency installation/remediation.
4. Observe attempted use of `apt` and failure.

### Expected
Dependency installation/remediation should be distro-aware (e.g. `pacman` on Arch, `dnf` on Fedora, `zypper` on openSUSE, etc.), or avoid shelling out to distro package managers directly and instead provide actionable instructions.

### Actual
Installer command is hardcoded to `apt`.

### Source reference
In `src/vs/workbench/contrib/terminalContrib/chatAgentTools/common/terminalSandboxService.ts`, `installMissingSandboxDependencies(...)` currently uses:

`sudo apt install -y ${depsList}`

This appears to be the root cause for Linux distro mismatch.

### Suggestion
- Detect distro/package manager at runtime and choose appropriate command.
- If unsupported/unknown distro, surface a targeted prompt with a copyable command suggestion instead of assuming `apt`.
- Optionally allow a configurable package install command for enterprise/custom environments.

Thanks!

title: Intermittant failure launching debugging - GDB: Failed to set controlling terminal: Operation not permitted
document:
Intermittant failure launching debugging - GDB: Failed to set controlling terminal: Operation not permitted

- VS Code Version: 
- OS Version: 

No idea how to make it occur... it appears infrequently and randomly.

Trying to debug a C++ application using gdb from the Visual Code GUI.

Platform Ubuntu 26.04, visual code 1.122 (happens on 1.126 as well). gdb 17.1.

This is a very frustrating bug... when it occurs nothing seems to resolve it.... until what I discovered just now.

I had Visual Code just sat there with this error in the terminal window
**"&"\342\232\240\357\270\217 warning: GDB: Failed to set controlling terminal: Operation not permitted\n""**
while I was doing something else eventually gdb woke up and I could see I could step \ continue without issue.

I figured out that when you launch a debugging session, you get this error message., and then after about 400 seconds, gdb activity happens. gdb is trrying to obtain Ubuntu gdb symbols from a remove internet server.

As usual with a bug issue microsoft are useless. Instead of highlighting the solution they just complain its not our code.

So I found the working solution - see https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-cpptools/issues/13667 for overview.

The jist of which is... Prevent gdb from trying to talk to remote symbols servers by adding this into your "launch.json" file -->

I hope that saves others from wasting hours of time trying to fix this issue.

title: Mermaid Markdown preview flashes then disappears; parser receives rendered CSS/SVG instead of diagram source
document:
Mermaid Markdown preview flashes then disappears; parser receives rendered CSS/SVG instead of diagram source

### VS Code Version
1.127.0-insider

### OS Version
Windows host + WSL2 Linux

### Repro Steps
1. Open a Markdown file containing a Mermaid code block.
2. Open Markdown Preview.
3. Diagram appears briefly, then disappears.
4. In failing state, preview may show error:
 "No diagram type detected matching given configuration for text: #dmermaid-... @keyframes ..."

### Expected Behavior
Mermaid diagram remains visible in Markdown Preview.

### Actual Behavior
Preview flashes, then diagram is removed or parser fails because rendered CSS/SVG is treated as Mermaid source.

### Suspected Area
Builtin extension: mermaid-markdown-features, file markdown-preview-out/index.js
Function: renderMermaidElement + rerender lifecycle in renderMermaidBlocksInElement.

### Patch/Workaround That Fixed It Locally
I applied a local runtime patch in both VS Code runtime copies (server + UI runtime):

1) Avoid destructive global cleanup that removes existing rendered nodes during rerender.
- Removed cleanup loop over ".mermaid > svg"
- Removed cleanup loop removing parent IDs starting with "dmermaid"

2) Make source recovery robust when container text is polluted by rendered output.
- In renderMermaidElement, detect polluted text patterns (for example "#dmermaid-", "@keyframes", "flowchartTitleText").
- Preserve canonical Mermaid source in dataset.vscodeMermaidSource.
- Reuse canonical source (or prior vscodeContext.mermaidSource fallback) instead of parsing rendered CSS/SVG.

Pseudo diff idea:

### Notes
After this patch, Mermaid preview is stable and no longer disappears.

title: Test: Windows agent terminal sandboxing
document:
Test: Windows agent terminal sandboxing

Refs #323224

- [ ] windows @vijayupadya

Complexity: 4

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323515%0A%0A&assignees=dileepyavan)

---

## Context

Windows agent terminal sandboxing uses the MXC runtime (`wxc-exec.exe`) . This test plan item validates that agent shell commands on Windows are wrapped through MXC when sandboxing is enabled, that workspace/temp filesystem access behaves as expected, configured filesystem rules are honored, and explicit unsandboxed execution does not happen silently.

## Setup

- Use a Windows machine preferebly a VM as this needs windows insider version. More details [here](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/windowsinsider/getting-started).
- Open a workspace folder.
- Enable agent terminal sandboxing:
 - `chat.agent.sandbox.enabled`: `on`
- For Agent Host testing, use the Agent Host/custom terminal tool path if available.

[Create issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%323515%0A%0A&assignees=dileepyavan)

## Test Steps

### 1. Verify a sandboxed shell command runs

1. Open Chat or the Agents Window.
2. Start a local Agent Host or Copilot agent session.
3. Ask the agent to run a simple shell command, for example:
 - `echo WINDOWS_SANDBOX_TEST`
 - `pwd`
 - `dir`
4. Verify the command completes successfully.

**Expected:**

- The command output is returned to chat.
- The command is reported/logged as sandboxed.
- On Windows, the wrapped command/config path uses MXC / `wxc-exec.exe`, not the POSIX `sandbox-runtime`.

### 2. Verify workspace write access

1. Ask the agent to create a temporary file in the workspace, for example:
 - `echo hello > .sandbox-windows-test.txt`
2. Ask the agent to read the file back.
3. Ask the agent to delete it.

**Expected:**

- Writing inside the workspace succeeds.
- Reading the file succeeds.
- Cleanup succeeds.
- The sandbox config exposes the workspace as writable.

### 3. Verify filesystem restrictions outside the workspace

1. Ask the agent to read or write a path outside the allowed workspace/temp locations, for example under the user profile.
2. If a custom sandbox filesystem setting is configured, include a specific denied/read-only path using the setting `chat.agent.sandbox.fileSystem.windows`

**Expected:**

- Disallowed writes are blocked or fail.
- Allowed read-only paths can be read but not modified.
- The agent reports failures clearly and does not silently rerun the command unsandboxed.

### 4. Verify unsandboxed execution confirmation

1. Ask the agent to run a command with explicit unsandboxed execution, or use a path that requires escaping the sandbox.
2. Test with unsandboxed commands allowed and disabled if possible.

**Expected:**

- If unsandboxed commands are allowed, VS Code asks for confirmation before running outside the sandbox.
- If unsandboxed commands are disabled, the command does not run unsandboxed and the failure is surfaced.

### 5. Verify shell/environment behavior

1. Run commands in PowerShell that depend on normal Windows environment setup, for example:
 - `$env:PATH`
 - `$env:USERPROFILE`
 - `Get-Location`
2. Optionally test with a non-default PowerShell path if available.

**Expected:**

- The shell launches with the expected environment.
- The working directory is the workspace.
- Basic PowerShell commands work inside the sandbox.

title: Explorer context menu command target depends on selection size
document:
Explorer context menu command target depends on selection size

I discovered a surprising Explorer behavior where the target of a context menu command depends on the size of the current selection.

When a context menu is invoked on a file that is not part of the current selection:

- No selection → command operates on the clicked file.
- Selection contains 1 different file → command operates on the clicked file.
- Selection contains 2+ different files → command operates on the selected files and ignores the clicked file.

The same interaction therefore produces different results depending only on the number of selected items. This feels inconsistent because the existing selection is either respected or ignored based solely on its size.

Besides being surprising, this can also lead to unexpected command execution. For example, a multi-selection may exist outside the current viewport and may not be visible when a user right-clicks another file, or the parent folder of a selection may be collapsed. In that situation, commands such as Copy, Cut, Paste, Delete, operate on the hidden selection. The selection may have been made some time ago and forgotten. 

To avoid this behavior, users must click the target file or press Esc to clear the selection before executing context menu commands. It feels that the selection should always be ignored when a context menu is invoked for an item outside the selection.

Is this behavior intentional? If so, what is the rationale for treating a single selected item differently from a multi-selection when the context menu is invoked outside the selection?

title: Inconsistent plugin+mcp behavior in VS Code
document:
Inconsistent plugin+mcp behavior in VS Code

### Description

Two inconsistencies between VS Code Copilot and Copilot CLI / Copilot App:

a) Copilot CLI/App by default set the cwd of a plugin-installed MCP server to the plugin root. VS Code sets the cwd to the user home folder.

b) Copilot CLI/App supports {PLUGIN_ROOT} and {CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} in .mcp.json, and will replace either, regardless of the origin of the plugin (Copilot/Claude/Open Plugin). VS Code supports those as well, but will only do the replacement depending on the origin of the plugin (Open Plugin for the former, Claude Plugin for the latter). Thus it won't do any replacement for Copilot Plugins. Documented here: https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agent-customization/agent-plugins#_plugin-environment-variables

At the moment it is impossible for an MCP server installed as a Copilot plugin to run any local scripts, since it has no way to specify their location (which is relative to the location where the plugin is installed). E.g. the MCP definition below will work in CLI and GitHub App but not in VSCode. 

Suggested Fix (for consistency with CLI and GitHub app (and SDK():
a) VS Code: Execute MCP server commands using the plugin install location as the cwd
b) VS Code: Apply {PLUGIN_ROOT} and {CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} replacements regardless of origin of the plugin.

I would suggest having a conversation with the Copilot team responsible for Copilot Plugins to fully specify the expected behavior of plugins and MCPs to ensure all implementations are consistent.

### Issue Details

Item|Value
---|---
Issue Category|Bug
Target|Agents Window
VS Code Version|Code 1.126.0 (7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a, 2026-06-23T01:46:13Z)
OS Version|Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

title: Add experiment-controlled setting for Markdown default editor in Agents window
document:
Add experiment-controlled setting for Markdown default editor in Agents window

The Agents window currently forces `vscode.markdown.editor` as the default editor for `*.md` files. We want to gate this behind an experiment so it can be rolled out gradually.

- New setting `workbench.editor.markdownDefaultEditorInAgentsWindow` (boolean, `tags: ['experimental']`, `experiment: { mode: 'startup' }`), default `false`.
- When enabled: `*.md` -> `vscode.markdown.editor`.
- When disabled (default): `*.md` -> `vscode.markdown.preview.editor` (prior behavior before #322703).

Tracking issue to monitor the experiment and decide on default rollout.

Selecting a custom agent at session creation is ignored — session starts with the default agent

When creating a new session in the Agents window and selecting a custom agent (e.g. **Agents Window Developer**) for a Copilot session from Agent Host, the created session does **not** use the selected custom agent — it starts with the **default** agent instead.

## Steps
1. Open the Agents window → New Sessions view.
2. Select the **vscode** folder using a Copilot session from Agent Host (this is needed to see the **Agents Window Developer** custom agent), and a worktree.
3. Select the **Agents Window Developer** custom agent.
4. Create/start the session.

🐛 The session runs the **default** agent, not the selected custom agent.

title: Invalid slash sub-command should respond with the valid options instead of a flat error
document:
Invalid slash sub-command should respond with the valid options instead of a flat error

Testing #323402

Running an unsupported sub-command, e.g. `/memory disable`, returns a bare "invalid command" error. The rejection is correct (`/memory` supports `on`/`off`/`show`, not `disable`), but the error is a dead end.

It would be much more helpful to **respond with the valid options** when a sub-command is unknown — for example:
- "Unknown option 'disable'. Did you mean: `on`, `off`, `show`?"
- or re-trigger completions for that command's sub-commands so the user can pick a valid one
- bonus: suggest the closest match (`disable` → `off`)

Note: the validation likely comes from the Copilot CLI / SDK rather than VS Code's completion layer, so this may be an upstream item (similar to #323401).

title: Slash command completions don't appear when / is not at the start of an empty input
document:
Slash command completions don't appear when / is not at the start of an empty input

Testing #323402

In a Copilot Agent Host session, slash command completions only appear when `/` is typed into a completely **empty** chat input. They do not trigger in these cases:

- Typing `/` in the **middle of a sentence** (when there is already text before the `/`).
- Typing `/` when there are **leading/trailing spaces** in the input (e.g. ` /` or text plus a space then `/`).

It would be helpful for completions to also trigger when `/` is typed mid-sentence or with surrounding spaces, so commands can be inserted without clearing/trimming the input first.

title: Slash command completions should indicate the source of each command
document:
Slash command completions should indicate the source of each command

Testing #323402

When typing `/` in a Copilot Agent Host session, the completion list shows the available slash commands but it's **hard to tell where each command comes from** — e.g. built-in Copilot CLI commands vs. ones contributed by extensions/plugins, MCP, or custom agents. They all look the same.

It would help to **indicate the source/origin** of each slash command in the completions list (for example a label, group heading, or icon), so users can tell which commands are native vs. contributed and avoid confusion when names overlap.

title: Agent files: improve the error message when `github/*` or `playwrite/*` are not found
document:
Agent files: improve the error message when `github/*` or `playwrite/*` are not found

The github and the playwrite mcp server have special short aliases in agent files: `github/*` and `playwrite/*`.
VS Code maps these aliases to the following real mcp server names: 'github/github-mcp-server', 'io.github.github/github-mcp-server', 'github-mcp-server' and tries to find these.
If not found, it shows ' Unknown tool 'github/*' will be ignored.'. 
It should give a better error message and even a quick fix.
To install these servers in VS Code:
- there's a built in github server that you can enable with the setting `github.copilot.chat.githubMcpServer.enabled`.
- To install from the marketplace, go to the extensions view, type `@mcp github`. The github mcp server should should up with id `io.github.github/github-mcp-server`

title: F2 Rename Symbol should not depend on Copilot credits
document:
F2 Rename Symbol should not depend on Copilot credits

F2 Rename Symbol stops working when Copilot credits are exhausted. 

`F2` should trigger VS Code’s normal `editor.action.rename` / language-server rename functionality, not Copilot for AI suggestions what to rename the symbol.

Expected behavior: Rename Symbol should work independently of Copilot subscription, quota, or credits.

Actual behavior: Copilot quota exhaustion appears to block or interfere with a core editor refactoring command.

Please keep AI rename/refactor features separate from the default F2 Rename Symbol command.

title: Evaluate Agent Host custom terminal tool vs SDK shell tool for chat terminal UX parity
document:
Evaluate Agent Host custom terminal tool vs SDK shell tool for chat terminal UX parity

For Agent Host Copilot sessions, would we be "Good" relying completely on CLI SDK's built-in shell tool, or continue to invest in custom terminal tool.

The reason this matters: VS Code's terminal is not just stdout/stderr. It is where users look when a command is slow, hung, waiting for input, asking for a secret, starting a server, or doing something surprising. If Agent Host only relies on the SDK shell tool, commands may still run, but we may lose "tight integration" and bunch of UX flows that chat had with integrated terminal. 

Current switch:

- `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled=false`: Copilot SDK sessions rely on the SDK shell behavior.
- `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled=true`: Agent Host overrides the SDK built-in shell tools with VS Code/Agent Host terminal-backed tools.

Important runtime context: Copilot CLI/runtime shell execution has moved away from `node-pty`-backed shell sessions toward direct `child_process.spawn` with stdio pipes. That is a good direction for predictable command output, but it also means the chat terminal experience can deviate quite a bit from our local agent harness atm.
 * Gaps are noted/listed down below in pros/cons section.

Also awkward: AHP itself treats terminals as first-class subscribable resources: https://microsoft.github.io/agent-host-protocol/guide/terminals.html. 

What we need to decide:

- Is the SDK/runtime shell tool enough for VS Code Agent Host sessions?
- Do we need the Agent Host custom terminal tool for VS Code-quality terminal UX?
- Or do we need a smaller SDK/API integration point so the SDK shell can still surface VS Code terminal affordances?

**Workflow tests**

Run each scenario three ways:

- Local agent / workbench `runInTerminal` path where VS Code owns the terminal.
- Agent Host with `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled=true`.
- Agent Host with `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled=false`, relying on the SDK shell tool.

For each test, record: Works / Partial / Missing, visible user behavior, model-visible output quality, accessibility notes, and whether the user can recover without another model turn.

- [ ] **Baseline command execution**: one-shot command returns correct stdout, stderr, and exit code.

- [ ] **Persistent shell state**: a separate follow-up command can observe state set by a previous command.

- [ ] **Python environment auto-activation**: agent shell execution uses the same Python environment a user would get from VS Code terminal activation. Related: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/323164

- [ ] **Interactive prompt**: the user or agent can answer a prompt in the same running process.
 - Side note: things like `sudo __` may take extra step or two: 

Not sure if this is something we can avoid by tweaking the prompt for the model now we are in non-pty world for CLI SDK built-in shell tool. Need to test other interactive workflow such as with installs and multiple prompts, etc

- [ ] **Sensitive prompt**: the user can type a secret directly into the terminal without sending it through the model.

- [ ] **Long-running command**: a slow command makes progress visible and can be focused while running.

- [ ] **Dev server / watcher**: a server/watch process can keep running while remaining visible and stoppable.

- [ ] **TTY-sensitive behavior**: we understand where non-TTY SDK execution helps and where it changes behavior.

- [ ] **REPL / TUI / alternate screen**: interactive programs are either supported or explicitly out of scope.

- [ ] **Large output and scrollback**: users can inspect large output without losing terminal affordances.

- [ ] **Multiline commands, heredocs, and pasted blocks**: multiline input executes exactly once and preserves bytes.

- [ ] **Hanging command / cancellation / recovery**: the user can tell what is happening and recover from a stuck process.

- [ ] **Terminal focus, reveal, hidden terminal, and telemetry**: the chat UI exposes an obvious way to inspect shell execution.

- [ ] **Session switch, reload, and terminal revival**: a terminal-backed command remains inspectable across chat/session transitions.

- [ ] **Default shell, profile, and environment parity**: agent shell behavior matches VS Code terminal expectations.

- [ ] **Accessibility-specific prompt flow**: screen reader users can understand that input is needed and act without guessing.

**Decision checklist**

- [ ] Complete the workflow matrix above for local/workbench terminal, Agent Host custom terminal, and SDK shell path.
- [ ] Decide whether missing terminal visibility/input is acceptable product behavior or a blocker for VS Code Agent Host.
- [ ] Identify which failures are resolvable via additional support from SDK.
- [ ] Decide whether `chat.agentHost.customTerminalTool.enabled` should remain experimental, become the supported/default path, or be replaced by a smaller SDK terminal integration API.
- [ ] Define minimum parity before disabling/removing the custom path: Focus Terminal, direct user input, prompt/accessibility behavior, long-running command visibility, cancellation/recovery, and terminal telemetry.
- [ ] If we keep the SDK shell default, define explicit UX for "no real terminal exists" so users do not think VS Code lost terminal support accidentally.

VS Code's terminal value is broader than command execution: users expect visible output, direct intervention, accessibility affordances, terminal scrollback, command reveal, profile/shell fidelity, and a way to recover when the agent gets it wrong. This issue tracks whether Agent Host needs a VS Code-owned custom terminal tool to preserve those workflows, and what must be true if we rely on the SDK shell tool instead.

title: [Test] BYOK Ollama Provider Deprecation
document:
[Test] BYOK Ollama Provider Deprecation

Refs: microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog/issues/323176

- [x] anyOS @ulugbekna 

Complexity: 1

Authors: @vritant24 

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323394%0A%0A&assignees=vritant24)

---

## Context

The internal Ollama BYOK provider is being deprecated in favor of the official extension. To nudge the users into using the official extension, three additions are made.

1. Ollama Provider marked as deprecated in the Provider list
2. If already used, the Ollama Provider Group Name has a link to install the extension
3. When a model from the Ollama provider is used, a notification is shown asking the user to migrate.

### Setup
- have Ollama installed, along with at least one model loaded.
docs.ollama.com/quickstart
- run `Ollama serve` and note the port number

## Test Steps

1. Open `Manage Language Models` View, and Click `Add Models`
2. Click `Ollama (deprecated) and set it up with the known port number on localhost.
3. The group should appear in the list in `Manage Language Models` and you should see the deprecated message and a link to install the official extension
4. go to the chat window, and select the Ollama model
5. Send a prompt to the model to start a chat. You should see a notification pop up to install the official extension

title: retrieval of remote server fails
document:
retrieval of remote server fails

Context
=======

`barley` is the "local" machine, amd64, Debian 11.
`VSCode` installed and updated via `.deb` on MS servers.

`faro` is the "remote" machine, M1 Mac mini, MacOS 26.5.1.
It has a `VSCode` client install (direct from MS), updated via the `VSCode` GUI update function. But I think the server install is completely separate.

Connect from `barley` to `faro` by `remote - SSH`, with `ssh-add` already run to avoid further prompts.

Steps
=====

1. Turned on `faro` at least a day ago. It has 2 user sessions going, though it has been inactive for hours.
2. Turned on `barley` and logged into my user account.
Set up the `ssh` agent. Downloaded a new version of `VSCode` via `aptitude`.
3. A few days later (to ensure the Apple Silicon version is available) launch `VSCode` on `faro`.
4. From the `VSCode` GUI (initially the connection indicator on the lower left) do a remote ssh connection to `faro`.
5. Select the `remote - SSH` `Output` window and watch.

Expected Behavior
================
The remote server is updated using most of my available bandwidth (~ 5.6Mbits) and the remote session proceeds.

Actual Behavior
=============
The download proceeds in fits and starts, getting generally slower until it times out completely after 26 minutes.
Log attached.

`VSCode` then falls back to a `local server download`, which I take to mean it will download the M1 server system to `barley` and then copy it to `faro`. This appears to work in about 5 minutes. (also in the log)

Comments
=========

This is not the first time I've encountered slow, and even failed, downloads for the remote server. While updating the client directly on `faro` is sometimes slow, it does not seem to have similar problems.

There have been recent changes, bugs and reworkings related to remote sessions in `VSCode`. Perhaps the update process is hitting some of the same channels? However, I'm pretty sure I saw similar problems *before* those updates.

There are a lot of places things could be going wrong:
 1. internal code of `VSCode`
 2. one of the helper applications on `MacOS`, e.g., `curl`.
 3. `MacOS` "features", e.g., it's very aggressive about shutting down if it thinks it's idle. It can cut off `ssh` sessions very quickly.
 4. Interactions with `homebrew` on `MacOS`. I don't know if there are any that concern the `VSCode` server.
 5. Hardware trouble in the machines, the NIC's, the cable,
 the DSL line and modem, the routers ....
 6. Problems with the host from which the server software is downloaded.
 7. Problems with my firewall (also running on `barley`, which functions as a router).
 
When I first encountered problems my theory was that the upstream servers were overloaded (item 6 above). Given that my latest attempt is several days after a general update, and given that this seems to happen specifically for the server download, that theory is looking doubtful.

As to item 1 (internal software) there is the additional issue that, to quote the log, 
`Support for architecture "arm64" is in preview`.

For item 2 (helper applications), note that the options understood by `MacOS`'s default `sshd` differ substantially from those used by `openssh`, my client on `Linux`. For that reason I sometimes invoke the `brew` version on `MacOS` (item 4). But I am not deliberately doing so for these connections.

At first glance item 5 (hardware) is not promising since the connections seem to work OK outside of the `VSCode server` download context.

Transcript Oddities
================

While the first download in the transcript was running I scrolled back in the `Output` window to see what had happened earlier, as well as to see what the column headings were. When I finally went back to the bottom, output seemed frozen. I think this corresponds to the period around 13:42:27, which you will see is a 17 minute jump from the previous lines, and which has some garbled output.

Remote OS version: Darwin arm64 25.5.0

title: Search "Files to Include" behaves incorrectly when workspace root and nested file patterns are specified together
document:
Search "Files to Include" behaves incorrectly when workspace root and nested file patterns are specified together

When a workspace root pattern and nested file patterns are specified together in `files to include`, the search scope is narrowed to the more specific patterns.

For example, searching with `./` searches the entire workspace.
However, searching with `./, ./folder/file` searches only `./folder/file`.

This may be considered an unusual pattern combination, but when include patterns are generated programmatically by an extension, the current behavior requires manual deduplication.

Also, semantically, adding an include pattern should not reduce the search scope. In this case, adding a pattern that is already contained within another include pattern changes the search results.

The issue appears to be specific to workspace root patterns. Similar combinations using ordinary folder paths work as expected (2.c vs 2.d test).

Reproduction tests for the attached zip file.

1. Open `singleFolderProject` and search for `content`
a) `./` works correctly
b) `./, ./folder1/file_1_1.txt` (or `, ./folder1/file_1_1.txt`) incorrectly narrows the search scope
c) `./folder1, ./folder1/file_1_1.txt` works correctly

2. Open `multiRootProject/workspace.code-workspace` to test in multi-root workspace
a) `./` works correctly
b) `./, ./wsFolder1/folder1` incorrectly narrows the search scope
c) `./wsFolder1, ./wsFolder1/folder1/wsf1_1.txt` incorrectly narrows the search scope
d) `./wsFolder1/folder1, ./wsFolder1/folder1/wsf1_1.txt` works correctly

[vscode.find.issue.rootIgnore.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29450168/vscode.find.issue.rootIgnore.zip)

title: Feature Request: Native support ${num:$Snippet(prefix)} to nest other code snippets & trigger snippets inside quotes/brackets
document:
Feature Request: Native support ${num:$Snippet(prefix)} to nest other code snippets & trigger snippets inside quotes/brackets

### 现有问题
1. VS Code原生snippet不支持内嵌其他片段，网上$Snippet()语法仅第三方插件可用，换环境插件失效
2. 光标在单/双引号、圆括号内部时，无法检索、触发code-snippets代码片段，复用片段很麻烦

### 期望新增功能
1. 原生支持 `${1:$Snippet(片段prefix)}` 语法，自动加载对应prefix的片段并继承下拉占位符
2. 增加设置开关：允许在字符串引号、括号内部检索、触发代码片段
3. 支持多段占位符独立跳转，不共享同编号同步编辑冲突

### 使用场景（我的PowerMill宏开发场景）
我大量复用「元素」下拉片段，在pathname函数参数引号内需要反复输入前缀手动Tab切换，原生不支持嵌套，代码冗余严重。
示例片段：
{
 "元素路径": {
 "prefix": "pathname = 取激活元素路径",
 "body": [
 "pathname('${1:$Snippet(元素)}','${2:$Snippet(元素)}'.name)"
 ]
 },
 "元素": {
 "prefix": "元素",
 "body": ["${1|toolpath,tool,Boundary,Pattern|}"]
 }
}

### 替代方案现状
只能拆分占位符到引号外，或安装第三方插件，兼容性差，团队共享片段会出现大量失效。

title: Renderer process crashes (`reason: crashed, code: 5`) — built-in agent sessions cache (`agentSessions.model.cache`) imported from `~/.claude` grows unbounded (~140 MB) and cannot be disabled
document:
Renderer process crashes (`reason: crashed, code: 5`) — built-in agent sessions cache (`agentSessions.model.cache`) imported from `~/.claude` grows unbounded (~140 MB) and cannot be disabled

- VS Code Version: 1.126.0
- OS Version: macOS 13.3.1, Apple Silicon (arm64)

### Description

VS Code's built-in agent/chat **sessions** feature automatically discovers external CLI agent history on disk — in my case the Claude Code CLI directory `~/.claude/projects//`. It serializes **every** discovered session (including a full per-session file-change/diff list) into the **workspace** key `agentSessions.model.cache` inside `…/User/workspaceStorage//state.vscdb`.

There is **no size cap / eviction** on this cache. After ~150 CLI sessions in one project it reached **~140 MB in a single key**. On window load this blob is read into the renderer, and the renderer process crashes:

`renderer.log` shows **no JS exception and no OOM message** at crash time — the renderer is killed at the native level. The dialog shown is "The window terminated unexpectedly (reason: 'crashed', code: '5')".

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Accumulate a large Claude Code CLI history for one folder (`~/.claude/projects//` ~150 sessions / ~120 MB).
2. Open that folder in VS Code 1.126.0.
3. Inspect the workspace storage:
 `sqlite3 "…/User/workspaceStorage//state.vscdb" "SELECT key,length(value) FROM ItemTable ORDER BY length(value) DESC LIMIT 5;"`
 → `agentSessions.model.cache | 140319369`
4. The renderer crashes (most reliably under an Extension Development Host / second window, where memory headroom is smaller).

### What does NOT stop it (the core complaint)

- Uninstalling the `anthropic.claude-code` extension — **no effect**; the discovery/caching is built into the workbench (`out/vs/workbench/workbench.desktop.main.js` hardcodes `claude-code`, `copilotcli`, `openai-codex`, …).
- `"chat.viewSessions.enabled": false`
- `"chat.agentHost.claudeAgent.enabled": false`

None of these prevent the background scan of `~/.claude` or the cache from being rebuilt to 140 MB. The only effective workaround was to **delete the key** and **archive most of the CLI history** so the cache rebuilds small.

### Design problems

1. VS Code scans a **third-party CLI's private data directory** (`~/.claude`) by default, without an opt-in.
2. The per-workspace cache (`agentSessions.model.cache`) has **no size limit and no eviction**.
3. The blob is loaded **synchronously into the renderer**, so an oversized cache can crash the entire window — there is no guard against an oversized state entry.
4. The user-facing settings that look like they should disable the feature do **not** stop the scan/caching.

### Suggested fixes

- Cap / paginate / lazily load `agentSessions.model.cache`; never block-load multi-hundred-MB values into the renderer.
- Provide a setting that actually disables external CLI session discovery (and honor it for the disk scan, not just the view).
- Make external-agent (Claude Code / Copilot CLI / Codex) discovery opt-in.

### Workaround for others hitting this

Quit VS Code completely, then:

and move old sessions out of `~/.claude/projects//`.

title: Agents: Open in Editor should restore Agent Host chat session
document:
Agents: Open in Editor should restore Agent Host chat session

### Repro steps

1. Start or open an Agent Host session with a worktree in the Agents window.
2. Select **Open in Editor**.

### Actual

VS Code opens the session worktree in a new Editor window, but does not open the corresponding Agent Host chat session.

### Expected

The Editor window should open both the worktree and the corresponding chat session, matching the extension-host Copilot CLI behavior.

### Proposed fix

Pass the session resource through the existing VS Code protocol URL handoff. The main process already forwards its `session` parameter as `vscode:openChatSession` to the target Editor window.

Draft fix: [#323316](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/323316)

(Written by Copilot)

title: MAI-Code-1-Flash tries to use Python to add a line to a YAML file
document:
MAI-Code-1-Flash tries to use Python to add a line to a YAML file

I'm using the new MAI-Code-1-Flash model with a prompt that was to do two things:

1. Switch to a new Git branch.
2. Add the line `jekt/status: started` to the ticket's `description.yaml` file to indicate that work had started on the ticket.

(You can refer to the actual `/jekt-ticket-start` [Jekt reference prompt](https://jekt.dev/tools/prompts), but it certainly doesn't mention using Python.)

My standing instructions even said to use VS Code tools instead of the command line for search/replace options!

**Expected:** MAI-Code-1-Flash uses VS Code tools (like every other model) to append a line to a YAML file.

**Actual:** MAI-Code-1-Flash creates an entire Python script just to append a line! I kid you not!

You might say, "LLMs will be LLMs; we cannot control them — who knows what they will do?" But this is Microsoft's own LLM specifically trained to work with VS Code! Surely it was extensively fine-tuned to use VS Code GitHub Copilot tools. And it thinks it has to use Python to append a line to a text file rather than just use a VS Code editing tool?

I have invoked only about half a dozen prompts using MAI-Code-1-Flash, and I hope this is not a harbinger.

title: Test: Client tools support in Agent Host/Copilot CLI harness
document:
Test: Client tools support in Agent Host/Copilot CLI harness

Refs: #321478

- [x] anyOS @aiday-mar
- [x] anyOS @dileepyavan
- [x] anyOS @hawkticehurst

Complexity: 3

Authors: @dmitrivMS

[Create issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23321478%0A%0A&assignees=dmitrivMS&labels=agent-host,chat,chat-tools)

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323371%0A%0A&assignees=dmitrivMS)

---

**Important:** Pick one of the **Main Editor Window** or **Agents Window**, but one per person so that both are tested) and use this target for all your testing.

Agent Host/Copilot CLI harness now supports customizable client tools - that is tools which run client-side in VS Code in a session that runs in Agent Host/Copilot CLI. Tools customizations page has been added to Chat Customizations UI - don't use the picker as it should not be enabled for Agent Host/Copilot CLI.

1. Ensure you don't see Tools in chat customizations for other harness types.
2. Ensure you don't see Tools picker or affordance for Agent Host Copilot CLI.
3. Pick "Copilot" harness, open Tools in chat customization, verify:
- You can navigate around the section (verify back buttons work where appropriate)
- Verify Tools view is keyboard-accessible and UI layout is responsive
- Verify CLI built-in tools are shown as readonly and what you see makes sense
- You can enable/disable tools and tool sets
- Verify number of enabled tools is reflected in the nav pane (in Agents window - in the main nav pane). Note: CLI tools are excluded from this number.
- You can install/uninstall tools from extensions in the marketplace
- Verify you can call extension tools from the session once installed
- You can view tools provided by extension by clicking on it
4. When you enable/disable tools and then try to use them form a session ("use {tool-name} tool to do {...}") it works correctly according to the enabled/disabled state of the tools.
5. Verify tool choices are persisted per user profile (close/reopen VS code, start a new sesion etc)

Note: you can ask the model to list all tools available, e.g. "list all tools you have, simple list, names only" to verify things. You should see built-in tools and whatever you have enabled in the Chat customizations.

title: Test: Agents window onboarding tours
document:
Test: Agents window onboarding tours

Refs: #322468, #323361

- [x] anyOS @ulugbekna
- [x] anyOS @dmitrivMS

Complexity: 3

Authors: @benibenj

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323362%0A%0A&assignees=benibenj)

---

This test is about the two **onboarding tours** in the **Agents window**: the spotlight tour shown when you land on the new-session view, and the one shown after the pulsing **New Session** button. Both walk a brand-new user through the pickers that shape a session. Go through the flows as a real user would — the goal is not only "does it work" but "does the tour point at the right things and read well". Please file issues for anything confusing, mistargeted, or awkward.

## Set up

To behave like a brand-new user and force the tours on demand, run a fresh install with a clean user data directory and enable the developer setting:

1. Launch with a throwaway profile, e.g. `code-insiders --user-data-dir /tmp/onboarding-test` (use any empty folder).
2. Add these to settings:
 

> The tours are shown at most once per window session, so **reload the window** to replay a tour. The two tours share state, so test them one at a time using the `onboarding.developerMode` flags above.

## Flows to try

### 1. New-session view tour

- Open the **Agents window** so it shows the **new-session view**.
- Pick a workspace
- A spotlight tour should appear and walk through up to three steps in order, each highlighting the matching picker:
 1. **Work Across Workspaces** — the workspace picker.
 2. **Choose a Harness** — the harness picker.
 3. **Isolate Your Work** — the isolation picker.
- Check that each spotlight is anchored to the correct control, the titles/descriptions match, and you can move through and dismiss the tour.

### 2. New Session button tour

- Switch the developer flags so only the button tour is on, then reload:
 
- Send a request in a session and keep that session visible. After a short delay the **New Session** button in the top left should **pulse**.
- Click the pulsing button — the new-session view opens and the tour walks two steps:
 1. **Work Across Workspaces** — the workspace picker.
 2. **Isolate Your Work** — the isolation picker.
- Check the pulse is noticeable but not annoying, and that the steps highlight the right controls with the right text.

> Throughout: note anything that pointed at the wrong element, showed an empty/blank step, or did not match your expectations — that feedback is the point of this TPI.

title: Opening a link from a staged .md file causes the wrong document to open
document:
Opening a link from a staged .md file causes the wrong document to open

- In a Git repo, create a .md file with a link to another source file (say, a .cs source file).
- Stage the .md file in the Git repo.
- In the Source Control view, double-click the .md file in the Staged Changes section to open it in a new tab (Index).
- Optionally, swap to the Markdown preview.
- Open the link to the source file with ctrl+click.

Observed behavior

- The correct source file and file contents are shown.
- Breadcrumb navigation on the opened source file works.

Actual behavior

- VSCode shows a tab with the name and breadcrumb path of the linked source file, but the contents are that of the Markdown file.
- Breadcrumb navigation doesn't work.

Extensions disabled

title: Copilot Chat /compact triggers MCP server startup (unexpected side effect)
document:
Copilot Chat /compact triggers MCP server startup (unexpected side effect)

1.119.0 (arm64)

Extension Versions:
GitHub Copilot Chat: 0.48.1

macOS

Description:
Running /compact in an active Copilot Chat session unexpectedly triggers MCP server startup/discovery, even when the prompt does not require any tools.
Observed UI status immediately after /compact: Starting MCP servers github ... Skip?

This appears to couple context compaction with tool lifecycle/discovery, which should be independent.

Configure at least one MCP server in workspace (for example github).
Open Copilot Chat and run a short conversation.
Enter /compact.
Observe chat status area right after compaction.
Actual Behavior:

Copilot starts MCP discovery/startup flow.
UI shows Starting MCP servers github ... Skip?.
Expected Behavior:

/compact should only compact/prune conversation history/context.
MCP servers should remain idle unless explicitly needed by a user prompt or agent tool call.
Reproducibility:
Intermittent to frequent in long-running sessions (observed in current session with screenshot evidence).

Impact:

Unnecessary MCP server startup overhead
Unexpected background activity
User confusion (compaction appears to “restart” tool engine)
Additional Notes:

The UI event is visible, but this exact runtime status may not always be persisted in transcript/debug artifacts, which makes debugging harder.
This may indicate a second issue: missing observability parity between UI status events and persisted logs.

title: [Linux][Wayland] Severe memory leak in VSCode main process on Fedora 44 (GNOME/Wayland) – persists with --disable-gpu, --disable-extensions, and fresh profile
document:
[Linux][Wayland] Severe memory leak in VSCode main process on Fedora 44 (GNOME/Wayland) – persists with --disable-gpu, --disable-extensions, and fresh profile

Environment

Kernel: Linux 7.0.13-200.fc44.x86_64
Display Server: Wayland (GNOME 4x, X11 session not available)
Graphics Driver: Mesa 26.0.8 (Compatibility Profile)

ElectronBuildId: 14159160

 Start VSCode with a fresh, clean profile and no extensions:
 bash

 code --disable-extensions --user-data-dir=/tmp/clean-vscode

 Open a typical workspace (e.g., a medium-sized project with a few folders).

 Leave VSCode running for 4–6 hours (or overnight) with minimal interaction.

 Monitor memory usage via GNOME System Monitor or VSCode's Process Explorer (Developer: Open Process Explorer).

Actual Result

 The main process (code) continuously consumes more memory over time.

 After ~4 hours, memory usage reaches 6–8 GB and keeps growing.

 System responsiveness degrades; swapping may occur; eventually the OOM killer may terminate the process.

 This happens regardless of:

 --disable-gpu flag

 --disable-extensions flag

 --ozone-platform=x11 (though X11 may slow the leak, it does not stop it)

 Changing GNOME shell extensions (tested with both dash-to-dock and dash-to-panel)

 Cleaning cache directories (~/.config/Code/GPUCache, etc.)

 The Process Explorer shows the main process as the primary consumer, not extension-host or GPU process.

Expected Result
Memory usage should stabilize after initial workspace load and remain within a reasonable range (e.g., ~500 MB – 1.5 GB) during idle periods.

Additional Observations

 Firefox (using native Wayland) does not show this leak under the same workload, indicating the issue is Chromium/Electron-specific.

 The leak is observed in all Chromium-based applications (Chrome, Edge, VSCode) on this system, suggesting a common root cause in the Chromium engine or its interaction with the Fedora/Wayland/Mesa stack.

 journalctl shows repeated GNOME Shell warnings about stage view updates (e.g., Can't update stage views actor...), but these persist even with shell extensions disabled – likely a symptom of deeper compositor communication issues.

 No obvious GPU-related errors appear in dmesg or journalctl; the leak appears to be in user-space memory (heap or shared memory).

 This issue is distinct from the known kdialog file-picker leak (KDE Bug 494160), as it occurs without opening any file dialog.

What I've Tried (with no effect)

 Disabling GPU acceleration (--disable-gpu)

 Disabling all extensions (--disable-extensions)

 Forcing X11 backend (--ozone-platform=x11)

 Running with a fresh profile (--user-data-dir=/tmp/clean-vscode)

 Updating to the latest VSCode stable/insiders

 Switching GNOME shell extensions (dash-to-dock → dash-to-panel) – leak persists

 Removing cache directories (~/.cache/mesa_shader_cache/, ~/.config/Code/GPUCache, etc.)

 Setting "files.watcherExclude" to exclude node_modules, dist, etc.

 Setting MALLOC_CHECK_=3 – no heap corruption output

Suggested Investigation Areas

 Wayland buffer management or shared memory allocation in Electron's Ozone platform backend.

 Interaction with Mesa's gbm or dri components (Fedora 44 uses Mesa 26.0.8).

 Potential reference counting leak in the compositor communication layer.

 Compare with Chrome/Edge behavior to isolate VSCode/Electron-specific vs. common Chromium code paths.

Additional Logs
If needed, I can provide:

 VSCode Process Explorer screenshots showing main process memory

 chrome://tracing-style memory snapshots (if applicable)

 Full journalctl -b logs

 strace output

title: Agents window: hidden editor part is restored when switching back to a session
document:
Agents window: hidden editor part is restored when switching back to a session

In the Agents window, the per-session layout did not remember when the user **hid the editor part** for a session, so switching away and back forced it open again.

**Repro:**
1. Open session A with only the editor part visible (not the aux bar).
2. Close the side pane using the **Toggle Side Panel** action (this hides both the editor part and the auxiliary bar while keeping editors open).
3. Switch to session B.
4. Switch back to session A.

**Bug:** the editor part was made visible again instead of staying hidden.

Fixed in https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/323029.

title: Agents window: editor layout actions missing in single-tab mode
document:
Agents window: editor layout actions missing in single-tab mode

In the Agents window, the editor-area layout controls — **Maximize Editor Area**, **Restore Editor Area**, **Close Editor Area**, **Open in Modal Editor**, and the secondary side bar toggle — disappeared when `workbench.editor.showTabs` was set to `single`.

These actions are contributed to the editor title layout menu, which in single-tab mode is rendered by `SingleEditorTabsControl` — and it returned an empty set of layout actions, so the whole group went missing.

Fixed in https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/322908.

title: Session header meta row with consistent pills and clickable diff/changes
document:
Session header meta row with consistent pills and clickable diff/changes

The Agents window **session header meta row** (the row under the title) has been reworked so contributed actions render consistently as compact secondary-button **pills**, and the diff/changes affordance is clickable and accurate.

## What shipped

- **Unified meta row** — workspace, changes, and pull request actions all render through `Menus.SessionHeaderMeta` as uniform secondary-button pills.
- **Workspace folder pill** — the workspace label is now a leading "Open Files" pill showing the workspace icon (cloud / folder / worktree per workspace kind) + label, with truncation for long names. Its hover shows the repo folder and working directory on separate lines, plus the git branch. The branch name was removed from the header title.
- **Changes / diff-stats pill** — a clickable `⊟ N files +X -Y` pill that opens the multi-file diff editor, reading and opening the session's default changeset (correct for both Copilot and agent-host providers).

title: Test: Responsive auto-collapse of the sessions sidebar (experimental)
document:
Test: Responsive auto-collapse of the sessions sidebar (experimental)

Refs: #322819

- [x] anyOS @Giuspepe
- [x] anyOS @kycutler

Complexity: 4

Authors: @sandy081

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323333%0A%0A&assignees=sandy081)

---

The Agents window can **automatically hide the sessions sidebar** on a narrow window when both the editor and the side panel are open, so the working area stays usable, and **show it again** when there's room. This is gated by an experimental setting. Go through the flows and report anything that hides/shows the sidebar at the wrong time.

## Set up

- Confirm the experimental setting **`sessions.layout.autoCollapseSessionsSidebar`** is enabled (default **on** in Insiders/exploration builds; turn it on if needed).
- Open the **Agents window** with a single session (a Copilot session in the Agent Host works well).
- The behavior triggers on a **narrow** window — the main container roughly **≤ 1800px** wide. Resize the window narrow before testing.

## Flows to try

1. **Auto-hide when editor + side panel are both open (narrow window)**
 - On a narrow window, open **both** the editor part and the side panel (auxiliary bar / Changes) for the session.
 - The **sessions sidebar should auto-hide** so the editor and side panel get the space.

2. **Auto-show again when there's room**
 - From the state above, either **close** the editor or the side panel, **or widen** the window past the threshold.
 - The **sessions sidebar should come back** automatically.

3. **A manual close is respected**
 - Manually close the sessions sidebar yourself.
 - It should **stay closed** — the responsive behavior should not auto-reopen it until you open it again yourself.

4. **Suspended with multiple sessions visible**
 - Show multiple sessions at once.
 - The auto-collapse should be **suspended** (not kick in) in that layout.

5. **Switching sessions must never auto-hide the sidebar**
 - With the sidebar visible, switch between sessions a few times (mouse and keyboard).
 - Navigation should **never** cause the sidebar to flicker/auto-hide just from switching.

6. **After reload**
 - Close the side pane, reload the window, then interact again.
 - A sidebar you had **closed before reloading** should stay closed — the feature should not wrongly reveal it after a reload.

> Report any case where the sidebar hides or shows at the wrong moment, flickers on navigation, or doesn't respect your manual close.

title: Test: Per-chat agent/model selection, aggregated progress & changes
document:
Test: Per-chat agent/model selection, aggregated progress & changes

Refs: #322300

- [x] anyOS @chrmarti
- [x] anyOS @dmitrivMS

Complexity: 3

Authors: @sandy081

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323332%0A%0A&assignees=sandy081)

---

This covers two related improvements for multi-chat agent host sessions: choosing the **agent and model per chat**, and surfacing **progress and file changes at the session level** across peer chats. Go through the flows and report anything confusing or wrong.

## Set up

- Open a **Copilot session in the Agent Host** (this is a multi-chat session).

## Flows to try

1. **Create a session with a custom agent**
 - Create a new session and select a **plugin/extension-contributed custom agent** (for example a custom agent contributed in your workspace), then send a message.
 - It should start and respond normally — no "Custom agent '…' not found" error.

2. **Per-chat agent & model**
 - Create more than one chat in the session. In each chat, change the **agent and the model** independently (using the pickers in the chat input).
 - Each chat should keep its own agent/model choice.

3. **Aggregated progress across chats**
 - Start work in a non-active peer chat (so another chat is the focused one).
 - While that chat is running: the **session status** should show as in-progress (not Idle), and **each chat tab** that is working should show its own progress — not just the first/main chat.

4. **Aggregated changes (Changes pill)**
 - Have one or more peer chats make file edits.
 - The session header **Changes pill** should appear and, when opened, show the **aggregated changes** from the peer chats (not only the main chat's edits).

> Note anything where the agent/model didn't stick per chat, a working chat didn't show progress, or changes were missing.

title: Test: Create, close, reopen & delete chats + New Chat UX in the agents window
document:
Test: Create, close, reopen & delete chats + New Chat UX in the agents window

Refs: #322844

- [x] anyOS @aiday-mar
- [x] anyOS @digitarald

Complexity: 2

Authors: @sandy081

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323330%0A%0A&assignees=sandy081)

---

This test is about the **multi-chat experience** in an agent host session — creating, closing, reopening and deleting chats. Go through the flows below as a real user would. The goal is not just "does it work", but **does the UX make sense** — is it discoverable, predictable, and does it feel right? Please file issues for anything confusing, surprising, or awkward.

## Set up

- Open a **Copilot session in the Agent Host** (this is a multi-chat session).

## Flows to try

1. **Creating chats**
 - Use the **`+ New Chat` button in the session header** (next to the session controls) to create a chat. Once more than one chat is open, a **chat tab strip** appears with its own trailing **`+`** — use that to add more. Continue real conversations in each.
 - Did the way you create a new chat feel obvious and stay consistent as the number of chats grew? Did switching between tabs feel natural?

2. **Closing and reopening**
 - Close a chat using the **close (x) icon on its tab**. Then reopen it from the **Conversations dropdown**. Note where the dropdown lives: while there is a single open chat (tab strip hidden) it sits in the **session header**; once the **chat tab strip is shown** (more than one open chat) it moves to the **right-aligned end of the chat tab bar**. Each chat is listed with a checkbox — uncheck hides it, check shows it.
 - Could you tell that closing **hides** a chat rather than losing it? Was the Conversations menu easy to find in both placements? Did moving it to the end of the tab bar (when tabs are shown) feel natural, or did you expect it somewhere else?

3. **Deleting**
 - Permanently delete a chat via **right-click on its tab → Delete Chat**.
 - Was the difference between **close** (x on the tab) and **delete** (context menu) clear? Did the destructive action feel appropriately distinct/safe? Were you ever unsure whether a chat was gone for good or just hidden?

4. **End-to-end sanity**
 - After creating/closing/reopening/deleting, switch away and back to the session (or reload).
 - Did everything stay in the state you expected?

> Throughout: note anything that was confusing, took more than one try to find, or didn't match your expectations — that feedback is the point of this TPI.

title: Test: Fork a chat in a multi-chat agent host session
document:
Test: Fork a chat in a multi-chat agent host session

Refs: #322799

- [x] anyOS @vritant24
- [x] anyOS @hediet

Complexity: 4

Authors: @sandy081

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23323329%0A%0A&assignees=sandy081)

---

Forking a conversation in an **agent host session that supports multiple chats** now creates a **new peer chat in the same session** (instead of a brand-new top-level session). Single-chat and non-agent-host sessions keep the existing fork-into-a-new-session behavior.

This is an **exploratory** test — try forking from different points and conversation states.

## Set up

- Open an **agent host copilot session that supports multiple chats**
- Have a conversation with at least a few back-and-forth turns so there is something to fork.

## Scenarios

1. **Fork creates a peer chat (multi-chat session)**
 - From a multi-chat agent host session, fork the conversation (fork action on the conversation / a selected request).
 - Expected: a **new chat appears in the same session** alongside its siblings — not a new top-level session.
 - The new chat should contain the inherited conversation up to (but not including) the request you forked from.

2. **Fork boundary**
 - Fork from a specific earlier request.
 - Expected: the new chat keeps everything **before** that request (the selected turn is the last one kept), matching the new-session fork behavior.

3. **Forked chat title**
 - After forking, the new chat should get an auto-generated title derived from the inherited conversation (it should read like a normal title, not literally say "forked"/"branched").
 - If you start a manual `/rename` right after forking, your rename should win (auto-title is suppressed).

4. **Forked chat is independent**
 - Continue the conversation in the forked chat.
 - Expected: it runs separately and does not affect the source chat or its siblings.

5. **Single-chat session keeps old behavior**
 - Open a **Claude Code session in the agent host** (single-chat — does not support multiple chats) and fork the conversation.
 - Expected: the existing behavior — fork opens a **new session** (not a peer chat).

6. **Exploratory**
 - Try forking an empty/very short chat, forking repeatedly, forking the forked chat, and forking while another chat is running. Note any odd titles, missing turns, or focus issues.

title: Notebook Outline shows LaTeX display equations despite “Markdown Headers Only”
document:
Notebook Outline shows LaTeX display equations despite “Markdown Headers Only”

- VS Code Version: 1.126.0
- Commit: 7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a
- OS Version: Windows NT 10.0.26200.0

## Description

The Outline view of a Jupyter notebook displays Markdown cells containing LaTeX display equations as outline entries, even when **Markdown Headers Only** is enabled and **Code Cells** and **Code Cell Symbols** are disabled.

The equations are incorrectly treated as section headings. This makes the Outline very noisy and interferes with hierarchical section folding.

The issue persists after:

- Reloading the VS Code window.
- Disabling all installed extensions.
- Confirming that `notebook.outline.showMarkdownHeadersOnly` is enabled.
- Checking that the notebook cells contain no unusual metadata.

The same notebook hierarchy is displayed correctly in Google Colab.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create or open a Jupyter notebook.
2. Add a Markdown cell containing:

 ```markdown
 # Section

Some explanatory text.

$$
x^2 + y^2 = 1
$$

The LaTeX equation appears as an additional item in the Outline, for example:
Section
└── $$ x^2 + y^2 = 1 $$

In larger notebooks, many equations are incorrectly displayed as nested outline entries.
Expected Behavior
Only actual Markdown headings beginning with #, ##, ###, etc. should appear in the Outline when Markdown Headers Only is enabled.
The equation cell should not appear as an outline entry.
