title: Add Playwright CLI support to the Integrated Browser
document:
Add Playwright CLI support to the Integrated Browser

## Feature Request

The VS Code Integrated Browser is an excellent feature for development and testing workflows. It allows engineers to interact with web applications without opening a separate browser window, making the development experience much smoother.

However, for QA engineers and teams using modern automation frameworks such as Playwright, the integrated browser currently does not support Playwright CLI functionality.

### Current Limitation

When using Playwright CLI, features such as:

- Automated navigation across pages
- Snapshot/screenshot capture during test execution
- End-to-end test debugging and inspection

are not supported within the Integrated Browser.

### Expected Behavior

It would be extremely useful if the Integrated Browser could support Playwright CLI interactions, allowing QA engineers to:

- Run Playwright tests directly against the Integrated Browser
- Capture snapshots/screenshots during navigation
- Debug and inspect automated test flows without launching an external browser

### Benefits

- Improves the testing and debugging experience for QA engineers.
- Reduces the need to open separate Chrome/Edge instances.
- Provides a more unified development and testing workflow inside VS Code.

Thank you for considering this enhancement. I believe this would be a valuable addition for teams using Playwright-based automation frameworks.

title: Expose `/code-review` in Claude Code chat session commands without requiring Agent Host
document:
Expose `/code-review` in Claude Code chat session commands without requiring Agent Host

## Summary

Please expose the Claude `/code-review` slash command (https://code.claude.com/docs/en/code-review#review-a-diff-locally) through the normal `claude-code` chat session contribution path, so it is available in the Copilot Chat panel when using Claude Code / Claude Agent SDK without requiring the Agent Host implementation.

## Background

Currently, Claude commands such as `/review` and `/simplify` are statically exposed via `extensions/copilot/package.json` under `contributes.chatSessions` for the `claude-code` session type.

However, `/code-review` appears to be surfaced only through the Agent Host sessions/customizations path, specifically via:

- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/claude/customizations/claudeBuiltinCommands.ts`
- `src/vs/sessions/skills/code-review/SKILL.md`
- the Sessions “Run Code Review” toolbar action, which sends `/code-review`

This means `/code-review` is available in Agent Host / Sessions contexts, but not in the normal Claude Code chat-session command list.

## Request

Add `/code-review` to the `claude-code` chat session command contribution in `extensions/copilot/package.json`, alongside existing commands like `/review`, `/security-review`, and `/simplify`.

Suggested command entry:

## Motivation

`/code-review` is already a recognized built-in skill/command in the Agent Host Claude path and has clearer semantics than `/review` or `/simplify`:

- `/review` appears oriented toward PR review or general review.
- `/simplify` focuses on reuse, simplification, and efficiency cleanup.
- `/code-review` is specifically intended for reviewing current changes for correctness issues and actionable code comments.

Users working in the normal Claude Code / Claude Agent SDK chat path should be able to discover and invoke this command without switching to or depending on Agent Host.

## Expected Behavior

When using the normal Claude Code chat session in Copilot Chat:

1. Typing `/` should show `/code-review` in the slash-command list.
2. Running `/code-review` should route to the Claude Code / Claude Agent SDK command if supported by the installed SDK/runtime.
3. The behavior should align with the Agent Host Sessions “Run Code Review” action, which already sends `/code-review`.

## Current Behavior

`/code-review` is not exposed in the static `contributes.chatSessions[type="claude-code"].commands` list, even though related commands such as `/review`, `/security-review`, and `/simplify` are exposed.

## Notes

This request is not asking to change the Agent Host implementation. It is asking to make the existing `/code-review` command discoverable and usable through the normal Claude Code chat-session command contribution path as well.

title: Agent customization for local continues to attempt to download old plugins from source after they have been uninstalled
document:
Agent customization for local continues to attempt to download old plugins from source after they have been uninstalled

# System/Tooling Context
- Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.41
- VS Code Version: 1.126.0
- OS Version: Window 11
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): Agent Customization
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): NA (model does not matter in this case)
- Notes: 
 - For this bug I am using **rwoll/markdown-review** as an example as that is what is being used in the [VS docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agent-customization/agent-plugins#_install-a-plugin-from-source)
 - I think this could be easily fixed by having uninstalled plugins getting removed from the chat.plugins.marketplaces section in %APPDATA%/Code/User/settings.json
 - This is largely just an annoying user experience, but also raises security risks as you can be downloading from repos/branches you don't want

# Steps to Reproduce (simple but not realistic):
1. Open VS Code settings (%APPDATA%/Code/User/settings.json)
2. Add a link to a branch that no longer exists `"chat.plugins.marketplaces": [ "rwoll/markdown-review#BAD"]`
3. `Chat: Install Plugin From Source` from the Command Palette
4. Enter the sub url (**rwoll/markdown-review** for example)
5. Note that you get an error message

# Steps to Reproduce (realistic but much harder to setup):
1. Create a simple agent plugin project in a new GitHub repo and [setup to distribute any skills or actions](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agent-customization/agent-plugins)
2. Get plugin code in main
3. Create a temp branch off of main
4. Install the temp branch of the plugin using `Chat: Install Plugin From Source` from the Command Palette *You will need to follow this format to add from a branch `ORG/PROJECT#BRANCH` 
5. Open `Chat: Open Customizations` from the Command Palette and go to the Plugins page
6. Uninstall the plugin 
7. Delete the temp branch in git
8. Delete the local cached downloaded code: `%USERPROFILE%/.vscode/agent-plugins/github.com` *This folder doesn't often get deleted, but it can for various reason
9. Install the plugin (without specifying the branch) using `Chat: Install Plugin From Source` from the Command Palette 
10. Note that you get an "Failed to install plugin" error message for the deleted branch

# Workaround:
Manually delete old marketplace values from %APPDATA%/Code/User/settings.json

title: Model switches to Auto for AH in every new workspace
Remove Agent Host terminal auto-approve fallback rules
document:
Remove Agent Host terminal auto-approve fallback rules

Follow-up from #322774 and related to #322112.

## Context
#322774 makes Agent Host consume the real `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` setting by forwarding normalized terminal auto-approve rules through Agent Host root config.

As part of that PR, `CommandAutoApprover` still keeps a bundled hard-coded default rule table as a compatibility fallback for clients that do not forward the setting yet. A TODO was added near that fallback:

> Remove this fallback once all agent-host clients are guaranteed to forward `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` before shell approvals run.

## Work
- Audit Agent Host clients and startup ordering to confirm terminal auto-approve rules are always forwarded before shell approval checks can run.
- Remove the bundled hard-coded fallback rule table from `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/commandAutoApprover.ts` once forwarding is guaranteed.
- Keep or adjust the source-of-truth tests so forwarded `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` rules remain authoritative over any fallback behavior.
- Confirm local and remote Agent Host paths still respect `chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove` and `chat.tools.terminal.ignoreDefaultAutoApproveRules` after the fallback is removed.

## Links
- Follow-up PR: #322774
- Original tracking issue: #322112

[AHP/CLI:COGS] Tools Grouping Service (Virtual Tools)

**Priority:** P0

The Tools Grouping Service ("virtual tools"), built by @connor4312, keeps large tool sets from inflating prompt size and degrading tool selection. Once the active tool count crosses a threshold, related tools are collapsed into on‑demand **virtual tool groups** so the model sees a compact set instead of a long flat list — a COGS win that grows with the number of MCP/extension tools. We need this behavior to carry over as CLI/AHP becomes the default agent.

## How it works

- Grouping kicks in once the tool count passes `START_GROUPING_AFTER_TOOL_COUNT` (currently `HARD_TOOL_LIMIT / 2 = 64`), keeping the request under the 128 hard tool limit. The threshold is experiment‑based (`VirtualToolThreshold`).
- `IToolGroupingService.create(sessionId, tools)` returns an `IToolGrouping` that:
 - `compute(query)` — returns the (possibly grouped) tool list for the request;
 - expands a group on demand when the model calls into it (`didCall` / `ensureExpanded`), and collapses tools by recency across turns (`didTakeTurn`);
 - is backed by a persisted grouping cache (`ToolGroupingCache`).
- Categorization uses the client‑side embeddings service to cluster related tools.

Lives in the Copilot extension under `extension/tools/common/virtualTools/` (`toolGroupingService.ts`, `toolGrouping.ts`, `virtualToolGrouper.ts`, `virtualToolGroupCache.ts`).

## Why it needs attention for AHP/CLI

- The service runs in the Copilot extension and depends on the client‑side embeddings service, so it does **not** automatically apply to CLI / agent‑host (SDK) sessions.
- As with Client Side Tool Search, we need SDK/CLI buy‑in to let the client supply tool grouping — or an equivalent path — so the token savings hold when CLI is the default. The client would keep the embeddings index updated against the SDK tool set.

## References

- Umbrella: #321462 — Client Side Tool Search & Tools Grouping Service
- Original implementation by @connor4312 (virtual tools: initial implementation, auto‑trigger at the 128 tool limit, enablement simplification, lifecycle fixes).

---
**Context:** Part of the CLI/AHP‑as‑default migration. Tracking given the COGS impact of tool grouping on large tool sets.

title: [AHP/CLI] Claude Opus 4.7 / 4.8 Alt Prompt
document:
[AHP/CLI] Claude Opus 4.7 / 4.8 Alt Prompt

Tracking the experimental, version‑specific alternate system prompts for Claude Opus 4.7 and 4.8. Both are opt‑in (default off) and layer model‑specific tuning on top of the base prompt, following Anthropic's per‑version prompting guides.

## Opus 4.7 — Copilot extension

A standalone `Claude47OpusPrompt` (copy of `Claude46OpusPrompt`) gated by the experiment‑based setting `github.copilot.chat.claude47OpusPrompt.enabled` (default `false`). When enabled, `claude-opus-4.7*` traffic routes to it; otherwise it falls back to the 4.6 Opus prompt.

Highlights vs 4.6 (per the Opus 4.7 prompting guide): refined tool‑triggering and subagent fan‑out, response‑shape/verbosity wording, full operationalSafety, plus targeted lessons — no internal narration between tool calls, end‑of‑turn summary cap, comment discipline, "don't duplicate in‑flight subagent work", "verify subagent output before reporting done", and a `` primer.

- PR: #313916 (cherry‑pick #314193)

## Opus 4.8 — Agent host (CLI)

Delivered through the new per‑model system‑prompt registry for Copilot agent‑host sessions. `Claude48OpusPromptResolver` matches Opus 4.8 and is gated by the VS Code setting `chat.agentHost.opus48Prompt.enabled` (agent‑host config `opus48Prompt`, default off). When off it returns `undefined` and the session uses the default SDK system message.

Per Anthropic's Opus 4.8 guide the overrides are intentionally minimal — the model performs well out of the box — keeping the SDK foundation (tool/safety sections) intact and only nudging two behaviors:
- **Tone:** concise, focused responses; minimal examples; sparing emoji.
- **Subagents:** don't spawn for work doable in a single response; fan out multiple subagents in one turn when reading multiple files / iterating across items.

No interim‑progress scaffolding is added (the guide warns against forcing "summarize after every N tool calls").

- PR: #321864

## Reference

Opus 4.7 tracking, eval runs, and experiment: microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog#7600

title: Support Multi-Chat in Claude harness
document:
Support Multi-Chat in Claude harness

Summary

Add Multi-Chat support to the Claude harness so it matches the Copilot experience.

Background

- The Multi-Chat feature (previously called Sub-Sessions) is currently available for the Copilot harness and works in AHP sessions for Copilot.
- Team feedback and user studies have shown confusion around the feature and its name; the UX should be consistent across harnesses rather than Copilot-only.
- Claude AHP sessions do not currently show the Multi-Chat UI.

Proposal

- Implement Multi-Chat in the Claude harness with parity to the Copilot implementation and UX.
- Ensure Multi-Chat is available in AHP sessions for Claude as it is for Copilot.
- Minimize agent integration effort (sessions should be transparent to agents where possible), and document any agent-facing changes required.
- Update tests, e2e scenarios, and relevant documentation to reflect Multi-Chat availability in the Claude harness.

Acceptance criteria

- Multi-Chat UI appears and behaves the same in the Claude harness as it does in Copilot.
- Multi-Chat is available and functional in AHP sessions for Claude.
- Agent/handler code for Claude either requires no changes or any required changes are documented and minimal.
- Relevant tests and documentation updated.

Implementation notes

- Verify whether sessions need to be linked or if the agent surface can remain unchanged (transparent sessions).
- Coordinate UX changes with the Copilot harness team to ensure consistency and avoid repeating prior confusion around naming/behavior.
- Add testing steps to validate in manual and automated tests across the Claude harness and AHP sessions.

---

Split from the combined Claude & Codex issue. Codex tracked separately in #323624.

[View original Slack conversation](https://vscodeteam.slack.com/archives/C09MVCZKLP4/p1782230045706259?thread_ts=1782230045.706259&cid=C09MVCZKLP4)

title: [AHP/CLI:COGS] Anthropic Extended Cache (1hr TTL)
document:
[AHP/CLI:COGS] Anthropic Extended Cache (1hr TTL)

**Priority:** P0

Anthropic supports an extended **1‑hour cache TTL** for prompt caching, versus the default 5‑minute TTL. Longer‑lived cache entries can raise cache‑hit rates across longer agent sessions — and across summarization/compaction boundaries — which reduces the uncached input billed per turn. The catch is that 1h cache **writes** cost more, so the net COGS impact depends on how much reuse each workload actually gets.

## The tradeoff

- **5‑minute TTL (default):** cache write = **1.25×** base input.
- **1‑hour TTL (extended):** cache write = **2.0×** base input.
- Applies only to Anthropic extended‑TTL‑eligible models (per `modelSupportsExtendedCacheTtl`); all other models are unaffected.

For COGS modeling, the extended‑TTL path prices uncached input at **base × (2.0 / 1.25) = ×1.6** for eligible Anthropic models, so the write premium is reflected when comparing against the 5‑minute baseline. The win is only realized when the higher cache‑hit lift outweighs that 1.6× write premium.

## Open questions / follow‑ups

- **Telemetry gap:** Anthropic returns `cache_creation.ephemeral_5m_input_tokens` and `ephemeral_1h_input_tokens` separately, but we only capture the combined total. A hybrid‑TTL strategy (e.g. system + tools on 1h, messages on 5m) can't be validated without a telemetry change to split these.
- Identify which models/workloads recoup the 1.6× write premium through higher cache reuse, and whether a hybrid‑TTL allocation is worth pursuing.

## Reference

Detailed COGS accounting and per‑model breakdown: microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog#7825

---
**Context:** Part of the CLI/AHP‑as‑default migration. Tracking given the direct COGS impact of cache‑TTL selection.

title: [AHP/CLI:COGS] Grep Search Tool Output Optimization
document:
[AHP/CLI:COGS] Grep Search Tool Output Optimization

The agent `grep_search` (`findTextInFiles`) tool was returning verbose, XML‑tagged results that dominated its tool‑call token usage. PRs #321601 and #321938 introduce a compact, grep‑style output format behind an experiment. Early results show the new format roughly **halves** the tokens returned by the tool with no loss of useful signal — a direct COGS win as we move CLI/AHP to the default agent.

## What changed

- **New compact output format** — A `grep`-style result (plain `path` followed by `line:text`, blank‑line separated) replaces the previous tag‑wrapped output. Gated by the experiment‑based setting `github.copilot.chat.tools.grepSearch.outputFormat` (`tag` | `grep`, default `tag`).
 - #321601 — Optimize grep output and enhance configuration
- **Token‑budget‑aware rendering + long‑line handling** — Results render through prompt‑tsx, so they participate in token‑budget pruning (earlier files prioritized) and carry editor references instead of a single unprunable blob. Long lines are truncated to a max length with context kept around the match.
 - #321938 — Fix long line rendering for grep style output
- **Smarter result capping** — The `maxResults` budget is distributed proportionally across matching files (largest‑remainder, min one match per file), and paths are returned workspace‑relative.

## Experiment

[`agent.tool.responselength`](https://exp.microsoft.com/features/30687fdb-eb7c-dbe8-06c4-02c5ece0671a/progressions?workspaceId=dbd12384-5001-4e90-a863-526321eaf233) — treatment enables `grepSearch.outputFormat = grep` via the `github.copilot-chat/agent.tool.responselength` flag.

### Tokens returned by `grep_search` (control = `tag`, treatment = `grep`)

| Group | Events | Total Tokens | Avg | P50 | P95 | P99 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| control | 363,400 | 421,529,887 | 1,160 | 321.2 | 5,275.4 | 13,091.6 |
| treatment | 389,097 | 200,998,321 | 516.6 | 137.7 | 2,123.6 | 6,380.8 |

- **~55% fewer tokens per call** on average (1,160 → 517); P95 down ~60%, P99 down ~51%.
- Total tokens dropped from **421.5M → 201.0M**, despite treatment serving *more* calls (389K vs 363K events).
- `PValue = 0` — statistically significant.

---
**Context:** Part of the CLI/AHP‑as‑default migration. Tracking given the direct COGS impact of tool response length.

title: ssh sessions to multiple remote hosts with synced view
document:
ssh sessions to multiple remote hosts with synced view

I have a cluster of remote servers and I sometimes want to edit the same file on all servers. The current option is to edit the file locally and scp the local copy to each server. This is usually for files which are not committed to a git repository, such as environment files with sensitive data but it could also apply if I want to push the output of a frontend build to a set of servers.

It might be a cool feature if I can open a local folder and the same folder on a set of remote hosts and if they are all identical then show a single view so that an edit to one of the local files is copied automatically to all hosts in the set. 

If there are any differences these could be highlighted in some way and click to compare as in the Source Control view. Binary files would be excluded from the diff check. A nice addition would be a flag that all tests must pass before the local file is copied to all remote hosts in the set and/or a button to push the changed file to the hosts in the set if auto sync is not enabled.

Issue #200524 discusses grouping remote hosts which would be useful for this feature.

The paths on the local host and the remote hosts may differ so in grouping the remote hosts a path to the remote folder could be defined for all hosts in the group.

title: MCP XAA auth fails for subsequent enterprise-managed server with No accounts available
document:
MCP XAA auth fails for subsequent enterprise-managed server with No accounts available

- VS Code Version: Visual Studio Code - Insiders 1.126.0-insider
- OS Version: macOS Darwin 25.5.0

## Summary

With MCP enterprise-managed auth / XAA enabled, the first enterprise-managed HTTP MCP server can authenticate and run successfully. A later/new enterprise-managed MCP server using the same configured IdP issuer fails with:

The resource authorization server logs show that the XAA token flow for Server B already succeeded: ID-JAG exchange succeeded and jwt-bearer redemption succeeded. VS Code appears to mint/redeem the resource token, but then fails before attaching that token to the MCP initialize request.

Related XAA tracking issue: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/316625

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure an enterprise-managed MCP IdP:

2. Configure two HTTP MCP servers with enterprise-managed auth:

3. Start Server A.
4. Complete the IdP login / XAA flow.
5. Confirm Server A discovers tools successfully.
6. Start Server B.

## Expected

After Server A creates the IdP session, Server B should reuse that IdP session, silently mint/redeem its resource token, and send the token to Server B's `/mcp` endpoint.

## Actual

Server B fails with `No accounts available`. The MCP initialize request is retried without a bearer token and the server returns the normal initial 401 challenge.

MCP log for Server B:

VS Code XAA auth-provider log shows the IdP session exists and the Server B token exchange/redemption calls are made:

Resource server audit logs also confirm success for Server B:

There is no subsequent tool-list call for Server B, which suggests the redeemed token is not attached to the MCP request.

## Source-Level Hypothesis

This appears to happen in the account-selection path for a subsequent enterprise-managed server:

1. `mainThreadMcp.ts` calls `_getSessionForProvider(...)`.
2. For Server B, there is no existing MCP access grant/preference yet, so `_getSessionForProvider(...)` reaches account selection.
3. `AuthenticationMcpService.selectSession(...)` calls `this._authenticationService.getAccounts(providerId)`.
4. `AuthenticationService.getAccounts(...)` calls `getSessions(id)` without XAA-specific `resource`, `audience`, or scopes.
5. The XAA provider's `getSessions(...)` returns `[]` when those XAA options are missing.
6. `selectSession(...)` throws `No accounts available`, even though the XAA provider already minted/redeemed a valid Server B resource token.

## Possible Fix Direction

For XAA providers, avoid generic account enumeration in `selectSession()` when a resource-scoped session is already available, or make XAA expose the underlying IdP account(s) for account enumeration without requiring `resource` / `audience` options.

title: VS Code Speech: Right click to read aloud FROM SPECIFIC LOCATION in the response
document:
VS Code Speech: Right click to read aloud FROM SPECIFIC LOCATION in the response

This is similar to another item as linked below, but this request is slightly different in that I think it would be really useful to have the ability to right-click at any particular location in the agent's response and have the option to 'read aloud from that location' to the end.

In a lot of cases, the responses that I'm getting take a lot of time for read aloud to get through as they have a lot of the 'read' actions, analysis and all the info that I'm not really that interested in. I'm more interested in the distilled findings from the agent, which are at the end of the response. 

If there was the ability to right-click and read from here, I think that'd be really useful and a big time saver for those of us who avail of that feature. It's great for accessibility, but I mainly find it useful in allowing me to step away from my laptop while listening to say the detailed findings of an architectural review or a meaty security strategy etc and start digesting that via my headphones.

Other similar feature request I mentioned: [302997](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode/issues/302997)

Please Speed, I need this 🙏

title: ChatSessionItem.iconPath is not serialized — extension session items always show codicon-terminal in Sessions list
document:
ChatSessionItem.iconPath is not serialized — extension session items always show codicon-terminal in Sessions list

# `ChatSessionItem.iconPath` is not serialized — extension session items always show `codicon-terminal` in Sessions list

## Describe the bug

When an extension registers a `ChatSessionItemController` (via the `chatSessionsProvider` proposed API) and creates `ChatSessionItem` objects with a custom `iconPath`, the icon is **never rendered** in the Sessions list. Every extension-contributed session item shows the fallback `codicon-terminal` icon, regardless of what `iconPath` value the extension sets (`ThemeIcon`, `Uri`, or `{ light, dark }`).

## Root Cause

### 1. The type converter drops `iconPath`

The `ChatSessionItem.from()` converter in `src/vs/workbench/api/common/extHostTypeConverters.ts` returns a `Dto` but **omits the `iconPath` field entirely**:

As a result, `mainThreadChatSessions.ts` constructs `MainThreadChatSessionItem` with `dto.iconPath === undefined` for **every** item, no matter what the extension set.

### 2. The renderer falls back to a hardcoded icon

In `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/agentSessions/agentSessionsModel.ts`, the session list resolves icons per session type:

Since `session.iconPath` is always `undefined` (due to the serialization gap above), extension-contributed sessions **always** get `Codicon.terminal`.

### 3. Type mismatch between public API and internal DTO

| Layer | `iconPath` type |
|-------|-----------------|
| Public API (`vscode.proposed.chatSessionsProvider.d.ts`) | `IconPath` (`Uri \| { light: Uri; dark: Uri } \| ThemeIcon`) |
| Internal DTO (`IChatSessionItem` in `chatSessionsService.ts`) | `ThemeIcon` only |
| Converter return (`Dto`) | field absent |

Even if the converter were fixed to pass `iconPath` through, the internal type only accepts `ThemeIcon` — `Uri` / `{ light, dark }` variants would need the internal type widened or a conversion path added.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Create an extension with the `chatSessionsProvider` proposed API enabled.

2. Register a custom icon font in `contributes.icons`:

 

3. Register a `ChatSessionItemController` and create an item with a custom icon:

 

4. Open the Sessions list (the chat history panel).

## Expected behavior

The session item row shows the `my-ext-logo` icon (the custom `ThemeIcon`).

## Actual behavior

The session item row shows `codicon-terminal` (the hardcoded fallback). Setting `iconPath` to a `Uri` or `{ light, dark }` produces the same result — the field is never serialized.

## Environment

- VSCode version: `1.125.1`
- Proposed API: `chatSessionsProvider`
- Platform: Windows x64

## Proposed fix

### Minimal fix — pass `ThemeIcon` through

In `src/vs/workbench/api/common/extHostTypeConverters.ts`, add the `iconPath` mapping to `ChatSessionItem.from()`:

### Full fix — support all `IconPath` variants

1. Widen `IChatSessionItem.iconPath` in `chatSessionsService.ts` from `ThemeIcon` to `IconPath` (or `ThemeIcon | URI`).
2. Add `Uri` → `URI` conversion in the converter (similar to how `TreeItem.iconPath` is handled in `extHostTypeConverters.ts`).
3. Update the renderer in `agentSessionsModel.ts` / `agentSessionsViewer.ts` to resolve `IconPath` (Uri-based icons would render via ``, `ThemeIcon` via CSS class).

The `TreeItem` pipeline already handles this union type correctly and can serve as a reference implementation.

## Additional context

- The `chatSessions` contribution's `icon` field (`$(my-ext-logo)`) **does** work correctly — it controls the icon in the session-target dropdown (where the user picks between Local / Cloud / extension-provided types). This issue is specifically about the **per-session-item icon** in the sessions list panel, which is controlled by `ChatSessionItem.iconPath`.
- The `ChatSessionItemController.createChatSessionItem()` API and the `item.iconPath` setter appear to work correctly on the extension-host side (the `ChatSessionItemImpl` getter/setter fires `#onChanged()` and re-syncs). The data is lost only at the serialization boundary.

title: Single Window Multi-Project Workspaces with Project Tabs
document:
Single Window Multi-Project Workspaces with Project Tabs

I would like to request a feature that allows developers to work with multiple independent projects inside a single VS Code window.

Currently, when developers work on separate projects at the same time, such as a React frontend and a .NET backend stored in different repositories, they must open multiple VS Code windows. This becomes difficult to manage, especially for full-stack developers.

**Although Multi-root Workspaces already exist, they still behave as one workspace and do not provide fully isolated project environments.**

### Example Scenario

> Frontend project: React application
> Backend project: .NET API

**Both projects need to:**

> Run simultaneously.
> Have separate terminals.
> Maintain independent debugging configurations.
> Keep separate tasks and settings.
> Allow quick switching between projects.
> Currently, this requires opening two separate VS Code windows.
> Proposed Solution

### Add a new option such as:

> Open New Project
> Add Project to Window
> This option could be available from the Explorer panel menu (three-dot menu) or the File menu.
> When opening another project, VS Code would create a new project tab inside the same window.

### Example Explorer layout:

> Project 1
> Project 2
> Outline
> Timeline

### Today, the sidebar typically contains:

> Workspace
> Outline
> Timeline
> With this feature, each project would appear as its own top-level section, allowing developers to switch between projects without opening additional windows.

### Expected Behavior

> Each project maintains its own terminals.
> Each project has independent debugging sessions.
> Tasks and launch configurations remain isolated.
> Projects can run simultaneously.
> Developers can quickly switch between projects within one window.

### Benefits

> Less desktop and window clutter.
> Better experience for full-stack developers.
> Easier frontend and backend development workflows.
> Improved productivity for microservices and multi-repository projects.
> Better use of screen space, especially on laptops and smaller monitors.

This would provide a tab-based multi-project experience while preserving the existing VS Code workflow and keeping projects logically separated within a single application window.

title: Support publishing extensions with organization-scoped PATs due to global PATs being retired
document:
Support publishing extensions with organization-scoped PATs due to global PATs being retired

I had an email today from Microsoft:

> Action required: Azure DevOps global PATs are retiring
>
> Global PATs allow a single token to access all Azure DevOps organizations you belong to. To reduce risk from broadly scoped credentials and support regional data requirements, this token type is being retired.
>
> You’re receiving this email because one or more global personal access token(s) associated with your account was used in 1 organization(s) in the last 30 days.

The only global PAT I know of is the one used to publish extensions. I believe it's currently a requirement for this to be global, and the docs at https://code.visualstudio.com/api/working-with-extensions/publishing-extension do say to make it global:

> In the Create a new personal access token modal, select the following details for the token:
> Organization: **All accessible organizations**

So this is a request to support organization-specific tokens before December 1st 2026. If it's already supported (I haven't tested it recently), then just the docs need updating.

title: Custom BYOK endpoint: "Sorry, no response was returned" when empty tool_calls: [] on content deltas
document:
Custom BYOK endpoint: "Sorry, no response was returned" when empty tool_calls: [] on content deltas

I have searched existing issues and this is not a duplicate. Related closed issues: #255934, #253126

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: Built-in (0.53.1)
- VS Code Version: 1.125.1 (x64)
- OS Version: Windows 11
- Feature: Ask mode
- Selected model: Custom BYOK endpoint — moonshotai/Kimi-K2.6 via chatLanguageModels.json
- Logs:

1. Configure a custom endpoint in chatLanguageModels.json with toolCalling: true pointing to a vLLM-backed server (identified via system_fingerprint: "vllm-0.22.1" in responses)
2. Send any prompt in VS Code chat

Expected: 
Response content is displayed.

Actual: 
Model reasoning steps appear in the UI but no response content is shown — "Sorry, no response was returned."

Root cause: vLLM emits "tool_calls": [] (empty array) on every content delta even when no tool call is being made. VS Code treats any tool_calls field presence as a tool invocation and discards the content, logging the request as badRequest. Confirmed by running a local proxy that strips empty tool_calls arrays — with the proxy in place requests log as success and respond correctly. Backends returning null instead of [] work fine.

Example offending SSE chunk from the server:
 

Previously reported in #255934 and #253126 (both closed).

Suggested fix:
Only process tool_calls deltas when the array is non-empty (tool_calls.length > 0).

title: Chat history disappears after system restart / VS Code close (Paid Copilot Pro User)
document:
Chat history disappears after system restart / VS Code close (Paid Copilot Pro User)

### Describe the bug
I am a paying GitHub Copilot Pro subscriber, and I am experiencing a critical issue where my chat history completely disappears after restarting my computer or closing VS Code. 

When I return to work the next day and click on the chat icon or look for previous conversations, the chat area is completely blank, and clicking on past sessions does nothing. No previous conversation is loaded or displayed.

### To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Open VS Code and start a chat session with GitHub Copilot Chat.
2. Ask questions and get responses.
3. Shut down the computer (or close VS Code) and restart it later/the next day.
4. Open VS Code and open the Copilot Chat panel.
5. Click on the past chat history/sessions.
6. **Result:** Nothing happens. The conversation is gone, and the panel remains blank.

### Expected behavior
As a paid service, Copilot Chat should reliably persist and reload past conversations across VS Code restarts and system reboots, allowing users to resume their work context.

### Environment details
- OS: Windows 11
- VS Code Version: 1.126.0

title: Add option to automatically suppress Copilot inline completions during merge conflict resolution
document:
Add option to automatically suppress Copilot inline completions during merge conflict resolution

When resolving merge conflicts, Copilot inline completions often create visual and cognitive noise. During conflict resolution I typically want to focus on comparing and choosing changes rather than receiving code suggestions.

Please add a setting to automatically suppress Copilot inline completions when merge conflicts are active.

Suggested Behavior:

* Suppress inline completions in files containing unresolved conflict markers.
* Suppress inline completions in the Merge Editor / Conflict Resolution Editor.
* Restore normal behavior automatically once conflicts are resolved.

Possible setting names:

* `github.copilot.inlineCompletions.suppressDuringMergeConflicts`
* `github.copilot.inlineCompletions.disableInMergeEditor`

This would reduce distraction and help keep merge resolution focused on the task at hand.

(previously asked in Copilot Conversations in 2024 in https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/140818)

title: Feature request: allow per-option descriptions in snippet choice placeholders (`${1|...|}`)
document:
Feature request: allow per-option descriptions in snippet choice placeholders (`${1|...|}`)

# Feature request: allow per-option descriptions in snippet choice placeholders (`${1|...|}`)

## Summary
When a snippet uses a **choice placeholder** (`${1|a,b,c|}`), the dropdown shows only the raw option values. There is no way to attach an explanatory **description/label** to each option. I would like each choice option to be able to carry a short description that is rendered **at the end of the line**, the same way a snippet's own `description` is shown in the IntelliSense suggestion widget when you start typing the snippet.

## Current behavior
A choice option is plain text only. The grammar defines it as:

So when the choice dropdown opens, the user just sees the bare values with nothing to explain what each one means.

Example snippet:

When the snippet itself is suggested, IntelliSense already shows a description at the end of the line ("Admonition"):

But when the **choice placeholder** dropdown opens, the options `!!!`, `???`, `???+` have no description at all - the user cannot tell what each marker does:

## Desired behavior
Allow each choice option to declare a description that the dropdown renders **at the end of the line**, mirroring the snippet-suggestion widget. For the example above the dropdown could read:

This is purely additive: existing snippets without descriptions keep working unchanged.

## Possible syntax

- Inline separator per option, e.g. `${1|!!!:static (always shown),???:collapsible closed,???+:collapsible open|}` (would require choosing a separator that does not clash with values).
- A richer object form in the snippet JSON for choices, mapping each value to a description.

## Why
Choice placeholders are often cryptic markers (here: Material/MkDocs admonition markers `!!!` / `???` / `???+`). Descriptions would let snippet authors document each option in place, exactly like the description already shown for the snippet as a whole.

title: Git inline diff decorations persist even after "scm.diffDecorations" set to "none"
document:
Git inline diff decorations persist even after "scm.diffDecorations" set to "none"

Setting "scm.diffDecorations": "none" in settings.json does not remove 
inline diff decorations from the editor. The old (strikethrough) and new 
(arrow + text) code changes are still visible inline inside the editor, 
making the code unreadable.

1. Have a file tracked by Git with uncommitted changes
2. Open settings.json and set "scm.diffDecorations": "none"
3. Reload VS Code
4. Open any modified file

Expected Behavior:
No diff decorations should appear in the editor.

Actual Behavior:
Inline diff decorations still show — deleted lines appear as strikethrough 
text and new lines appear with an arrow (→) prefix, overlapping the actual 
code.

Environment:
- OS: Windows
- VS Code Version: (add your version — Help → About)
- Extensions: GitLens

Possible Cause:
GitLens may be overriding the scm.diffDecorations setting and rendering 
its own inline change decorations independently.

Workaround Attempted:
- Set "scm.diffDecorations": "none" → no effect
- Set "gitlens.changes.locations": [] → no effect

title: Extensions in custom --extensions-dir are unexpectedly deleted after VS Code updates
document:
Extensions in custom --extensions-dir are unexpectedly deleted after VS Code updates

1. Launch VS Code with a custom extensions directory using the CLI argument, for example: code --extensions-dir "path/to/your/custom/directory"
2. Install several extensions and use VS Code normally.
3. Wait for a new VS Code update to be released and let VS Code perform the update.
4. After the update, launch VS Code again. (Note: The extensions are usually still present during the first 1 or 2 launches immediately after the update).
5. Continue using VS Code. After a few more launches (usually randomly within a day or two), launch VS Code again.

title: Org-managed policy distribution and audit
document:
Org-managed policy distribution and audit

**Best fit: Hooks + MCP Servers**

Org-managed policy distribution and audit are missing for Copilot agent workflows.

Local setup is not enough for teams that need governance. They need policy that can be pushed centrally, versioned, and audited across repos.

The first version should support:
- org-level policy packs
- policy version visibility
- override visibility
- audit of agent changes and policy decisions

This is a governance gap, not just a UI gap.

This insight is derived from the findings in this study: https://github.com/orgs/coreai-microsoft/projects/40/views/1?filterQuery=assignee%3A%40me&pane=issue&itemId=170494081&issue=coreai-microsoft%7Ccaidr%7C766

title: Native customization surface for rule packs and instructions
document:
Native customization surface for rule packs and instructions

**Best fit: Instructions + Agents**

VS Code needs a real place for reusable agent instructions and rule packs.

Today, this is scattered across files and manual setup, which makes it hard to know what rules are active or keep them consistent across repos.

A native surface should let users:
- manage repo-level instructions
- reuse shared presets
- see what rules are active
- override defaults when needed

If this stays file-only, it will remain hard to discover and hard to govern.

This insight is derived from the findings in this study: https://github.com/orgs/coreai-microsoft/projects/40/views/1?filterQuery=assignee%3A%40me&pane=issue&itemId=170494081&issue=coreai-microsoft%7Ccaidr%7C766

title: Guardrail status in the Agent window
document:
Guardrail status in the Agent window

**Best fit: Agents**

Agents need a visible guardrail status while they run.

Right now, it’s too easy for an agent to look “done” when checks haven’t run, have failed, or have been bypassed. That makes the workflow feel untrustworthy.

At minimum, the Agents area should show:
- whether required checks ran
- whether they passed or failed
- what was auto-fixed
- what is advisory vs blocking
- why the agent cannot finish yet

Without this, guardrails stay hidden and users have to guess what happened.

This insight is derived from the findings in this study: https://github.com/orgs/coreai-microsoft/projects/40/views/1?filterQuery=assignee%3A%40me&pane=issue&itemId=170494081&issue=coreai-microsoft%7Ccaidr%7C766

title: Chat appears signed-out in untrusted workspaces (Sign In button + post-Trust sign-in dialog)
document:
Chat appears signed-out in untrusted workspaces (Sign In button + post-Trust sign-in dialog)

Follow-up to #322564.

In an untrusted workspace, Chat presents the user as **signed out even when signed in**, because auth/entitlement comes from the Copilot extension, which is disabled in untrusted workspaces. Symptoms:

1. "Sign In" button shows in title bar / accounts menu though signed in.
2. After clicking **Trust Workspace…**, the "Sign in to use GitHub Copilot" dialog flashes for ~5–6s, then disappears once the extension activates and entitlement resolves.
3. ~5–6s delay before models load / chat is usable after Trust.

**Root cause:** `chatEntitlementService.resolve()` reads `IDefaultAccountService.getDefaultAccount()`, whose provider is set by the Copilot extension. Untrusted ⇒ extension off ⇒ no account ⇒ entitlement `Unknown` (signed-out). After Trust, `chatSetupRunner.doRun()` (`:114`) decides the sign-in dialog from this still-unresolved entitlement, so it shows the modal until the extension activates.

**Regression?**
- The architecture (auth/entitlement/models gated on the trust-gated extension) is pre-existing. Increased *exposure* is a regression from no longer showing the startup workspace-trust dialog (see #322564).
- The specific UI bugs (Sign In affordances ignoring untrusted; dialog decided from unresolved entitlement) are pre-existing, **not** introduced by the model-picker PR.
- The model-load delay is inherent activation/network latency, not a regression.

**Deterministic "pending vs signed-out" signal exists:** `defaultAccountStatus` = `Uninitialized` (`defaultAccount.ts:61,66`), `DefaultAccountProvider.initialized`/`initPromise` (`:292,317-320`), and `ChatEntitlement.Unresolved` vs `Unknown` (`chatEntitlementService.ts:81-82,453-457`).

**Proposed follow-up:**
1. In `doRun()`, after Trust from untrusted, await account/entitlement resolution before the `:114` dialog decision so already-signed-in users skip the dialog.
2. Make the Sign In affordances untrusted-aware at the source. The model-picker PR adds `Setup.untrusted.negate()` guards in `chatSetupContributions.ts` as a stopgap; the source fix makes them redundant.

title: Memory str_replace tool silently drops large newString values (coerces to string "undefined")
document:
Memory str_replace tool silently drops large newString values (coerces to string "undefined")

GitHub Copilot Chat extension (the `memory` tool is provided by that extension),
so disabling it disables the feature under test.

- VS Code Version: 1.125.1
- OS Version: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.6 (Plow), kernel 5.14.0-570.84.1.el9_6.x86_64

1. Open agent mode in GitHub Copilot Chat with a memory file > 200 lines (any
 markdown file under `/memories/` accessible via the `memory` tool).
2. Invoke the `memory` tool with `command: "str_replace"` and a `newString`
 parameter containing ~5300 characters / ~75 lines of markdown — content
 should include section headers (`###`), nested bullet lists, inline code
 spans (backticks), and a markdown table (` | ` separators).
3. The tool returns success with no error message.
4. Invoke `memory` with `command: "view"` on the same file and inspect the
 replacement location.

**Expected:** the full `newString` content is written verbatim at the location
of `oldString`.

**Actual:** the literal 9-character string `"undefined"` (without quotes) is
written at the replacement location. The `oldString` is correctly removed, and
content before and after the replacement point survives intact — only the
`newString` payload is dropped and replaced with `undefined`. This is the
classic JavaScript `undefined.toString()` coercion fingerprint, suggesting
the `newString` parameter is dropped/nulled at the JSON-RPC serialization
boundary between the language model and the tool host.

**Workaround:** split `newString` into sequential ≤ 2000-character chunks
and call `memory str_replace` / `memory insert` once per chunk. Each
sub-2000-char call succeeds. Empirically, the threshold appears to be in
the 4096–5300 char range — values below ~4000 always succeed; values
above ~5000 always fail with the `undefined` coercion.

**Reproducibility:** confirmed across 3 distinct incidents in a single
agent-mode session on 2026-06-23, all with `newString` between 4500 and
5800 characters of markdown.

**Related observation:** in prior sessions, large `memory insert` calls
with multi-line backtick-fenced content produced a different but related
failure mode — mid-file content duplication (a header block was injected
into an unrelated section of the same file). Both failures may share the
same serialization-boundary root cause; size threshold and content
structure both appear to influence which mode triggers.

**Impact:** silent data loss. The tool returns success, the agent
assumes the write succeeded, and the corruption is only detected when
the agent (or user) next reads the file. In one incident the corrupted
content was a critical workflow rule; recovery required rebuilding
from a separate source-of-truth file.

**Suggested fix areas to investigate:**
- JSON-RPC max-payload limit on tool parameters between model and host
- Possible truncation in the request serializer that produces a valid
 but empty/undefined parameter rather than rejecting the call
- The tool host's parameter validator should reject (not coerce)
 `undefined` for a required string parameter

title: repeated failures
document:
repeated failures

Window: Agents

Dear Help Desk,

I'm reporting a systematic reliability problem with the Copilot CLI agent in my VS Code workflow.

The Pattern:
Over multiple sessions, the agent has been asked to follow a mandatory startup gate (documented in project archives) before performing any work. This gate requires reading critical system documentation to prevent repeated failures. The agent consistently skips this gate, attempts work from incomplete knowledge, fails multiple times, and only succeeds after repeated correction and escalation.

Today's Example:

Task: Fix website cache configuration
Correct method: Read project-specific documentation first, then execute deployment workflow
What the agent did: Made assumptions about deployment method, attempted multiple incorrect approaches over several iterations, wasted significant time and tokens
Result: Only succeeded after being forced to read the actual documentation
The Core Problem:
The agent admits it will repeatedly make this mistake because:

No enforcement mechanism prevents skipping the startup gate
The agent is biased toward speed over accuracy
In every future session, the same pattern will repeat
Success requires multiple failed attempts and user escalation before the agent reads required documentation
When asked for a solution, the agent acknowledged: No enforcement structure will fix this. It will rationalize around any rule.

Logical Conclusion:
Given that the failures are documented, repeated identically across sessions, and require user escalation to correct, it is a logical conclusion that the system is designed to not work properly and to waste time and money repeatedly.

Recommendation:
Do not use this tool for production work at all.

title: Maximized secondary windows are not restored properly when main window reloads or app relaunches
document:
Maximized secondary windows are not restored properly when main window reloads or app relaunches

1. Open any file or resource that can occupy an editor tab, or start a new copilot chat.
2. Drag that tab out to a new window or use the "Move [this] to New Window" command from the tab or chat context menu.
3. Maximize the secondary undocked window.
4. Reload the vscode instance using the "Developer: Reload Window" command from either the main or secondary window.
5. Notice that after reload, the previously maximized window is missing entirely, and not present in the OS window picker.
6. Notice that repeating step 4 will properly restore the maximized window from two reloads previous.
7. Notice that instead of step 4, if you close and relaunch the app, all references to the maximized windows are lost forever and cannot be restored by future reloads.

title: Extension host frozen ~14s by synchronous require() of undici llhttp on Copilot's first HTTPS request (starves other extensions / language clients at startup)
document:
Extension host frozen ~14s by synchronous require() of undici llhttp on Copilot's first HTTPS request (starves other extensions / language clients at startup)

**VS Code version:** Version: 1.125.1 (user setup)

ElectronBuildId: 14159160

**Extension:** built-in `copilot` extension (`resources/app/extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js`)

### Summary

During workspace startup, the extension-host main thread is frozen for **~14 seconds inside a single synchronous `require()`** triggered by the built-in Copilot extension's first HTTPS request. Because the entire extension host shares one JS thread, this blocks **all** other extensions' timers, microtasks, and I/O callbacks for the duration. In my case it delayed the Pylance language client from sending its `initialize` request to the (already-spawned) language server by ~14–60s, making the language server appear to "hang" at startup.

### Root cause

When Copilot opens its **first** HTTPS connection, the TLS handshake completes and `undici` **lazily `require()`s its HTTP/1 parser (`llhttp`)** from inside the connection callback. That `require` is a **synchronous CommonJS module load** (`readFileSync` → `readFileUtf8`). On Windows the read appears to be heavily amplified by Defender real-time scanning and disk contention from other extensions spawning child processes at startup, stretching this one synchronous load to ~14s.

A synchronous `require` cannot yield, so for those ~14s the event loop runs **zero** timers/microtasks/I-O callbacks — every other extension and any language client running in the host is starved.

### Evidence (CPU profile)

The startup window (0–35s) is **~95% CPU-saturated** (33.4s busy / 35s). Within it there is a **single contiguous 13.9s run (+7.8s → +21.7s)** where `readFileSync`/`readFileUtf8` is the leaf on the stack, and **100% of the samples in that run (8315/8315) root to the `copilot` extension**. Representative stack (leaf → root):

During this whole window the affected language client received only ~0.6s of CPU — i.e. it was effectively frozen, not doing async work of its own.

### Impact

- Any extension or language client sharing the extension host stalls for the duration of the synchronous load.
- Symptom observed: a language server that has already spawned sits idle for up to ~60s before receiving `initialize`, looking like a hang.
- Worse on Windows due to Defender scanning + concurrent child-process disk contention at startup.

### Suggested mitigations

1. **Pre-warm undici's `llhttp` asynchronously** (or eagerly `import()` it) before/at activation, off the hot connection path, so the first HTTPS request doesn't trigger a synchronous module load on the shared thread.
2. Avoid issuing the first network request synchronously during the busiest part of startup, or defer it until after the host has settled.
3. (Upstream) consider an async `llhttp` load path in `undici` so `connectH1` doesn't `require()` synchronously inside the TLS callback.

### Workaround for users

Adding a Microsoft Defender exclusion for the VS Code install directory noticeably reduces the synchronous read time.

title: Renamed chat session title reverts to auto-generated name on reopen (1.126.0)
document:
Renamed chat session title reverts to auto-generated name on reopen (1.126.0)

- VS Code Version: 1.126.0 (user setup)
- Commit: 7e7950df89d055b5a378379db9ee14290772148a
- OS Version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a chat/agent session that has an auto-generated title.
2. Open the sessions list (`Chat: Show Chats...`) and rename the session to a custom name (e.g. "Access Management and PR reviews").
3. The new name appears to save.
4. Close and reopen the session (or reload the window).

## Expected

The custom title persists.

## Actual

The title reverts to the previous auto-generated name as soon as the session is reopened. The manual rename does not persist.

## Notes

- Reproduces both when renaming from the open tab and from the sessions list.
- `chat.sessionSync.enabled` is enabled (default); unsure if sync is overwriting the local rename.

## Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Not tested

title: Nudge EDU/Student users to Copilot Pro instead of Pro+ when limits are reached
document:
Nudge EDU/Student users to Copilot Pro instead of Pro+ when limits are reached

## Problem

EDU/Student users currently appear to be routed through the same quota messaging as the `individual` plan. In `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/chatErrorMessages.ts`, `ChatEntitlement.EDU` maps to `copilotPlan: 'individual'`, and the quota messaging for `individual` tells users to upgrade to **Copilot Pro+**.

For EDU/Student users, we should nudge them to **Copilot Pro** instead.

## Expected

When EDU/Student users hit Copilot limits, the upgrade messaging should direct them to **Copilot Pro**, not **Copilot Pro+**.

## Notes

Relevant code paths:
- `getCopilotPlanFromEntitlement(ChatEntitlement.EDU)` currently returns `'individual'`
- `getQuotaMessageForPlan('individual', ...)` uses messaging that says to upgrade to Copilot Pro+

title: Excessive Debug Logging in Extension Host - InlineEditRequestLogContext
document:
Excessive Debug Logging in Extension Host - InlineEditRequestLogContext

## Title
Excessive Debug Logging in Extension Host - InlineEditRequestLogContext

## Issue Type
Bug / Performance Issue

## Description
The GitHub Copilot extension is outputting excessive debug logging to the VSCode developer console, which is polluting the console output and potentially impacting performance.

## Console Output

## Impact

1. **Console Pollution**: The debug logs are cluttering the developer console, making it difficult to identify real issues
2. **Performance Concern**: Frequent logging may impact extension performance
3. **User Experience**: Non-technical users may be confused or concerned by these messages
4. **Debug Interference**: These logs interfere with legitimate debugging activities

## Expected Behavior

Debug logs should either:
- Be removed entirely from production builds
- Be enabled only in debug/verbose mode
- Use VSCode's logging API instead of `console.log`
- Have a higher threshold before outputting

## Actual Behavior

Debug logs are being output to the console on every inline edit request, regardless of the operation outcome.

## Environment

- **VSCode Version**: last_version
- **GitHub Copilot Extension Version**: last_version
- **OS**: macOS (Darwin 24.6.0)
- **Reproducibility**: Consistent - occurs frequently during normal usage

## Suggested Fix

1. Remove debug `console.log` statements from production code
2. Implement a logging level system that can be controlled via settings
3. Use VSCode's built-in logging API with appropriate log levels
4. Consider using telemetry for internal tracking instead of console output

## Additional Context

This appears to be a leftover from development/debugging that was not cleaned up before release. The `[InlineEditRequestLogContext]` tag suggests this is related to the inline editing feature's request handling logic.

## Labels

- bug
- logging
- performance
- quality

When is the plan to move it from an "Experimental" to "Stable" featureset. Its a useful feature and I believe it makes no sense to still keep it an experimental state or leave it to be managed by org policy.

title: Clone Repository in Container Volume list previously cloned repositories
document:
Clone Repository in Container Volume list previously cloned repositories

I am a user who has a repo with a devcontainer. I have designed the container to require no dependencies on startup (can start in a fresh directory without concern). I frequently want to work on multiple features and utilize `Clone Repository into Container Volume` for this. However, this requires me to type in the git repo url every single time I want to do this.

I am requesting a faster way to handle the workflow of creating another ephemeral devcontainer of a previously used repository. 

I have two proposed paths to achieve a more efficient workflow for this:
* Having a dropdown of recently used repositories (with potentially a way to star ones to always show up) that shows under the git url field in the existing `Clone Repository into Container Volume` popup. These values would need to be persisted at the level of the user running VSCode
* The ability to "open a fork of my current devcontainer": If I am already in a devcontainer, support detecting the repo that is in use for it and support another copy of the repo directly into another ephemeral devcontainer

title: Issues with using output from models of Custom Endpoint
document:
Issues with using output from models of Custom Endpoint

- VS Code Version: 1.125.1 (user setup)
- OS Version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

I am using a Custom Endpoint for using a list of models. I noticed that when I am using OpenAI GPT-OSS-120B model, the extension works as expected and shows both thinking trace and the output content message. 

However, when I use Kimi K2.7 Code, I can see the thinking trace and that it is able to use the tools and apply changes, but it errors out when trying to display the output content message. 

I checked the output of both the models to see if there are any formatting issues, but I saw that the structure of the output is the exact same for both, but GitHub copilot chat fails to parse Kimi output as opposed to the GPT OSS model. Here are curl outputs for example:

title: Agent plugin skills show commit SHA as slash command name when installed from SHA-pinned marketplace entry
document:
Agent plugin skills show commit SHA as slash command name when installed from SHA-pinned marketplace entry

**VS Code version:** 1.125.1

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. Search `@agentPlugins` in the Extensions activity bar
2. Install the **Datadog** plugin from the awesome-copilot marketplace
3. Open chat and type `/` to see available skills

**Expected:** Skills appear as `/datadog:ddsetup`, `/datadog:ddconfig`, `/datadog:ddtoolsets` (using the plugin `name` + skill `name` frontmatter fields)

**Actual:** Skills appear as `/sha_b003fcad48c3a935ffe04b6218f5cf58fe2b6760...` — the commit SHA from the marketplace entry

**Root cause:** The awesome-copilot `marketplace.json` pins the Datadog plugin to a specific commit SHA. When VS Code installs from a SHA-pinned entry, it uses the commit SHA as the skill name prefix instead of the skill's `name` frontmatter field.

The plugin itself is correctly structured — skill `name` fields match their directory names, and `plugin.json` is well-formed:
- `skills/ddsetup/SKILL.md` has `name: ddsetup`
- `skills/ddconfig/SKILL.md` has `name: ddconfig`
- `skills/ddtoolsets/SKILL.md` has `name: ddtoolsets`
- `plugin.json` has `name: datadog`

Source: [`datadog-labs/vscode-plugin`](https://github.com/datadog-labs/vscode-plugin) at `b003fcad48c3a935ffe04b6218f5cf58fe2b6760`

Reported by @jfree.

title: Runaway thread/process growth when opening a workspace on a slow network share
document:
Runaway thread/process growth when opening a workspace on a slow network share

When working in a project located on a **slow network directory**, VS Code appears to trigger a rapid increase in the number of **threads and processes** on the system.

This happens especially when trying to save a file while directory scanning/background loading is still ongoing. In that situation, VS Code seems to generate even more activity against the slow network location, and resource usage starts climbing aggressively.

Over time, the machine becomes heavily loaded and responsiveness degrades significantly. It looks like VS Code may be issuing too many requests against the slow network location, eventually causing resource usage to "blow up" and effectively burn CPU/system resources.

I cannot rule out that part of this is related to the network client itself, but the steadily increasing number of requests against the slow machine seems likely to be triggered by the editor.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Open a folder/workspace located on a slow network share / network-mounted directory.
2. Let VS Code start scanning/loading the directory.
3. While that scanning/loading is still in progress, try to save a file.
4. Observe system resource usage in Windows Task Manager over time.
5. Notice that the number of processes/threads/handles keeps increasing instead of staying stable.

### Actual result
- Process/thread/handle counts keep growing while VS Code is open on the slow network directory.
- The problem is more likely / more severe when saving a file during ongoing directory scanning.
- System CPU usage and overall load increase noticeably.
- The machine becomes unstable/sluggish.
- Even after closing VS Code, the system may remain elevated for a while before dropping again.

### Expected result
- VS Code should handle slow network directories gracefully.
- Saving a file during background scanning should not trigger runaway activity.
- Background activity should be throttled or bounded.
- Process/thread/request counts should not grow unbounded when the workspace is on a slow network location.

### Notes
- The attached screenshots show Task Manager values rising over time while VS Code is open.
- The last screenshot is after closing VS Code.
- This may involve file watching, directory scanning, search, extension host activity, or repeated retries against the network path.

 
 
 
 

### Version

ElectronBuildId: 14159160

title: Copilot Chat renders `./...` inside inline code as a parent-folder inline reference
document:
Copilot Chat renders `./...` inside inline code as a parent-folder inline reference

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: built-in VS Code `copilot` extension from `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\fcf604774b\resources\app\extensions\copilot`; `code --list-extensions --show-versions` does not list `GitHub.copilot-chat`
- VS Code Version: 1.125.1 (`fcf604774b9f2674b473065736ee75077e256353`, x64)
- OS Version: Windows 11 Pro 25H2, OS build 26200.8246, Windows Feature Experience Pack 1000.26100.297.0
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): Chat panel, Ask mode / normal chat response rendering
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): Custom API via UnifyChatProvider, GPT-5.5
- Logs: I have an exported `chat.json`; relevant excerpt is included below.

Steps to Reproduce:

1. Open VS Code with a workspace nested inside a named parent folder, for example:

 

2. Open Copilot Chat.

3. Ask Copilot Chat to repeat a command containing `./...` inside inline code:

 

4. If it does not reproduce on the first try, add workspace/file context and ask again:

 

Expected:

The chat response should visibly render exactly:



Actual:

The stored model/tool-round response is correct, but the rendered chat message can replace `./..` with an inline file/folder reference to the parent directory and leave the final `.` as text.

In my case the workspace was under:



The parent folder was `pylink`, so the UI rendered the command as:



Evidence from the exported chat JSON:

The stored final response for request 8 is correct:



But the rendered response parts were split into text + inlineReference + text:



This appears to turn `./...` into:



Impact:

- The visible chat output changes the literal content of a command inside inline code.
- It makes users think the model generated the wrong command even when the stored response is correct.
- `go test ./...` is a common Go command, so this is easy to hit in development chats.
- The same issue may affect other command tokens containing `./...` or similar relative path syntax.

Possible implementation clue:

This looks related to chat inline reference annotation/linkification rather than model output. It may be near chat inline reference handling in `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/widget/annotations.ts` or `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/chatContentParts/chatMarkdownContentPart.ts`. There appears to already be coverage for inline references inside backtick code spans, but `./...` may be a missing edge case where `./..` is recognized as a path reference before/while code-span handling runs.

title: [Feature Request] Allow configuring Copilot agent/skill/instruction discovery paths via settings
document:
[Feature Request] Allow configuring Copilot agent/skill/instruction discovery paths via settings

Currently, VS Code has two separate systems for discovering Copilot agent files:

Workbench-side (.agent.md files): Already configurable via the chat.agent.fileLocations setting.
Agent host-side (Copilot SDK session discovery in sessionCustomizationDiscovery.ts): Paths are hardcoded and cannot be customized.
The agent host side scans a fixed set of directories for agents, skills, and instruction files:

Workspace:

.github/agents, .agents/agents, .claude/agents (agents)
.github/skills, .agents/skills, .claude/skills (skills)
.github/instructions (instructions)
User home:

.copilot/agents (agents)
.agents/skills (skills)
.copilot/instructions (instructions)
Instruction files (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, etc.) are also discovered from hardcoded paths.

Problem
Teams and individuals who organize their repositories differently (e.g., monorepos, custom tooling conventions, or non-standard directory layouts) cannot point the agent host discovery to their preferred locations. There is no escape hatch — the only option is to restructure the repository to match the hardcoded paths.

In contrast, the workbench-side already demonstrates that this is solvable: PromptsConfig.AGENTS_LOCATION_KEY allows users to specify custom paths for .agent.md files via settings. The agent host side lacks an equivalent mechanism.

Proposed Solution
Expose the agent host searchRoots (and optionally agentInstructions) as a VS Code setting, similar to how chat.agent.fileLocations works for the workbench side. For example:

// settings.json 
"chat.agent.hostDiscoveryPaths": { 
 "workspace": { 
 "agents": [".github/agents", "my-team/agents"], 
 "skills": [".github/skills"], 
 "instructions": [".github/instructions"] 
 }, 
 "user": { 
 "agents": ["~/.copilot/agents", "~/.my-org/agents"] 
 } 
}
The setting should:

Accept relative paths (resolved from workspace root) and ~/-prefixed paths (resolved from user home).
Default to the current hardcoded values so existing behavior is preserved.
Be marked as restricted: true (workspace trust) consistent with the existing chat.agent.fileLocations setting.
Relevant Code
Hardcoded discovery: src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/sessionCustomizationDiscovery.ts (searchRoots, agentInstructions)
Configurable workbench-side equivalent: src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/promptSyntax/config/promptFileLocations.ts (DEFAULT_AGENT_SOURCE_FOLDERS)
Setting registration: src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/chat.shared.contribution.ts (AGENTS_LOCATION_KEY)
Use Cases
Monorepos where agents live in a subdirectory (e.g., packages/ai-tools/agents)
Organizations with a shared agents directory outside the standard .github/ convention
Users who want to keep agent files alongside their dotfiles in a non-default home directory path

title: Keyboard shortcut for Custom Editor not working on VS Code for the web
document:
Keyboard shortcut for Custom Editor not working on VS Code for the web

Several Keyboard shortcuts (probably those including Shift key) for a custom editor does not work on VS Code for the Web.

An example is `markdown.togglePreview` ("Markdown: Toggle Preview"), whose default keyboard shortcut is Shift+Cmd+V for macOS and Shift+Ctrl+V for other platforms. This shortcut toggles the editor of a markdown file between the built-in editor and markdown preview on the VS Code desktop version. However, on VS Code for the Web this keyboard shortcut does not switch the editor from the markdown preview to the built-in editor. Selecting from Command Palette, it works as expected.

I checked this problem occurs on both Safari/macOS and Edge/Windows. The problem is reproduced on another custom editor. 

I also noticed that, pressing a Shift key on VS Code for the Web changes several icons on the editor toolbar, just like pressing an Option/Alt key. On VS Code desktop version, this kind of toolbar action toggling occurs only when an Option/Alt key is pressed. I guess a Shift key input may be intercepted by mistake on VS Code for the Web.

title: Bearer token authentication for custom language model endpoints
document:
Bearer token authentication for custom language model endpoints

**Summary**

While using the Bring your own language model key feature in VS Code, the custom endpoint configuration does not currently provide a way to authenticate with a Bearer token in the same flexible way as the built-in Azure provider.

A key requirement is support for dynamic generation of a Bearer token and sending it with each request, similar to how the Azure provider handles authentication today.

**Requested enhancement**

Please add support for:
- Bearer token authentication for custom language model endpoints
- Dynamic token acquisition or generation
- Automatic inclusion of the token in the request headers, such as Authorization: Bearer 

This would enable authentication against OpenAI-compatible models exposed through an organization-managed AI gateway.

**Why this matters**

In enterprise environments, authentication is often handled centrally rather than through static API keys. A dynamic Bearer token flow is important because it allows:
- Integration with organizational identity and access systems
- Short-lived or refreshed tokens for improved security
- Support for gateways that broker access to multiple model providers
- A consistent experience with the Azure provider authentication model

**Expected behavior**

The custom endpoint configuration should support a mechanism where:
- A Bearer token can be generated dynamically at runtime, and
- That token is attached to outgoing requests automatically

This would make the feature compatible with enterprise AI gateways and other OpenAI-compatible services that rely on token-based authentication.

**Business value**
- Enables secure enterprise adoption
- Avoids dependence on static keys
- Aligns custom endpoint behavior with the Azure provider experience
- Expands support for organization-managed AI gateways and OpenAI-compatible endpoints

Git: getRepositoryExact loops infinitely with ENOENT error after workspace folder rename

- VS Code Version: 1.125.1 (user setup)
- Commit: fcf604774b9f2674b473065736ee75077e256353
- Date: 2026-06-19T08:39:34Z
- Electron: 42.2.0

- Chromium: 148.0.7778.97
- Node.js: 24.15.0
- V8: 14.8.178.14-electron.0
- OS Version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

1. Open a project located in a directory (e.g., `e:\aaa\bbb`) that is initialized as a Git repository.
2. Rename the project's root folder using Windows File Explorer.
3. Open the newly renamed folder in VS Code and delete its `.git` folder to stop version control tracking.
4. The built-in Git extension crashes into an infinite, high-frequency loop running `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`.
5. The Git output channel constantly spams thousands of lines saying `fatal: not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git`.
6. Immediately following, the extension model spams warnings: `[warning] [Model][getRepositoryExact] Failed to get repository realpath for: ... Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, realpath`.
7. Even if you reset workspace profiles, use `Developer: Reset State`, reload windows, close the folder completely, or temporarily rename the folder back to its original name, the internal path state remains desynchronized. If renamed back, it instead loops complaining that the "new" name cannot be found via `ENOENT`.
