title: Preserve user agency when cleaning up completed agent terminals
document:
Preserve user agency when cleaning up completed agent terminals

# Preserve user agency and auditability when cleaning up completed agent terminals

**Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: YES
I ran SHIFT + CTRL + P and did "disable all installed extensions" and still saw copilot working with the terminal.

- VS Code Version: 1.126.0-insider
- OS Version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.26200

---

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Use Copilot Chat / agent mode to run one or more background terminal commands.
2. Let a background command complete while the terminal remains hidden.
3. Try to inspect, reveal, preserve, group, export, or otherwise audit that terminal execution after completion.
4. Compare the available post-completion affordances against what a user would need if the agent made a mistake, missed an error, or ran a destructive command that exited successfully.

---

## Actual Behavior

Completed hidden background terminals may be disposed/cleaned up automatically, leaving only [mirrored or snapshot-style output available in the chat UI](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/323016).

This creates a user-agency and auditability problem. Chat agents do not always correctly understand or surface errors that happened in terminals. They may miss command failures, misinterpret output, overlook warnings, or continue as though an operation succeeded when the terminal output shows otherwise.

A terminal created by an agent is not merely a live process/PTY resource. It is also an execution record. It is evidence of what the agent actually did.

The current behavior appears to treat "hidden + completed" as a reasonable proxy for "safe to dispose." That is not safe enough.

A command can exit successfully and still be the exact execution the user later needs to inspect. For example:

- A destructive command may delete or move files and still exit with code 0.
- A script may mutate a repository, database, generated artifacts, permissions, ownership, branches, tags, or remotes.
- An install/build/migration command may complete while producing warnings or unintended changes.
- The agent may fail to notice that the command output contains an important error or warning.
- The user may only realize after the chat response that they need to inspect the terminal.

In these cases, the fact that the terminal was hidden at completion does not mean the user no longer needs it. The user should not lose post-hoc access to the execution artifact simply because they did not reveal the terminal before it finished.

---

## Expected Behavior

VS Code should separate terminal resource cleanup from terminal artifact retention.

It is reasonable to clean up live terminal/PTY/process resources so completed background terminals do not accumulate indefinitely. Or present a background compromise vector. However, cleanup should **not** remove the user's ability to inspect, preserve, reveal, group, export, or **reason** about the execution afterward.

At minimum, completed agent terminal executions should remain available as first-class execution-history artifacts containing:

- Command executed
- Working directory
- Shell/session identity
- Exit code / signal / cancellation state
- Timestamps and duration
- Full output
- Whether the terminal was hidden, revealed, failed, cancelled, timed out, or disposed
- Relevant tool metadata
- Association with the chat turn/session that created it

There should also be user-facing affordances such as:

- Reveal related terminal/execution
- Show only terminals/executions related to the current chat
- Group related terminals by chat/session/turn
- Clear terminal history for the current chat, explicitly deactivating associated shells/resources
- Persist terminals for this chat/session
- Export/download/view chat history and terminal execution history together
- Pin important terminal executions
- Rerun or recreate a completed execution where practical

Terminal reuse should also be considered as a preferable alternative to immediate destruction in cases where reuse is safe and understandable. If the agent already sometimes reuses terminals, the lifecycle should be explicit and inspectable rather than hidden behind automatic disposal. Reuse has risks because shell state can persist, but those risks argue for clearer session management, not for silently removing evidence.

This is especially important for failed, cancelled, timed-out, nonzero-exit, suspicious, destructive, or high-impact commands. Those executions should be retained more aggressively, not cleaned up less visibly.

---

## The Provenance Graph Problem

Agent terminal executions are not isolated UI details. They are part of the causal history of the chat session. The product already has many of these edges at runtime:

This is naturally a DAG. A user should be able to inspect the chain of events that led to a result:

- Which chat turn initiated the action
- Which model/tool decision created the terminal command
- Which terminal/session/shell executed it
- What command ran
- What working directory and relevant environment were used
- What output, warnings, and errors occurred
- What exit status resulted
- What files, repository state, generated artifacts, or external state may have changed
- What later agent claims depended on that execution

**Removing or collapsing the terminal artifact breaks this graph exactly where users most need traceability.**

For AI agent workflows, the safest default should be evidence preservation, not evidence disappearance. If storage or resource use is a concern, live shells can be deactivated, old records can be compacted, and retention policies can be explicit. But the execution record should not silently vanish merely because the terminal was hidden when the command completed.

A better design would provide a chat/session execution history view that lets users trace from an agent response back to the commands and terminal sessions that produced it. This could include:

- Grouping by chat turn
- Showing related terminals only
- Revealing terminal history
- Exporting chat plus terminal history together
- Preserving tombstones for disposed/deactivated shells

The goal should be simple: **when an agent does something, the user should be able to see what led to it. Agent actions should be more inspectable than ordinary user actions, not less.**

---

## On User Agency and Consent

The implicit design assumption underlying the current behavior appears to be that **users who did not reveal a terminal before it completed did not need it.**

This conflates *unawareness* with *consent*.

A user who did not know to reveal a terminal is not a user who chose not to preserve it. They are a user who did not yet know they would need it — which is precisely the situation that post-hoc inspection exists to address.

Removing access to execution artifacts from users who did not know they needed them is not a neutral UX decision. It is a decision about whose judgment prevails when the agent's behavior and the user's interests diverge. The answer should not be "the agent's" — and it should especially not be implemented as a silent default that requires users to have pre-revealed terminals before they knew they would need them.

An escape hatch that requires the user to act before they know they need it is not an escape hatch. It is an illusion of agency.

---

## Related Context

- [[#287177](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/287177)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/287177) — appears to describe the legitimate resource accumulation problem
- [[#316278](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/316278)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/316278) — implemented disposal of completed hidden background terminals
- [[#302109](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/302109)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/302109) — user frustration when agent terminal output becomes unavailable or difficult to inspect
- [[#316492](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/316492)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/316492) — appears to test/validate the new ephemeral behavior
- [[#321049](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/321049)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/321049) — session-switch disposal of agent terminals (related, separate mechanism)
- [[#322121](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/322121)](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/322121) — partial fix: protects user-revealed terminals on session switch; hidden terminals remain subject to disposal

---

## Summary

The request here is not "never clean up background terminals."

The request is:

> **Clean up live resources without taking away the user's ability to inspect and preserve evidence of what the agent did.**

- Hidden is not abandoned.
- Completed is not worthless.
- A clean exit is not proof that the command was harmless.
- [A snapshot is not necessarily product-equivalent to an inspectable terminal execution artifact.](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/323016)
- Removing the terminal breaks the provenance graph.

That last point is the core of this issue. You are not deleting a UI object. You are deleting a causal link in the user's ability to understand what their agent actually did.

title: Agent sessions favor creating new terminals instead of reusing existing terminals
document:
Agent sessions favor creating new terminals instead of reusing existing terminals

Title: 

VS Code: 1.126.0-insider

1. Open a VS Code workspace with one or more existing integrated terminals already running.
2. Start a Copilot/Agent session and ask it to perform work that requires terminal commands.
3. Observe whether the agent reuses an existing suitable terminal or creates a new terminal.
4. Repeat across multiple requests/sessions.

Actual Behavior:

When I ask agent sessions to do work, they mostly do not reuse existing terminals. Instead, they often create new terminals even when an appropriate existing terminal is already available.

Expected Behavior:

The agent should prefer reusing an existing suitable terminal when one is already available, especially when that terminal is already in the correct workspace, shell, environment, or remote session.

Why this matters:

This causes terminal clutter and can break workflow continuity. In some cases, the existing terminal has important context such as the correct working directory, SSH/session state, environment variables, Docker context, or previously prepared commands. Creating a new terminal instead of reusing the existing one makes agent-driven work less predictable.

Frequency:

This happens often / most of the time in my workflow.

Additional Notes:

This is especially noticeable during agentic coding sessions where I repeatedly ask the assistant to run commands, validate work, inspect output, or continue from prior terminal state.

In the "terminal tabs" the terminal with a double icon was the only one that was reused.

title: Stepping sometimes causes focus to pass to another thread
document:
Stepping sometimes causes focus to pass to another thread

When I step in a multi-target debugging scenario, the source code and call stack should remain focused on the debug session/thread that I am stepping in. I am noticing that sometimes that is true, and sometimes the focus swaps to another debug session/thread that is stopped.

For context, I am maintaining a custom debug adapter that performs remote debugging, so stepping can often take longer than 800ms. The behavior that I expect seems to conflict with the behavior that was introduced in these issues/commits and their followups:
- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/commit/30d73ea6dfafaa65c6aef8194f988475f105336c
- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/125144#issuecomment-869641057

In order to keep both of these behaviors, here are some ideas:
- Create a user setting to choose whether focus should be automatically passed to another thread or not when the current one starts running
- Create a user setting to adjust the timeout for the automatic focus passing
- Add a new DAP property for StoppedEvent or use the existing "preserveFocusHint" to change focus back to the stopped thread if the focus has been passed away

Let me know if you want me to recreate this with the Mock Debug Adapter.

title: Terminal prompt for SSH passphrase auto-submits input when terminal is focused after prompt appears
document:
Terminal prompt for SSH passphrase auto-submits input when terminal is focused after prompt appears

Quando uma ação do agente/Copilot dispara um comando Git que pede passphrase SSH, se eu abrir/focar o terminal somente depois que o prompt já apareceu, a entrada é enviada automaticamente antes de eu terminar de digitar.
Se eu deixo o terminal aberto/focado antes do prompt aparecer, a digitação funciona normalmente.

Passos para reproduzir

Iniciar uma ação via agente/Copilot que execute git commit e peça passphrase SSH.
Não deixar o terminal aberto/focado antes do prompt.
Quando aparecer Enter passphrase for "...id_ed25519", abrir/focar o terminal manualmente.
Tentar digitar a passphrase.
Comportamento atual
A entrada é auto-enviada cedo demais (não consigo digitar a passphrase completa normalmente), causando falha de autenticação em algumas tentativas.

Comportamento esperado
O terminal deve aguardar a digitação completa da passphrase e só enviar quando eu confirmar manualmente com Enter.

Condição comparativa importante

Falha: terminal aberto/focado depois do prompt já visível.
Funciona: terminal aberto/focado antes do prompt aparecer.
Frequência
Intermitente/recorrente, principalmente em fluxos com agente executando git commit assinado por chave SSH.

Impacto
Interrompe fluxo de commits no agente e exige repetição manual do comando.

Ambiente

VS Code: preencher versão
Extensão GitHub Copilot Chat: preencher versão
Shell: PowerShell integrado
Chave SSH com passphrase: id_ed25519
Workaround atual
Abrir/focar o terminal antes do comando chegar ao prompt de passphrase.

title: setModelConfiguration write per-model settings to the wrong provider group when multiple groups share the same vendor
document:
setModelConfiguration write per-model settings to the wrong provider group when multiple groups share the same vendor

- VS Code Version: 1.125.1 (Universal)
- OS Version: macos15 latest

in `setModelConfiguration` :

https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/cb128cbeffb5e3fda13c3fc6fa67e766eac408e6/src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/common/languageModels.ts#L1211-L1217

## Bug Description

**Title:** `setModelConfiguration` writes per-model config to the wrong provider group when multiple groups share the same vendor

**Location:** `setModelConfiguration()` in languageModels.ts

**Root Cause:**

When a model has not yet been configured in any group (i.e., `group.settings` does not contain an entry for that model's `metadata.id`), the fallback logic picks the group to write to solely by vendor name:

This picks the **first** group in the list that matches the vendor — regardless of which group the model actually belongs to.

Additionally — the first-step lookup has the same flaw (and runs before the fallback above):

The settings key metadata.id is not unique across groups of the same vendor (two endpoints can serve the same model id). So when more than one same-vendor group already has an entry under that id, .find() returns the first match — again regardless of which group the model actually belongs to — and the fallback is never reached. Both steps resolve the group by vendor (+ model id) instead of consulting the authoritative model→group mapping the service already maintains (_modelsGroups, where each entry carries modelIdentifiers + its owning group).

**Concrete Scenario:**

A user's `chatLanguageModels.json` has two provider groups for the same vendor (e.g., two separate API endpoints from the same third-party LM provider):

When the user sets a per-model configuration (e.g., context size) for a model belonging to the second group ("endpoint-b") via the model picker UI, `setModelConfiguration` is called. Since the second group has no prior settings for that model, the first-step lookup (`g.settings?.[metadata.id]`) fails. The fallback finds the first vendor-matching group ("endpoint-a") and writes the config there instead of to "endpoint-b".

**Impact:** Per-model settings land in the wrong group entry in `chatLanguageModels.json`, which can cause confusion, lost settings on reload, and cross-contamination between logically separate provider instances.

## Copilot Completion Feedback
### Describe the issue, feedback, or steps to reproduce it:

### Completion text:

title: Test Plan: Run sandboxed before requesting terminal access elevation
document:
Test Plan: Run sandboxed before requesting terminal access elevation

Refs: #321647

- [ ] macOS @TylerLeonhardt 
- [ ] Linux @rzhao271 

Complexity: 2

Author: @dileepyavan

---

**Context:** When an agent requests to run a terminal command outside the sandbox or with unrestricted network access, the user currently has to approve that elevated access before the command runs.

**What Changed:** `chat.agent.sandbox.forceFirstExecutionInSandbox` is a preview setting, disabled by default. When enabled, a foreground `run_in_terminal` request that asks for elevated access first runs with the existing sandbox restrictions. The user is prompted to approve the requested elevated retry only after that sandboxed command exits with a nonzero exit code.

## Test Cases

For all test cases, use Agent mode with a functional terminal sandbox and configure:

### 1. Outside-sandbox request is deferred until the sandboxed run fails
- Ask the agent to use the terminal tool to write a uniquely named file under the user home directory (outside the workspace), and to request execution outside the sandbox if required.
- Verify that the command initially runs in the sandbox without showing an outside-sandbox confirmation.
- Verify that the sandboxed command fails because the target location is not writable from the sandbox.
- Verify that an **Allow** confirmation for the outside-sandbox retry appears only after that failed run. Approve it.
- Verify that the command reruns outside the sandbox and the file is created with the requested contents.

### 2. Unrestricted-network request is deferred until restricted networking fails
- Ask the agent to fetch `https://example.com` with the terminal tool and to request unrestricted network access while remaining sandboxed.
- Verify that the initial command runs with restricted sandbox networking, without an unrestricted-network confirmation.
- Verify that the initial request fails because network access is restricted.
- Verify that an **Allow** confirmation for the unrestricted-network retry appears only after that failure. Approve it.
- Verify that the command reruns successfully with network access while remaining sandboxed.

### 3. Default behavior remains unchanged when disabled
- Set `chat.agent.sandbox.forceFirstExecutionInSandbox` to `false`.
- Repeat either elevated-access scenario above.
- Verify that the elevated-access confirmation appears before any command is executed, rather than after a failed sandboxed attempt.

title: Copilot CLI agent (Agents window) gets stuck reading files via GitHub MCP instead of the local worktree
document:
Copilot CLI agent (Agents window) gets stuck reading files via GitHub MCP instead of the local worktree

## Description

When using the **Copilot CLI agent inside the Agents window** (with a local git worktree open), the agent frequently gets stuck. Instead of reading files directly from my **local filesystem**, it repeatedly falls back to **GitHub commands/tools** (e.g. `get_file_contents`, code search against the remote repo) to read source files that already exist locally.

This causes the agent to:
- Loop for a long time fetching files remotely that are sitting right there on disk
- Burn through premium/usage cost ("dollars") without making progress
- Effectively stall for long stretches before producing any useful output

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open VS Code with a local git **worktree** of a repository.
2. Open the **Agents** window and start a **Copilot CLI** agent session.
3. Ask the agent to perform a task that requires reading several local files.
4. Observe the agent choosing GitHub remote file-reading tools instead of local file reads.

## Expected behavior

- The agent should **prefer local file reads** (the open worktree on disk) over GitHub remote fetches whenever the files are available locally.
- Remote GitHub tools should only be used when the content genuinely is not available locally.
- The agent should not get stuck in long loops re-fetching files remotely.

## Actual behavior

- The agent uses GitHub commands to read files that already exist in the local worktree.
- It gets stuck "analysing for hours," consuming usage/cost with no progress.

## Impact

- Wasted time (sessions stall for long periods).
- Wasted money — usage is consumed without delivering results.

## Environment

- Surface: VS Code **Agents** window, **Copilot CLI** agent
- Working directory: local git **worktree**

## Additional notes

A clear local-first file-access policy (or stronger tool-selection guidance that deprioritizes remote GitHub reads when a local workspace/worktree is present) would likely resolve this.

title: Voice mode: holding push-to-talk keybinding rapidly toggles instead of hold-to-talk
document:
Voice mode: holding push-to-talk keybinding rapidly toggles instead of hold-to-talk

### Describe the bug

In voice mode, holding down the push-to-talk keybinding (`Ctrl+Shift+Space`) in the chat input behaves erratically instead of acting as push-to-talk.

**Expected:** Pressing and holding the keybinding should enter listening mode, and releasing the key should stop recording and send the request.

**Actual:** Holding the key rapidly toggles recording on and off ("weird things").

### Root cause

The chat-input voice actions (`agentsVoice.startVoiceInChat` and `agentsVoice.pttStopInChat`) called `voiceController.pttDown()` and `voiceController.pttUp()` synchronously, back-to-back:

Because `pttUp()` fires within ~0ms — below the controller's tap threshold (`_PTT_TOGGLE_THRESHOLD_MS = 300`) — the controller always interprets it as a *tap* and enters toggle mode, never true hold-to-talk. Worse, while the key is held, the OS emits keyboard auto-repeat `keydown` events, which re-dispatch the command repeatedly. Each repeat flips between `pttDown` (start listening), toggle, and finish/send, producing rapid start/stop cycling.

### Fix

Use `IKeybindingService.enableKeybindingHoldMode(commandId)` to implement real push-to-talk:

- On key press, call `pttDown()` (enter listening).
- The keybinding service swallows auto-repeat `keydown` events while hold mode is active, so the command is not re-dispatched mid-hold.
- When the key is released, the hold-mode promise resolves and we call `pttUp()` (stop + send).
- When invoked without a held key (toolbar button / command palette), `enableKeybindingHoldMode` returns `undefined` and we fall back to a single tap (toggle), preserving existing click behavior.

This mirrors the existing pattern in `voiceChatActions.ts` (`startVoiceChatWithHoldMode`).

### Area

`src/vs/workbench/contrib/agentsVoice/browser/agentsVoice.contribution.ts`

The approval pop-over is dismissed when the mouse leaves the session row, making it impossible or very difficult to click `Allow` or `Skip`.

Expected Result:

The pop-over should remain open while the pointer moves from the session row into the pop-over, so the user can click the approval controls. The pop-over should behave as hoverable/interactive content, not disappear before interaction.

Additional Notes:

This occurs in VS Code Insiders. Screenshot attached showing the pop-over with the `Allow` and `Skip` controls disappearing when attempting to move toward it.

title: Allow a custom/arbitrary sort order for files in the Source Control 'Changes' list
document:
Allow a custom/arbitrary sort order for files in the Source Control 'Changes' list

## Problem

The Source Control **Changes** list can only be sorted by **Name**, **Path**, or **Status** (`scm.defaultViewSortKey`). There is no way to give the files in a resource group a **custom / arbitrary order**.

Internally, `SCMTreeSorter` always sorts resources within a group by one of those three keys and discards the extension-provided `resourceStates` array order. Two resource *groups* preserve their creation order (the comparator returns `0` when comparing two groups), but resources *within* a group are always force-sorted — there is no opt-out, and no way for an extension or the built-in Git provider to supply a custom comparator or explicit order.

## What I want

I want the files in the Changes list to be displayed in a **custom order that I (or a tool) define** — not alphabetical, not by path, not by status — so the most important / highest-consequence changes appear at the top and I review them first.

Concrete motivating use case: I have an AI assistant rank a diff from **most → least consequential** (e.g. security rules and core data-layer changes first; tests, config, and docs last). I want that ranking to drive the order of the built-in Changes list so my review reads top-to-bottom in importance order. Today this only works in the terminal via `git diff -O` (`diff.orderFile`); the GUI Source Control view ignores it entirely.

## Proposed solutions (any one of these would solve it)

1. **Extension API** — allow a `SourceControl` / `SourceControlResourceGroup` to provide a custom sort comparator, or an explicit ordering (e.g. a priority index per `SourceControlResourceState`), that the SCM view honors instead of `SCMTreeSorter`'s fixed keys. This is the most powerful option: it lets tooling (AI review assistants, linters, risk scanners) drive the order programmatically.

2. **New "Custom" sort mode** — add `scm.defaultViewSortKey: "custom"` (or `"manual"`) that either:
 - preserves the provider's `resourceStates` array order, and/or
 - supports drag-to-reorder in the view, persisted in workspace storage.

3. **Honor `diff.orderFile` in the built-in Git provider** — when git's `diff.orderFile` is configured, order the built-in Git **Changes** / **Staged Changes** lists to match, giving GUI/CLI parity. Simplest option; reuses an existing, well-understood Git mechanism.

Option 1 is the most powerful (lets tooling drive the order); option 3 is the simplest.

## Current behavior (verified on latest `main`)

- `scm.defaultViewSortKey` enum is still only `name | path | status` (default `path`) — there is no custom/manual mode.
- `SCMTreeSorter.compare()` force-sorts resources within a group; it returns `0` for two groups, so groups keep their creation order but resources within a group never do.
- The `resourceStates` array order provided by an extension is not used for display.

## Prior art / why this isn't already covered

- **#141319** (✅ completed) — added the sort-*mode* setting (`name`/`path`/`status`) + workspace persistence. This is mode *switching*, not custom ordering.
- **#127952** (✅ completed) — made the chosen sort mode persist across restarts.
- **#146507** (✅ completed) — repository ordering in a multi-root workspace (a different concern: ordering repos, not files within a group).
- **#235493** (❌ closed as not planned) — the closest prior "add a sort order option" request. This issue differs by asking for an **arbitrary/custom order** (ideally programmable via an extension API), not another fixed preset mode.

None of the completed work provides a way to define an arbitrary per-file order in the Changes list.

title: Integrated Browser shows blank page instead of HTTP Basic Auth prompt for localhost web apps
document:
Integrated Browser shows blank page instead of HTTP Basic Auth prompt for localhost web apps

* VS Code Version: 1.125.1
* OS Version: Windows 11

1. Start a local web app protected with HTTP Basic Authentication.
 Example: OpenCode web/server running on `http://127.0.0.1:4096` with password authentication enabled.

2. Open the same URL in a normal browser such as Chrome or Edge.

3. The browser correctly shows the HTTP Basic Auth username/password prompt and the app loads normally after entering the credentials.

4. Open the same URL inside VS Code using Integrated Browser or Simple Browser.

5. Instead of showing the HTTP Basic Auth prompt, the browser view renders a completely blank white page.

Expected behavior:

VS Code’s Integrated Browser / Simple Browser should display the HTTP Basic Auth prompt, or provide another way to enter the username and password.

Actual behavior:

The page stays blank white. No authentication prompt or input is shown, so the local web app cannot be used inside VS Code.

Additional information:

The issue does not happen in external browsers. It only happens inside VS Code’s browser view.

This makes VS Code’s internal browser unusable for localhost developer tools that are protected with HTTP Basic Authentication.

A workaround is to run a local proxy that injects the `Authorization` header before forwarding the request to the protected app, but this should not be necessary.

title: Copilot Chat auto-switches to 1M context after VS Code restart, ignoring selected model setting
document:
Copilot Chat auto-switches to 1M context after VS Code restart, ignoring selected model setting

* Copilot Chat Extension Version: latest
* VS Code Version: latest
* OS Version: latest
* Feature: Copilot Chat / existing chat session after VS Code restart
* Selected model: GPT 5.5 High 272k

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Set Copilot Chat to use GPT 5.5 High with 272k context.
2. Start or continue a Copilot Chat session.
3. Restart VS Code.
4. Continue the existing Copilot Chat session.
5. Check the model/context shown in the Copilot UI.

## Expected Behavior

Copilot should respect the currently selected model/context setting after VS Code restart.

If I selected GPT 5.5 High 272k, continuing an existing session should keep using GPT 5.5 High 272k, unless I explicitly choose another model/context.

## Actual Behavior

After restarting VS Code and continuing an existing session, Copilot appears to ignore the selected setting and silently switches to the 1M context option.

This is especially serious because 1M is much more expensive. The switch is not explicitly confirmed by the user, but the user is still charged for the more expensive option.

## Impact

This caused unexpected billing charges for me. In one case, I paid around $10 because Copilot silently switched to the 1M option.

This makes Copilot unsafe to use with paid models/context tiers, because the UI setting is not reliably respected across VS Code restarts.

## Request

Please investigate why continuing an existing session after VS Code restart can override the user-selected model/context setting.

At minimum, Copilot should never silently switch to a more expensive model/context tier without explicit user confirmation.

VS Code shows GitHub account signed in, but Backup/Settings Sync still says “Please sign in” and does not restore extensions

1. Open Visual Studio Code.
2. Sign in with GitHub from the Accounts menu.
3. Complete the GitHub browser authentication flow.
4. Return to VS Code.

6. VS Code still shows: “Please sign in to backup and sync your data across devices.”

Run Button Missing for Source Files After Update and Execution Workflow Fails

### VS Code Version

Latest Stable Release (issue started after updating)

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open VS Code.
2. Open a workspace containing C++ source files.
3. Ensure the Microsoft C/C++ extension is installed and enabled.
4. Open a `.cpp` file (e.g. `DSA_Array_02.cpp`).

6. Open `.vscode/launch.json`.

title: Copilot Chat BYOK /responses appears to resend large local history after compaction
document:
Copilot Chat BYOK /responses appears to resend large local history after compaction

When using Copilot Chat with a BYOK model on a Custom Endpoint that exposes an OpenAI-compatible /responses API, compaction appears to occur, but the next user turn still sends a large serialized local history payload instead of a compact continuation.

In contrast, I tested the same general workflow with chat-completions and the next-turn request shrank as expected after compaction.

The strongest symptom on the failing path is:
* the next request succeeds
* the next request includes a valid previous_response_id
* but the next request still contains a large serialized local history input

I have only reproduced this on the Custom Endpoint path so far. My Custom Endpoint is fronted by Bifrost or LiteLLM, but the oversized payload is already present in the request before any upstream rewriting, so this does not appear to be caused by the gateway.

# Environment
VS Code: Version: 1.125.1 (user setup)

ElectronBuildId: 14159160

Failing path: BYOK Custom Endpoint using an OpenAI-compatible /responses API
Working comparison: BYOK chat-completions

# Observed Comparison
I tested two BYOK paths:

1. Custom Endpoint BYOK
* uses an OpenAI-compatible /responses endpoint
* in my setup this is fronted by Bifrost (custom to forward compaction/context_management params) or LiteLLM (custom to enable Azure compaction support)
* after compaction, the next request still carried a large serialized local history payload

2. BYOK with chat-completions
* after compaction from Custom Endpoint BYOK, the next request with Azure BYOK shrank as expected

So the behavior difference I am reporting is:
* Custom Endpoint BYOK /responses: next-turn request remains unexpectedly large
* BYOK chat-completions : next-turn request becomes compact as expected

I have not proven from source why the two paths differ, only that this difference is observable at runtime.

# Steps to Reproduce
1. Configure a BYOK Custom Endpoint that uses the Responses API (/responses).
2. Start a conversation long enough to trigger compaction / summarization.
3. Send the next user message in the same conversation.
4. Inspect the outgoing request body for that next turn.
5. Observe that:
* previous_response_id is present
* the request succeeds
* but the request still contains a large serialized local history payload

For comparison, repeat the same general workflow with BYOK chat-completions and observe that the next-turn request becomes smaller after compaction.

# Expected Behavior
After compaction, when a valid previous_response_id is available, the next request should send only the minimal continuation history required by the Responses API, plus any necessary compaction round-trip item.

That is the behavior I observed with chat-completions BYOK, and what I expected from the /responses BYOK path as well.

# Actual Behavior
On Custom Endpoint /responses BYOK, the next request includes previous_response_id, but also re-sends a large local serialized history blob.

# Why I think this is client-side
I captured the failing request at my upstream gateway. The large serialized local history was already present before the request was forwarded upstream. That rules out the gateway as the origin of the oversized payload.

I also reviewed the relevant VS Code source and found logic that appears intended to shrink history once continuation state is available:

That suggests the next request should shrink when continuation state is available.

# Sanitized Evidence
I am not attaching raw logs because they contain private conversation contents, local paths, shell history, endpoint/provider metadata, and request identifiers.

The relevant redacted shape of the failing Custom Endpoint request is:

The important part is:
* request succeeded
* previous_response_id was present
* a large serialized local history payload was still sent

# Related Issues
I found some related issues, but none that seem identical:
* #316747 BYOK Responses retry for previous_response_not_found re-sends previous_response_id
* #316576 customendpoint provider sends invalid item type in Responses input
* #318125 Responses API requests should include explicit message item types
* #321200 foreground compaction stores raw summary output incorrectly

This report seems different because the request here is successful and already carries previous_response_id, but the post-compaction next-turn payload still appears much larger than expected on the Custom Endpoint path.

title: SIGPIPE error in Remote SSH Extension Host on client disconnect
document:
SIGPIPE error in Remote SSH Extension Host on client disconnect

## Description

The VS Code Remote SSH server logs an `Unexpected SIGPIPE` error when a client disconnects gracefully. The error occurs at the moment the `ManagementConnection` reports a graceful disconnect, and the Extension Host Process exits immediately after.

## Log excerpt

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to a remote host via Remote-SSH.
2. Work in the remote session for a few minutes.
3. Disconnect/close the VS Code window (graceful disconnect).
4. Check the remote server log at `~/.vscode-server/data/logs//remoteagent.log`.

## Expected behavior

Graceful client disconnect should not produce an error. The SIGPIPE should be handled silently (or the write that causes it should be guarded).

## Actual behavior

An `Unexpected SIGPIPE` error is logged every time the client disconnects, even though the disconnect is graceful and the extension host exits cleanly (code 0).

## Environment

- VS Code: 1.123.0 (Stable, commit 6a44c352bd24569c417e530095901b649960f9f8)
- Remote SSH server: Linux x64
- Node.js: (bundled with VS Code server)
- Platform: Linux (RHEL 8.10)

## Analysis

The SIGPIPE likely occurs because the server attempts to write to the management connection's IPC channel after the client has already closed its end. On Linux, writing to a closed pipe/socket raises SIGPIPE. The error is caught by a generic `process` error handler that logs it as `Unexpected SIGPIPE`, but since the disconnect is graceful, this should be suppressed or handled explicitly.

The error is non-fatal (extension host exits cleanly with code 0), but it pollutes the logs on every disconnect and can be alarming to users reviewing server logs.

## Suggested fix

Ignore SIGPIPE during connection teardown, or check if the connection is still writable before writing. A `process.on('SIGPIPE', () => {})` handler or using `Stream#write` with error callbacks would prevent the uncaught error.

title: Allow customizing Git Blame date formatting
document:
Allow customizing Git Blame date formatting

The built-in Git Blame editor decoration allows customizing the template, but not the formatting of date values.

For example:

`{
 "git.blame.editorDecoration.template": "${authorName} (${authorDate})"
}`

The value of ${authorDate} is generated using the runtime locale and formatting, which cannot be customized.

Depending on the selected locale, this may produce undesirable output (for example, localized relative time strings or date formats that don't match the user's preference).

### Proposed solution

Allow configuring the formatting of date variables independently from the template.

### Some possible approaches:

Add settings such as:

git.blame.dateFormat
git.blame.relativeDateStyle
Support formatting options in template variables, for example:
${authorDate:yyyy-MM-dd}
${authorDate:short}
${authorDateAgo:short}

### Why

Many developers prefer a consistent date format regardless of the UI locale, or would like to choose between different relative date styles without changing the entire application locale.

This would make the built-in Git Blame decoration significantly more flexible while keeping the existing template mechanism.

title: Git: `isRepositoryHuge` flag set during transient clone/checkout permanently freezes stale git status with no recovery short of restart
document:
Git: `isRepositoryHuge` flag set during transient clone/checkout permanently freezes stale git status with no recovery short of restart

## Summary

When a git repository is re-cloned (or undergoes a large checkout) while a VS Code window is open on that workspace, VS Code's built-in git extension captures a **transient, momentary state** where all files appear as deleted (`D`). The `git.statusLimit` guard then permanently sets `isRepositoryHuge = true`, which **freezes all subsequent `git status` refreshes** — including manual "Refresh" from the Source Control panel — until VS Code is restarted.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a large git repository (e.g., 10,000+ tracked files) in VS Code
2. From the integrated terminal, delete the repo directory and re-clone it (e.g., `rm -rf . && git clone .`)
3. Wait for the clone to complete
4. Observe the Source Control panel — many files show as `D` (deleted) even though they exist on disk
5. Click the "Refresh" button in Source Control — the stale `D` decorations persist
6. Only restarting VS Code (or reloading the window) clears the stale state

## Expected Behavior

After the clone completes and files are present on disk, VS Code should re-run `git status` and clear the stale `D` decorations — either automatically (once the transient state resolves) or at minimum when the user manually clicks "Refresh" in the Source Control panel.

## Actual Behavior

- During the clone, the file watcher fires and triggers `git status -z`
- At that instant, all tracked files are missing from the working tree → git reports them all as deleted
- `git.statusLimit` (default: 10,000) is hit, so only the first 10,000 entries are captured (early-alphabetical files like `.config`, `Jenkinsfile`, `README`, etc.)
- The `didHitLimit` flag sets `isRepositoryHuge = true`
- From that point on, **all** file-watch events and manual refresh attempts are short-circuited by the huge-repository guard
- Stale `D` state is frozen until the extension host restarts

## Evidence from logs

The VS Code remote server log shows the file watcher shutting down during the re-clone:

## Root Cause

The `isRepositoryHuge` flag was designed to protect against repositories that *persistently* have an enormous number of changes (e.g., Linux kernel). However, it gets permanently set from a **transient spike** during clone/checkout — when the working tree is momentarily empty. Once set, there is **no recovery path**:

- ❌ Manual "Refresh" in Source Control → ignored (guard short-circuits)
- ❌ Subsequent file events → ignored
- ❌ Only a full window reload / extension host restart clears it

## Suggested Fixes

Any of the following would resolve the issue:

1. **Re-evaluate `isRepositoryHuge`**: On the next `git status` call, if the result count is below the limit, reset `isRepositoryHuge = false` so automatic refresh resumes.
2. **Allow manual "Refresh" to bypass the guard**: The user explicitly clicking "Refresh" should always trigger a fresh `git status`, regardless of the huge-repo flag.
3. **Detect transient clone state**: If `.git/index.lock` exists or `.git/` is rapidly changing, suppress `git status` until the operation settles — rather than capturing a snapshot of an incomplete working tree.
4. **Increase default `git.statusLimit`** or make the limit configurable per-repository.

## Environment

- VS Code: 1.123.0 (commit 6a44c352bd24569c417e530095901b649960f9f8)
- OS: RHEL 8.10 (Remote - SSH)
- Git: 2.x (shallow clone, `--depth 1`)
- Repository size: ~15 GB, ~74,000 tracked files, 150+ submodules

## Workaround

Restart VS Code or reload the window (`Developer: Reload Window`). Alternatively, increase `git.statusLimit` in settings:

Or close VS Code before performing large re-clones and reopen after completion.

title: Add Confirmation Dialog for "Close All Editors" Command
document:
Add Confirmation Dialog for "Close All Editors" Command

## Feature Request

### Problem

I occasionally trigger the "Close All Editors" command (Ctrl+K, W) by mistake. When this happens, all open editor tabs are closed immediately without any confirmation prompt. While tabs can often be restored, it can be disruptive, especially when working with many open files.

### Proposed Solution

Add an optional setting that displays a confirmation dialog before executing the "Close All Editors" command.

Example:

"Are you sure you want to close all open editors?"

* Close All
* Cancel

### Suggested Setting

The setting could be disabled by default to preserve the current behavior and enabled by users who prefer additional protection against accidental tab closure.

### Benefits

* Prevents accidental closure of all editor tabs.
* Improves usability for users who frequently use Ctrl+K shortcuts.
* Provides consistency with other confirmation dialogs available in VS Code.
* Optional setting ensures no impact on users who prefer the current workflow.

### Additional Context

This is particularly useful when working with many open files across multiple editor groups, where accidentally triggering "Close All Editors" can interrupt the current workflow.

title: Test: In-editor Copilot PMF survey
document:
Test: In-editor Copilot PMF survey

Refs: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog/issues/8095

- [x] anyOS @ulugbekna
- [x] anyOS @dmitrivMS

Complexity: 3

Authors: @digitarald

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23322330%0A%0A&assignees=digitarald)

---

## Context

Copilot PMF surveys now open as a native VS Code editor pane instead of sending users to an external survey URL. The first migrated survey requires only the score question and submits any optional fixed-choice answers through structured telemetry.

## Test Steps

1. Open a Code OSS development build.
2. Run `Developer: Open Survey` from the Command Palette.
 - Verify a survey opens in an editor tab.
 - Verify no external browser window opens.
3. Try to submit the survey before answering the first score question.
 - Verify submission is blocked.
4. Answer the first score question.
 - Verify the survey now has enough required information to submit.
5. Answer some optional questions and leave at least one optional question unanswered.
 - Verify optional answers can be selected and changed.
 - Verify unanswered optional questions do not show as required.
6. Navigate the survey using only the keyboard.
 - Use Tab to move between question groups.
 - Use arrow keys to change radio options.
 - Verify focus is visible and selection changes are clear.
7. Press `Alt+F1` while focus is in the survey.
 - Verify accessibility help opens with survey navigation guidance.
 - Close the help and verify focus returns to the survey.
8. Resize the editor group to a narrow width.
 - Verify the segmented choices stack cleanly.
 - Verify list options collapse to one column.
 - Verify the submit button remains reachable.
9. Submit the survey.
 - Verify a success state appears.
 - Verify the survey editor closes automatically after about 5 seconds.
10. Reopen the survey with `Developer: Open Survey`.
 - Verify the same editor-style survey opens again and remains usable.

title: Incorrect position of "--args" gdb argument prevents correct processing of target program arguments.
document:
Incorrect position of "--args" gdb argument prevents correct processing of target program arguments.

- VS Code Version: 1.124.2
- OS Version: Ubuntu 26.04 kernel 7.0.0 (compiled from source).

1. Find a example C program that demonstrates the use of argp (there are lots around).
2. Using gdb from a command line, debug that app using the "--args" flag plus various args for the app.
 for example **/usr/bin/gdb --args ./testapp --device=/dev/xyz --syslog --ver=9 --show --quiet --config=/etc/xyz.conf**
2. Now try debugging that using Visual Code, without using the launch setting "miDebuggerArgs".
 You will find the test app won't process its args corrrectly.
4. Now try debugging again but using the launch setting "miDebuggerArgs": "--args".
 You will find the test app still won't process its args correctly.

The problem (this took me an entire day to figure out because the problem wasn't in my test app at all!).
(The following example outputs are from my test app using the different ways of launching gdb described above) .

Debugging my test app using gdb from the command line works perfectly because the "--args" option appears just before the target executable, and so gdb passes all the following arguments to the target main().
**ARGP_KEY_INIT
parse_opt() key:64 char:d arg:/dev/xyz
parse_opt() key:6C char:l arg:(null)
parse_opt() key:76 char:v arg:9
parse_opt() key:73 char:s arg:(null)
parse_opt() key:71 char:q arg:(null)
parse_opt() key:63 char:c arg:/etc/xyz.conf
ARGP_KEY_NO_ARGS
ARGP_KEY_END
ARGP_KEY_SUCCESS
ARGP_KEY_FINI
argp_parse() done**
The above is the expected output with all app arguments correctly processed.

However using Visual Code debugging without the "miDebuggerArgs": "--args" launch property -
**ARGP_KEY_INIT
parse_opt() key:64 char:d arg:/dev/xyz --syslog --verbose=9 --showkey --quiet --config=/etc/xyz.conf
ARGP_KEY_NO_ARGS
ARGP_KEY_END
ARGP_KEY_SUCCESS
ARGP_KEY_FINI
argp_parse() done**
This is incorrect. The app only processed a single argument that was the entire argument string given to the app.

And using Visual Code debugging with the "miDebuggerArgs": "--args" launch property -
**ARGP_KEY_INIT
parse_opt() key:64 char:d arg:/dev/xyz --syslog --verbose=9 --showkey --quiet --config=/etc/xyz.conf
ARGP_KEY_NO_ARGS
ARGP_KEY_END
ARGP_KEY_SUCCESS
ARGP_KEY_FINI
argp_parse() done**
This is still incorrect, adding the "--arg" option via the "miDebuggerArgs" launch property didn't do anything as it was not placed in the correct position when invoking gdb.
I get this in my Visual Code terminal window after a run using the "--arg" property -
**[1] + Done "/usr/bin/gdb" --interpreter=mi --args --tty=${DbgTerm} 0"/tmp/Microsoft-MIEngine-Out-lot3j3jx.fqc"**

Perhaps Visual Code should have placed "--args" after the "--tty" ????

Environment:
 - OS: Windows 10 22H2 (19045)
 - VS Code version: 1.125.1 (happens every update, across multiple versions)
 - Install type: System Installer, custom path E:\Program Files\ide\Microsoft VS Code

 1. Pin VS Code to the Start menu (tile area). The tile displays correctly.
 2. Wait for an auto-update, or trigger one via "Check for Updates".
 3. After the update completes, open the Start menu.

 Observed behavior:
 The pinned VS Code tile becomes completely invisible/transparent (not a white square —
 truly empty, nothing renders). The shortcut itself is intact, Code.exe exists at the
 correct path, and clicking the invisible tile still launches VS Code normally.

 Workaround that works every time:
 Right-click the blank tile → "Unpin from Start" → re-pin from Code.exe. Or, right-click
 the .lnk shortcut → Properties → Shortcut tab → "Change Icon..." → OK → Apply (without
 actually changing the icon). Both restore the tile immediately.

 What has been ruled out:
 - ❌ Shortcut target path is NOT broken — TargetPath still points to correct Code.exe
 and IconLocation is Code.exe,0
 - ❌ No _ (underscore) subfolder issue — Code.exe is directly in install directory, not
 moved
 - ❌ Icon cache corruption — ie4uinit.exe -show alone doesn't always fix it; restarting
 StartMenuExperienceHost.exe does
 - ❌ Not specific to custom install path — the Code.VisualElementsManifest.xml and its
 referenced hash-named PNG folder are both intact post-update

 Possible root cause (AI-generated analysis):
 The root cause appears to be that VS Code's Inno Setup-based updater replaces Code.exe
 in-place during the update but does not call SHChangeNotify(SHCNE_ASSOCCHANGED, ...) or
 ie4uinit.exe -ClearIconCache after the file replacement. Windows' Start menu tile host
 process (StartMenuExperienceHost.exe) holds a cached rendering of the old Code.exe's
 icon resources in memory. Because the file path hasn't changed, Windows does not
 automatically invalidate the cache, and the stale cached data fails to map to the new
 binary's icon resources — resulting in the tile rendering nothing (invisible rather than
 a white fallback).

 Additionally, Code.VisualElementsManifest.xml references icon PNGs via a hash-named
 subfolder (e.g. \resources\app\resources\win32\code_150x150.png). Even though the
 manifest is updated to the new hash during installation, if StartMenuExperienceHost.exe
 has cached the manifest content from before the update, it may still reference the old
 (now-deleted) hash folder path. This would also cause a transparent tile.

 Suggested fix:
 In the Inno Setup installer script, add a post-install step:

 [Code]
 procedure CurStepChanged(CurStep: TSetupStep);
 begin
 if CurStep = ssPostInstall then
 SHChangeNotify(SHCNE_ASSOCCHANGED, SHCNF_IDLIST, 0, 0);
 end;

 Or in the [Run] section:

 [Run]
 Filename: "{cmd}"; Parameters: "/c ie4uinit.exe -ClearIconCache"; Flags: runhidden

title: Allow Git worktree entries in Source Control Repositories view to default collapsed
document:
Allow Git worktree entries in Source Control Repositories view to default collapsed

## Feature request

Please add a way to keep Git worktree entries collapsed by default in the Source Control Repositories view.

## Current behavior

When a workspace has Git worktrees detected, the Source Control Repositories view shows them under the parent repository. Even if the repository nodes are manually collapsed, opening the Source Control sidebar again can expand the parent repository and reveal the worktree entries.

This makes the Repositories view noisy in multi-root workspaces where each root repository has many worktrees.

## Expected behavior

There should be a way to keep worktree entries collapsed by default while still showing the parent repositories.

For example, one of these would work:

- A setting that makes Git worktree entries default collapsed in the Source Control Repositories view
- A setting that makes repository nodes with worktrees default collapsed
- Preserving the collapsed state of repository nodes when reopening the Source Control sidebar

## Why existing settings do not solve this

`git.detectWorktrees: false` hides worktrees entirely, which is not the desired behavior. I still want worktrees to be available in the Repositories view, just not expanded by default.

`scm.autoReveal: false` does not appear to prevent the repository node from reopening in this case.

I also observed that the workspace storage state can contain an empty expanded list, for example:

but reopening the Source Control sidebar can still show the worktree entries expanded.

## Use case

In a workspace with several repositories and many worktrees, the desired default view is compact:

The worktrees should still be accessible by expanding a repository, but they should not make the top-level Repositories view tall every time the Source Control sidebar is opened.

title: Agent session stuck showing "running" in the UI after the agent has finished
document:
Agent session stuck showing "running" in the UI after the agent has finished

## Impact

A background agent session in the Agents window can stay showing as **running** in the session list indefinitely, even though the agent has finished its work and gone idle. The badge never clears, and in the affected case it stayed stuck across multiple subsequent messages. This is misleading — the user can't tell which sessions are actually still working, and a "completed" session looks like it's hung.

## What's happening

When a session goes idle, the final status/summary updates that should flip the badge from **running → idle** are dispatched against a session that has already been **evicted from the agent host state manager** (`AgentHostStateManager._sessionStates`). The state manager doesn't recognize the session anymore, so it **drops the update** and logs a warning. The badge is left at whatever it last showed — `running`.

This is visible directly in the logs. The moment the agent finishes (`Session idle`), the very next status/meta updates are rejected as belonging to an "unknown session":

The `session/metaChanged` and `setSessionSummaryChanges` actions that carry the post-turn state are the ones being dropped, so the session summary (which drives the list badge) is never updated to `idle`.

The same pattern keeps repeating afterwards for the affected session every time something tries to refresh its summary, which is why the badge never recovers on its own.

## Who is pushing the dropped updates

In a later trace-level capture, the producer is clearly the changeset service. It periodically recomputes git/branch changes for sessions and pushes a summary update keyed by the chat-channel URI (`ahp-chat://default/`) — but the underlying session has already been evicted, so the update is dropped as "unknown session":

(The two base64 values decode to `copilotcli:/25951062-…` and `copilotcli:/d984292b-…` — two different background sessions, so this is not specific to one session.)

## Reproduction

I have not been able to capture the exact failure transition (see caveat below), but based on the logs the trigger is a background session being evicted while idle. Best-known repro:

1. Run several agent sessions in the Agents window so some are in the background (not the focused/subscribed session).
2. Let a background session finish its work and go idle while it is **not** the focused session (so it is unsubscribed and eligible for idle eviction).
3. Observe the session in the list: it can remain showing **running** even though the agent has completed.

In the capture the session was evicted from in-memory state (`_maybeEvictIdleSession` → `removeSession`) while idle; once evicted, the end-of-turn status update lands on an unknown session and is dropped.

## Caveat: the original transition was hard to capture

In a second capture taken specifically to catch this, the window started **after** the session was already stuck. Opening the stuck session and sending it a new message just **restored** it into state and ran new turns:

So that capture shows the recovery, not the failure. To catch the original transition you need logging running **while the session is still in the background going idle/evicted**, before it is touched again.

---

## Analysis / where to look (not a prescribed fix)

There appears to be a **key-form inconsistency** around eviction and summary updates that is worth investigating:

- `AgentHostStateManager._sessionStates` is keyed by the **raw session URI** (`copilotcli:/…`).
- `getSessionState()` normalizes a **chat-channel URI** (`ahp-chat://default/`) down to the session URI before lookup, so it accepts both forms.
- `removeSession()` does a **raw lookup with no normalization**, so it only accepts the session-URI form.
- `AgentHostChangesetService` pushes summary updates keyed by the **chat-channel URI**.

The eviction path can be invoked with either form. In the trace capture the same session was "evicted" twice with different keys — once with a chat-channel key that produced **no** removal, and once with a session key that did:

Two directions worth considering when fixing:
- A producer (e.g. `AgentHostChangesetService`) should not keep computing and pushing summaries for a session that has been evicted — or a summary update for an unknown-but-known-on-disk session should re-register/restore it rather than being silently dropped.
- The eviction/removal and lookup paths should normalize chat-channel vs session URIs consistently so eviction can't half-apply.

Relevant code: `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentHostStateManager.ts` (`getSessionState`, `removeSession`, `setSessionSummaryChanges`, the "Action for unknown session" path), `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentService.ts` (`_maybeEvictIdleSession`), and `AgentHostChangesetService`.

Note: PR microsoft/vscode#322309 promotes the idle-eviction log to `info` and adds a turn-start desync log to make the next capture of this easier to read; it does not fix the underlying drop.

_(Written by Copilot)_

title: Bug: Custom endpoint crashes with "No lowest priority node found" under context limits and silently ignores plain-text apiKey
document:
Bug: Custom endpoint crashes with "No lowest priority node found" under context limits and silently ignores plain-text apiKey

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: [Please fill from the Extensions tab, e.g., v0.23.0]
- VS Code Version: [Please fill from VS Code -> About, e.g., 1.90.1]
- OS Version: macOS (MacBook Air)
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): Custom Language Model Integration (chatLanguageModels.json)
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): Custom Endpoint (minimaxai/minimax-m3 via NVIDIA NIM)
- Logs: See below

### Steps to Reproduce:

1. Configure a custom endpoint in `chatLanguageModels.json` with `"toolCalling": true` and `"maxInputTokens": 16000` (or 8000 to match typical trial API constraints).
2. Install any agent extensions that register multiple tools/schemas (e.g., extensions that register dozens of custom tools, bloating the prompt payload).
3. Open Copilot Chat, select the custom model, and send a message.
4. The client-side token-pruning algorithm crashes with a fatal exception.

---

### Description and Root Cause

I am configuring a custom language model (`minimaxai/minimax-m3` on NVIDIA NIM) using the `chatLanguageModels.json` configuration in VS Code.

When using the model with `"toolCalling": true`, the extension crashes locally with the following error:
`Error: No lowest priority node found (path: Xre) at eEt (/Applications/Visual Studio Code.app/Contents/Resources/app/extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js:173:4513)`

**Detailed Root Cause:**
1. **Excessive Tool Injection:** VS Code automatically injects the JSON schemas of all available agent/extension tools (in my case, over 70 tools totaling ~86KB) into the payload.
2. **Pruning Algorithm Failure:** When trying to compress the prompt payload to fit a lower `"maxInputTokens"` constraint (such as 8000 or 16000) to respect the custom API limits, the token-pruning algorithm fails to safely discard low-priority nodes and throws a fatal client-side exception: `No lowest priority node found`.
3. **UI Selection Disappearance:** If I try to work around this by setting `"toolCalling": false` in `chatLanguageModels.json`, the custom model completely disappears from the Copilot Chat model selection dropdown UI, making it impossible to use without tool injection.

---

### Call Stack / Error Logs:

---

### Related Bug: Plain-text `apiKey` silently ignored in configuration

During troubleshooting, we also found that if an API key is provided as a plain string in `chatLanguageModels.json`:
`"apiKey": "nvapi-SK1VhLO..."`

VS Code does not raise a configuration error. Instead, it silently drops/ignores the key and sends the HTTP request to the custom endpoint with **no `Authorization` header at all**, leading to authorization failures on the receiving server. 

The header is only populated if we force VS Code to use the secret store reference syntax:
`"apiKey": "${input:chat.lm.secret.nvidia-api-key}"`

---

### Expected Behavior
1. **Tool Control**: Setting `"toolCalling": false` in `chatLanguageModels.json` should successfully disable tool schema injection, but the model must remain selectable in the UI dropdown.
2. **Robust Pruning**: The token-pruning engine should degrade gracefully (e.g., truncation or dropping systemic messages) without raising fatal runtime exceptions when payload constraints are tight.
3. **Consistent Auth**: VS Code should support plain-text API keys in `chatLanguageModels.json` as fallback, or at least throw a validation warning/error instead of silently omitting the Authorization header in the outgoing HTTP requests.

title: Chat model picker crashes with 'a.charAt is not a function'
document:
Chat model picker crashes with 'a.charAt is not a function'

## Summary

Clicking the model picker button in VS Code Chat throws `TypeError: a.charAt is not a function` and the dropdown never appears. The crash happens in `getCategoryLabel()` in `chatModelPicker.ts` when it receives a non-string `category` value (including `null` or omitted). The function only guards against `undefined` and `''` in the switch, then unconditionally calls `.charAt(0)` in the default branch.

This is important: the crash is in VS Code's code, not an extension. With all extensions disabled, only Copilot's built-in models register, and those always provide string categories like `'lightweight'`, so the bug doesn't manifest. But the picker still crashes on any third-party provider that omits `category` or passes `null`, which is valid per the API spec (`category?: string`).

I filed this here (not on the extension publisher) because the root defect is the unguarded `.charAt` call in VS Code's picker code. The extension already shipped a workaround in v2.1.6, but the upstream bug means the next extension that doesn't guard `category` will hit the exact same crash.

## Version

- VS Code Version: **Insiders 1.126** (bug introduced here; **Stable 1.125 not affected** — same provider, same account, same quota state, picker works fine there)
- OS Version: **macOS 26.5**
- Copilot Chat Extension Version: built-in (bundled with VS Code)
- Feature: model picker (clicking the model picker button in the Chat input)
- Selected model: N/A — crash happens before any model is selected

## Extensions

The bug requires a BYOK provider registered via `vscode.lm.registerLanguageModelChatProvider` to trigger. I have one extension installed: `gethnet.litellm-connector-copilot` (LiteLLM-backed OpenAI-compatible proxy, vendor: `litellm-connector`). With that extension disabled (or all extensions disabled), the picker opens normally because the only models registered are Copilot's own, which all carry a known string `category`.

Extension Bisect was not used because the crash is in VS Code's code, not the extension's. The extension simply provides a `LanguageModelChatInformation` entry whose `category` field was set to a grouping object `{ label: , order: }` — a non-string value. VS Code's picker iterates all registered models, hits `getCategoryLabel` with that value, and throws.

## Steps to Reproduce

The bug appears when:
1. A language model provider (built-in or third-party) registers a model via `vscode.lm.registerLanguageModelChatProvider` where `LanguageModelChatInformation.category` is not a string (e.g., omitted, `null`, or an object).
2. You click the model picker button in the Chat input (or invoke `workbench.action.chat.openModelPicker`).

**Why quota exhaustion in the original report:** I discovered this with Copilot's quota exhausted because that's when the picker starts showing "Other Models" (third-party vendors). On an active Copilot subscription, the only models listed are Copilot's own, which all use valid string categories, so the bad code path never runs. The bug exists regardless of quota state—it's just more visible (and discoverable) once Copilot models are unavailable.

## Expected Behavior

The picker dropdown should appear and list all registered models. If `category` is non-string, missing, `null`, or empty, the picker should treat it the same way it already handles `undefined` and `''` — omit the category tag and move on.

## Actual Behavior

The dropdown does not appear. No error toast, no log channel entry. The console records one `TypeError: a.charAt is not a function` per click, one per registered model.

## Console Output

Three identical traces on three consecutive clicks:

Quota state in the same session:

## Root Cause

In `src/vs/workbench/contrib/chat/browser/widget/input/chatModelPicker.ts`, the function `getCategoryLabel` has a type signature that says `category: string | undefined`, but the code doesn't match:

The problem: JavaScript `switch` uses **strict equality**, so `case undefined` only matches `undefined`, not `null`. If `category` is `null`, an object, a number, or any other non-string value, it falls through to `default:` where `.charAt(0)` is called unconditionally. That throws.

The public API for `LanguageModelChatInformation` declares `category?: string`, meaning providers are allowed to omit it entirely or pass `null`. VS Code should handle that gracefully, but instead one bad model aborts the entire picker dropdown.

The same `.charAt` pattern appears in two other functions in the same file:
- `getPriceCategoryLabel`
- `getVendorDisplayName`

Both have the same unguarded fallback that will throw on non-string input.

## Suggested Fix

Add a `typeof` guard before the switch, treating non-strings the same way undefined and empty string are already handled:

Apply the same guard to `getPriceCategoryLabel` and `getVendorDisplayName` in the same file. This is behavior-preserving: existing translations stay the same, and any non-string category just gets omitted from the UI instead of crashing.

Optionally (for better diagnostics): add a `logService.warn` in the guard so silent picker crashes show up in the Log (Window) output instead of being invisible outside DevTools.

## Additional Context

**Silent failure.** The crash is invisible in the UI — no error toast, no log channel entry, no status bar hint. Only DevTools console shows the exception. This makes it hard for users to diagnose why the picker won't open.

**Why it's rare today.** Most users see Copilot models first, which all use correct string categories, so the bug path doesn't run. But the picker should be defensive against *any* model with a missing or malformed `category`, not just happen to work because Copilot's descriptors are well-formed.

**Why this is VS Code's responsibility, not the extension's.** The API spec for `LanguageModelChatInformation` declares `category?: string` — it's optional, and it's optional for a reason (not every provider needs or wants to categorize their models). Code that consumes optional fields should always guard against their absence. VS Code's picker iterates and renders models from multiple vendors; it's responsible for handling any value an extension can legally supply under the API contract. This isn't about a misconfigured extension—it's about defensive programming in code that has to work with many different providers.

**Our workaround.** The LiteLLM Connector extension (v2.1.6, already shipped) fixed its side by ensuring `category` is always a valid string. This issue exists to harden the picker itself so future extensions don't need the same workaround.

title: Test: Multi-chat in a Local Agent Host Copilot CLI session (create, switch, rename, delete, restore)
document:
Test: Multi-chat in a Local Agent Host Copilot CLI session (create, switch, rename, delete, restore)

- [x] anyOS @aiday-mar
- [x] anyOS @benvillalobos

Complexity: 4

Authors: @sandy081

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23322285%0A%0A&assignees=sandy081)

---

An Agent Host **Copilot CLI** session in the **Agents window** can now hold **multiple chats** ("peer" chats) side‑by‑side, each with its own conversation, title and status. Peer chats are now **persisted and restored across a window reload**, and deleting a chat asks for **confirmation** first.

This test covers creating, switching, renaming, deleting and restoring multiple chats within a single Copilot CLI session.

## Set up

1. Open the **Agents window**
2. This test is specifically about a **Copilot CLI session created from the Local Agent Host** (the built‑in, locally‑running Copilot CLI agent host — **not** a Copilot Chat session, and **not** a remote/tunnel agent host). Make sure you are signed in to Copilot and the local Copilot CLI agent host is available.
3. Start a **new session** and, in the session‑type picker, choose **Copilot CLI** (the entry provided by the Local Agent Host). Multi‑chat is only enabled for these **Copilot CLI** sessions — other session types intentionally show a single chat.
4. Send a first message so the session is created (no longer "untitled").

## Test steps

### 1. Create additional (peer) chats
- With the **Local Agent Host** Copilot CLI session active, click the **New Chat** (`+`) button in the session toolbar.
- ✅ A new chat **tab** appears in the chat bar next to the existing one and becomes active, showing an empty/new‑chat input.
- Send a message in the new chat and confirm it gets its own response, independent of the first chat.
- Add a second peer chat the same way.
- ✅ The session now shows 3 tabs, each with independent conversations.

### 2. Switch between chats
- Click each tab in turn.
- ✅ Switching shows that chat's own request/response history immediately (no perpetual loading spinner), and the input/scroll position is per‑chat.
- ✅ The in‑progress spinner / unread indicator on a tab reflects only that chat's state (e.g. start a long request in one chat, switch away — only that tab shows the spinner).

### 3. Rename a chat tab (independent of the session)
- Double‑click a chat tab (or right‑click the tab → **Rename**), type a new name, press **Enter**.
- ✅ Only that tab's title changes. The **session title** (in the sessions list / header) is unchanged.
- Press **Escape** while editing → rename is cancelled, the old title stays.

### 4. Rename the session does NOT rename the chats
- Rename the **session** (double‑click the session title in the header / sessions list), give it a new name.
- ✅ The session title updates.
- ✅ Any chat tab you renamed in step 3 keeps its own name.
- ✅ The **default** (first) chat tab — if you never renamed it — follows the session title; if you did rename it, it keeps its independent name.

### 5. Delete a chat asks for confirmation
- Hover a **peer** chat tab and click its **✕ (Close)** button.
- ✅ A confirmation dialog appears: *"Are you sure you want to delete this chat?"* with detail *"This action cannot be undone."* and a **Delete** primary button.
- Click **Cancel** → ✅ the chat is **not** deleted, the tab stays.
- Click the **✕** again → **Delete** → ✅ the chat tab is removed and the remaining chats are unaffected.
- Note: the **default** (first) chat has no close button — it lives and dies with the session.

### 6. Persistence across reload (the main fix)
- In the session, make sure you have the default chat plus **at least one peer chat**, each with some conversation history.
- Run **Developer: Reload Window**.
- After reload, re‑open the same Copilot CLI session.
- ✅ **All** chats are restored — the peer chat tabs are still present (not just the default chat).
- Click into the **restored peer chat**.
- ✅ Each one loads its own history reliably.
