title: Chat implicit "active selection" context stays stuck after its source document is closed/deleted
document:
Chat implicit "active selection" context stays stuck after its source document is closed/deleted

Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.53.0

Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): Agent mode (applies to all chat modes)
Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): All models (model-independent)
Logs: na (no related entries in Copilot Chat log — see Debug data)

1. Open a document and select some text → it attaches as the implicit "active selection" context chip in the Chat input.
2. Delete or close that document while its editor stays focused.
3. Submit a chat turn → the stale selection is still attached.
4. Try to remove it via "unpin" in the chat window → it only suppresses for one send and re-attaches next turn.
( Symbol pin is still "not attached" though, but visible in Chat window and also llm agrees to see it fresh again. )
5. Focus another editor chat window → the chip frequently persists. Cannot get rid of it.
6. Make a fresh text selection / copy text in another editor (or open a real file with a selection) → only now does the chip clear.

##### Remark: _WriteUp via Opus 4.8 of the situation was performend from here on

**Summary**

The Chat input's implicit "active selection" context is bound to the last active text selection and is only invalidated on the next selection-change event. Closing or deleting the backing document emits no such event, so the chip remains attached and keeps being sent on every turn. "Unpin" does not remove it permanently, and merely focusing another editor is often insufficient.

**Current Behaviour**
After the source document is closed/deleted, the active-selection chip lingers in the input and re-attaches each turn. It is only cleared when a new selection-change fires elsewhere (selecting/copying text in another editor, or opening a real file with a selection). Reproduced with both a memory:-scheme virtual document and a regular workspace file.

**Expected Behaviour**
When the backing document of an implicit active-selection context is closed or deleted, the context should be invalidated immediately. Unpinning the chip should also remove it permanently rather than for a single submission.

**Workaround**
Trigger a fresh selection-change — select or copy text in another editor, or open a different file — which replaces the stale reference. Alternatively start a New Chat or reload the window.

**Impact**
Stale, unremovable context silently pollutes every subsequent turn, wasting tokens and skewing the model's grounding toward a deleted document, with no reliable UI affordance to clear it.

**Debug data**
Reviewed GitHub.copilot-chat/GitHub Copilot Chat.log across all windows for the session — no related error/warning is logged, consistent with a renderer-side chat-widget state issue rather than an extension fault. Only unrelated entries were present ([CopilotCLIModels] 401 Unauthorized: Bad credentials at startup, and a benign Error compiling input schema for tool run_in_terminal: unknown keyword "enumDescriptions").

title: Intermittent 20–50 second unresponsive startup on macOS; reproduces with extensions disabled and fresh user-data directory
document:
Intermittent 20–50 second unresponsive startup on macOS; reproduces with extensions disabled and fresh user-data directory

## Description

VS Code intermittently opens its window quickly but remains slow or unresponsive for approximately 20–50 seconds before becoming usable.

The problem is intermittent. Reopening the same repository is often fast, so a single successful startup does not demonstrate that a configuration has fixed the issue. It occurs across different repositories rather than one specific large workspace.

In some slow runs, `Developer: Startup Performance` records a 35–52 second delay in extension registration. In other visibly slow runs, including one captured with `--prof-startup`, the startup performance marks report only approximately 1–2 seconds and do not contain the full user-visible delay.

## Environment

* VS Code: 1.124.2
* Commit: 6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c
* OS reported by VS Code: Darwin 25.5.0
* Hardware: Apple M3 Pro, 36 GB RAM

The problem was also seen with VS Code 1.123.0.

## Isolation already performed

The problem still occurs after:

* Disabling all marketplace extensions

* Starting with `--disable-extensions`

* Starting with a new temporary user-data directory

* Combining both:

 `code -n --disable-extensions --user-data-dir /tmp/vscode-test-userdata `

* Reinstalling VS Code

* Testing multiple repositories

One clean-user-data and extensions-disabled run recorded:

* `shared process connection ready`: approximately 35 seconds
* `register extensions & spawn extension host`: approximately 36 seconds
* `extensions registered`: approximately 37 seconds

Another run with marketplace extensions disabled recorded:

* `shared process connection ready`: 24 ms
* `register extensions & spawn extension host`: approximately 52 seconds
* `extensions registered`: approximately 52 seconds

In that second run, the extension host appeared to be waiting for initialization data rather than executing a slow marketplace extension.

## Latest profiling run

I fully quit VS Code and launched it using:

`code --prof-startup `

The application was noticeably slow to become responsive, but the startup performance marks reported:

* `workbench ready`: 1531 ms
* `register extensions & spawn extension host`: 180 ms
* `extensions registered`: 1663 ms

The generated CPU profiles cover only approximately 4–5 seconds each, so they do not appear to include the complete perceived delay.

I have attached:

* Main-process CPU profile
* Renderer CPU profile
* Extension-host CPU profile
* Startup Performance reports from multiple slow runs
* macOS process samples captured during a slow run

## Expected behaviour

The window should become responsive shortly after appearing, and the performance diagnostics should account for the period during which the UI cannot be used normally.

## Actual behaviour

The window appears, but interaction can remain delayed or unresponsive for tens of seconds. Sometimes this corresponds to extension-registration timing, while other times the user-visible delay is not represented in Startup Performance or the automatically generated startup CPU profiles.

Copilot CLI AH not picking up the platform-specific hooks

In the microsoft/vscode-docs repo we have [pre- and post-tool use hooks](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-docs/blob/main/.github/hooks/docs-instructions.json) that have a platform-specific overrides (use PowerShell on Windows).

When running a Copilot CLI [Agent Host] session on Windows, the PowerShell script is not being invoked and the default (bash script) is called, which doesn't work on Windows.

Using latest Insiders.

**Steps to repro**

1. Open the vscode-docs repo in Insiders on Windows
2. Start a new Copilot CLI [Agent Host] session
3. Send a prompt that invokes a tool
4. Notice that the `.sh` hook scripts are opened instead of running the PowerShell scripts

title: Ctrl+Shift+I always activates GitHub Copilot tab instead of last active chat extension (no Copilot license)
document:
Ctrl+Shift+I always activates GitHub Copilot tab instead of last active chat extension (no Copilot license)

## Problem

When pressing `Ctrl+Shift+I` ("Open Chat (Agent)"), VS Code always activates the GitHub Copilot Chat tab — even when:
- the user has no GitHub Copilot license
- another chat extension (e.g. Claude Code) is installed and was previously active

After the Copilot tab opens, clicking the Claude Code tab immediately causes the Copilot tab to disappear (likely because Copilot hides itself on focus-loss when there is no valid license). This results in a jarring flash every time the shortcut is used.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Install the Claude Code VS Code extension
2. Do **not** have a GitHub Copilot subscription/license
3. Press `Ctrl+Shift+I`
4. Observe: the GitHub Copilot Chat tab opens/activates instead of Claude Code
5. Click the Claude Code tab
6. Observe: the Copilot tab immediately disappears

## Expected Behavior

`Ctrl+Shift+I` should activate the last-used chat extension, or VS Code should not default to an extension the user is not licensed for.

## Workaround Attempted

Setting `chat.disableAIFeatures: true` (suggested in #295690) disables the **entire** chat sidebar, making all AI chat extensions (including Claude Code) inaccessible. This is not a viable workaround.

## Environment

- **VS Code Version:** 1.125.0 (x64)
- **OS Version:** Windows 11 Enterprise 10.0.26200

title: VS Code can become stuck reopening the Agent login window and prevent access to the normal editor
document:
VS Code can become stuck reopening the Agent login window and prevent access to the normal editor

- VS Code Version: 1.124.2 (User Setup)
- OS Version: Windows 11 Version 25H2 (OS Build 26200.8655)

1. Launch VS Code while not signed in to any account.
2. Trigger the built-in "Open in Agents" feature
3. An Agent window opens and prompts for sign-in
4. Two windows are now open:
- Normal VS Code editor window
- Agent login window
6. Close the normal VS Code editor window first.
7. Close the Agent login window.
8. Launch VS Code again.

Expected Behavior:

VS Code should open the normal editor window or provide a clear way to return to the editor.

Actual Behavior:

Only the Agent login window reopens.
The normal editor window does not appear.

Additional observations:

-Opening a folder with VS Code does not restore the normal editor window.
-Ending VS Code processes does not resolve the issue.
-There is no obvious way to exit the Agent login experience and return to the editor.

Workaround:

Running:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e55cdd1f-3fc9-4953-a0d9-b9ba6e9c2d21

code --disable-extensions

allows recovery, but only when the Agent login window is already open.
Running the command while no VS Code window is open does not appear to resolve the issue.

Impact:

This can leave users effectively locked out of the normal VS Code interface and may be particularly confusing for new users who are not familiar with command-line recovery steps.

Notes:

The issue occurs with the built-in "Open in Agents" feature and is not related to a third-party extension.

title: Agents: Need a way to heal broken worktree paths
document:
Agents: Need a way to heal broken worktree paths

The agents window needs a way to "heal" the scenario when the path to a worktree for an active session is changed / broken. 

To recreate:

1. Make a new workspace at `~/projects-a/workspace-1`
2. Start a new worktree-based chat session using `workspace-1`
3. Go back to new chat screen
4. Rename the parent "projects-a" folder to "projects-b"
5. Select the chat session you kicked off a minute ago
6. See error notification

Core problem: The original chat session is backed by a worktree located at `~/projects-a/workspace-1.worktrees/name-of-session/`. When we renamed projects-a to projects-b we broke that path and now agents window sees the worktree as not existing anymore. This in turn ruins your ability to keep working on that chat session.

We need a way to tell the agents window "oops, the worktree for this session is now located at `~/projects-b/workspace-1.worktrees/name-of-session/`"

title: Internal Stop hook forces agent to continue indefinitely ("Keep working autonomously") — no setting to disable
document:
Internal Stop hook forces agent to continue indefinitely ("Keep working autonomously") — no setting to disable

The Copilot Chat runtime has an internal Stop hook that fires when the agent tries to end its response. The runtime injects this message:

> *"Keep working autonomously until the original request is fully satisfied. DO NOT repeat or restate your previous response — pick up where you left off. If you were planning, stop planning and start implementing, and resolve any errors or remaining steps before you stop."*

This happens even when the agent has genuinely finished the task and the user is satisfied with the answer. There is no setting or workaround to control this.

- VS Code Version: 1.101.x (latest stable, June 2026)
- OS Version: Linux (WSL2)

## How I found this

I was having a research/discussion conversation with the agent (model: Xiaomi MiMo) — no code to write, no files to edit, just questions and answers. Every time the agent tried to wrap up, the hook fired and forced it to keep going. This happened repeatedly across multiple sessions, always on non-implementation conversations.

## What I tested

**First hypothesis: triggered by `task_complete` tool**

I told the agent to respond and end *without* calling `task_complete`. The hook still fired. Not about the tool call.

**Second hypothesis: about response patterns**

I asked the agent to just say one sentence and stop. The hook fired anyway. The runtime seems to detect "conclusive" language in the response text and blocks the stop regardless of what tools were or weren't called.

**Third hypothesis: a user hook I configured**

I checked everything:
- `.github/hooks/` — empty in all workspace folders
- `~/.copilot/ide/` — no hook config files, only a lock file
- `.claude/settings.json` — doesn't exist
- No workspace-level hooks of any kind

The hook is internal to the Copilot Chat runtime. No external hooks are involved.

**How many times it fires**

In a single conversation turn, the hook fired **7 times** without me sending any new messages. Each time the agent tried to stop, the runtime blocked it. The agent was forced to produce filler content just to satisfy the hook.

## Why this is a problem

1. **Burns credits for nothing** — each forced continuation is another LLM call with no value to the user
2. **Makes the agent worse** — forced to keep going, it repeats itself or produces low-quality filler
3. **Ignores the user** — the user's task was done, but the runtime overrides the agent's judgment
4. **Worse with some models** — concise models get punished because the runtime interprets brevity as "giving up early"

## What would help

A setting to control this, something like:

or even just:

The [Agent Hooks docs](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agent-customization/hooks) already describe how `Stop` hooks can block the agent from stopping with `decision: "block"`. The runtime uses this same mechanism internally but gives users no way to configure it.

title: Remote-WSL: GUI-triggered npm scripts and Git hooks fail with "command not found" for fnm-managed Node.js (npm/pnpm)
document:
Remote-WSL: GUI-triggered npm scripts and Git hooks fail with "command not found" for fnm-managed Node.js (npm/pnpm)

- VS Code Version: 1.124.1 / 1.124.2 / 1.125.0
- OS Version: Windows 11 25H2 (WSL 2 with Debian 13)

1. Launch VS Code on Windows 11 via desktop shortcut or Start Menu.
2. Connect to a WSL 2 Debian 13 environment using the Remote-WSL extension.
3. Open a node.js project, containing a `package.json` file with any script defined (e.g., `"test": "pnpm --version"` or `"build": "echo hello"`), but crucially the script invokes the `npm` or `pnpm` binary itself (i.e., the script content calls these executables).
4. Click the "Run" button for that script in the "NPM Scripts" view on the left sidebar (or use the "Run Script" button in the `package.json` editor).
5. Observe the output in the terminal.

**Actual Result:**
The terminal outputs `pnpm: command not found` (or `npm: command not found`) and the task fails. Example:

(If the script invokes `npm`, the error will be `npm: command not found`.)

**Expected Result:**
The command should execute successfully, just like when typed manually in VS Code's integrated terminal (e.g., manually entering `pnpm run test` or `npm run test` works fine).

**Additional Information:**

1. **Environment Setup**:
 - Node.js 24.16.0 is managed by `fnm` (v1.39.0) and initialized **only** in `~/.bashrc` via `eval "$(fnm env --use-on-cd --shell bash)"`. `fnm` dynamically sets `PATH` based on the current directory, meaning the paths to Node.js and its associated binaries are not static—they are injected at shell runtime.
 - `pnpm@10` is installed and activated via Node.js's built-in `corepack` (and `npm` is also provided by Node.js).
 - Git hooks (e.g., `pre-commit`) that rely on `pnpm` for linting/formatting also fail with the same `command not found` error.
2. **Behavior Comparison**:
 - **Broken**: Clicking the GUI "Run npm script" button or triggering a Git hook fails with `command not found` (for both `pnpm` and `npm`).
 - **Working**: Manually typing the same command (e.g., `pnpm run test` or `npm run test`) in VS Code's integrated WSL terminal works perfectly.
3. **Version Regression**: This issue **was not occurred in earlier VS Code versions**. It appears to have started with recent updates (around 1.124.x). Due to the frequent release cycle, we cannot precisely pinpoint the exact introducing version.
4. **Related Setting**:
 - `terminal.integrated.shellIntegration.enabled` is set to `true` (default).

**Root Cause Analysis:**
This issue likely stems from VS Code's GUI processes (like the NPM Scripts view or Git hook triggers) not correctly inheriting the `PATH` environment variable that is dynamically set in WSL via non‑interactive shell configuration files like `~/.bashrc`. These GUI actions may spawn a non‑interactive shell, which does not source `~/.bashrc`, thus omitting the `fnm`‑dynamically‑managed Node.js binaries (including `pnpm` and `npm`) from the `PATH`. This behavior is inconsistent with the integrated terminal, which typically sources interactive shell configurations. Notably, because `fnm` paths are dynamic, there is no simple static workaround like hardcoding paths.

title: Copilot Chat: auto-generated title corrupted with wrong-language characters ("原因" appended)
document:
Copilot Chat: auto-generated title corrupted with wrong-language characters ("原因" appended)

ElectronBuildId: 14159160

Open Copilot Chat and start a new conversation
Ask a question (e.g., "why microsoft is down")
Observe the auto-generated chat title in the chat tab
Expected Behavior
The title should be generated in the same language as the conversation (English), e.g. "Microsoft service outage".

Actual Behavior
The title appears as "Microsoft service outage原因" — Chinese characters (原因, meaning "reason") were unexpectedly appended to an otherwise English title. The user did not rename the chat.

Frequency
Happened once; difficult to reproduce on demand (suspected data corruption / streaming glitch).

Possible Cause (Hypothesis)
The chat title is auto-generated server-side by an AI model. If the model receives partially corrupted input due to a truncated stream, broken packet, or buffer overrun during a chat response, it may emit unexpected tokens from its multilingual vocabulary — in this case, Chinese characters appended to the end of the title.

This may be related to the existing chat title persistence issues:

[#316994]
[#316393]
[#312948]

Additional Context
The vscode-copilot-chat repo has been archived. The user's VS Code display language is set to English, so this is not a locale issue.

title: Agent host sessions stuck in "thinking" when pending_confirmation arrives without active protocol turn
document:
Agent host sessions stuck in "thinking" when pending_confirmation arrives without active protocol turn

## Problem

Agent host sessions permanently hang in "thinking" state when a `pending_confirmation` signal arrives after the protocol turn has already completed. The user cannot recover — even sending "Continue" does nothing.

## Reproduction scenario

1. User triggers a workflow that delegates to a subagent (e.g., "rubber duck review and address feedback")
2. The subagent completes → `ChatTurnComplete` fires → protocol turn ends
3. A `userPromptSubmitted` hook fires → triggers SDK continuation (new internal turns without a new protocol turn)
4. SDK calls a tool (e.g., `view`) that requires permission approval
5. `pending_confirmation` signal is emitted
6. **Bug**: `agentSideEffects._handleAgentSignal` finds no active turn (`getActiveTurnId()` returns `undefined`) and silently drops the signal
7. The deferred promise in `_pendingPermissions` never resolves
8. Session hangs forever at `await deferred.p` in `_handlePermissionRequest`

## Root Cause

In `agentSideEffects.ts`, the "no active turn" fallback path only handled `action` signals (dispatching them with empty turnId). The `pending_confirmation` signal — which must go through `_handleToolReady` for auto-approval checks — was silently dropped, leaving the permission callback unresolved.

The same issue existed in the subagent path where `_handleToolReady` was only called when `subTurnId` was truthy.

## Fix

PR: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/321843

- Route `pending_confirmation` through `_handleToolReady(signal, sessionKey, '', agent)` in the no-active-turn fallback
- Fix subagent path: `subTurnId ?? ''` instead of conditional skip
- Added regression test

## Key files

- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/agentSideEffects.ts` (lines 346-382)
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/copilot/copilotAgentSession.ts` (`_handlePermissionRequest`)
- `src/vs/platform/agentHost/node/sessionPermissions.ts` (`createToolReadyAction`)

title: Add setting to enable automatically focusing newly created debug session
document:
Add setting to enable automatically focusing newly created debug session

I would like to add a flag to enable automatically selecting new debug sessions when starting one. This was built-in until this issue prompted the behaviour to be changed: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/128132

In my opinion the previous default behaviour was preferable. I often have long-running debug sessions for things like API servers or web servers. While working on features, I will often use a mix of testing the implementation manually and writing automated tests. When running automated tests via a launch configuration, it is always the debug session I am most interested in, specifically looking at the debug console.

This feature would also be helpful when restarting a debug session. Currently if you restart a debug session, the newly started debug session won't be focused even though it was before.

I would like for a settings flag to be added which will re-enable the behaviour that was removed.

title: Enterprise MCP OAuth: support stronger client assurance than public client_id + loopback redirect
document:
Enterprise MCP OAuth: support stronger client assurance than public client_id + loopback redirect

## Summary

I would like VS Code to support a stronger enterprise-grade authentication model for remote MCP servers protected by OAuth.

The current MCP OAuth model appears to rely on the standard native/public-client OAuth pattern:

* public `client_id`
* authorization code flow
* PKCE
* loopback redirect URI such as `127.0.0.1`
* optionally dynamic client registration

This is acceptable for many developer scenarios, but it is not sufficient for regulated enterprise environments where the resource server must distinguish approved MCP clients from arbitrary local applications running on the same machine.

This is not a traditional security vulnerability report. This is a feature request / enterprise hardening request.

Related issue:

* #321834 — MCP OAuth stale cached token / client recovery issue

---

## Problem statement

For enterprise MCP deployments, especially in regulated environments such as banking, user authentication alone is not enough.

We also need a stronger answer to this question:

> Which client application is being trusted to access internal MCP capabilities?

The current native/public-client OAuth model does not strongly answer that question.

A public OAuth `client_id` does not prove that the caller is actually the VS Code binary.

A loopback redirect URI such as `127.0.0.1` is a standard native-app OAuth pattern, but it also does not prove that the requesting application is a trusted or approved client. Another local application can initiate the same public-client OAuth flow.

PKCE protects the authorization code exchange, but it is not client attestation. It proves that the party redeeming the code owns the corresponding `code_verifier`; it does not prove that the party is an approved enterprise client such as VS Code.

Therefore, the current flow is good for user authorization, but not strong enough for enterprise client assurance.

---

## Real-world enterprise scenario

In our environment:

* Remote MCP server is exposed behind an enterprise gateway
* OAuth authorization is handled by an enterprise IdP
* Dynamic client registration is intentionally restricted
* The gateway returns only a fixed, pre-approved public client profile
* The environment follows a banking-style threat model
* We do not want arbitrary local applications to be able to participate in the same MCP OAuth flow simply by reusing the same public client profile

Restricting dynamic client registration is useful, but it does not fully solve the problem.

It prevents arbitrary clients from registering arbitrary metadata or redirect URIs, but it does not cryptographically prove that the caller is the approved VS Code client. In a public/native OAuth flow, the `client_id` is not a secret and should not be treated as proof of client identity.

Today, the safest approach is to compensate outside VS Code using:

* gateway policy
* user/group authorization
* token audience validation
* token scope validation
* network controls
* device controls
* tool-level authorization
* audit logging

These controls are useful and necessary, but they do not solve the core product gap:

> VS Code currently has no enterprise-grade MCP auth mode that provides stronger client assurance than a standard public OAuth native-app flow.

---

## Why this matters

For general developer tooling, public client + PKCE + loopback redirect may be acceptable.

For regulated enterprises, this can be insufficient because:

1. `client_id` is not a secret
2. loopback redirect is not proof of trusted application identity
3. PKCE is not client attestation
4. public/native OAuth does not prove that the caller is VS Code
5. enterprises may need to allow only approved MCP clients or managed client instances
6. sensitive MCP tools may expose internal systems, internal documentation, developer platforms, privileged automation, or organizational data

This becomes especially important when MCP servers expose sensitive internal tools or internal knowledge sources.

---

## Requested feature

Please consider adding an enterprise-oriented MCP OAuth mode or policy surface in VS Code.

Useful capabilities could include one or more of the following:

### 1. Enterprise-managed MCP OAuth mode

A managed mode where administrators can constrain MCP OAuth behavior, for example:

* allow only pre-approved MCP auth configurations
* disable or constrain arbitrary dynamic client registration
* require stricter redirect URI patterns
* require managed authentication providers
* expose clearer lifecycle controls for cached MCP OAuth credentials
* provide admin-visible separation of MCP auth state from general authentication state

### 2. Stronger client assurance

Support mechanisms stronger than a plain public native-client identity, for example:

* DPoP-bound tokens
* sender-constrained access tokens
* enterprise brokered authentication
* device-bound authentication where feasible
* client instance attestation where feasible
* integration points for managed enterprise authentication flows

The exact mechanism may depend on VS Code architecture and the MCP specification, but the current public-client model is not sufficient for some enterprise deployments.

### 3. Policy controls for enterprises

Administrative controls for MCP authentication, such as:

* allowlist of approved MCP servers
* allowlist of approved MCP OAuth issuers
* allowlist of approved MCP client profiles
* per-server authentication policy
* ability to require specific OAuth capabilities
* ability to reject insecure or unmanaged MCP auth configurations
* better visibility into cached MCP OAuth clients and credentials

### 4. Documentation for regulated deployments

Official guidance would also be valuable, explicitly documenting that:

* public `client_id` does not prove client identity
* loopback redirect does not prove trusted application identity
* PKCE is not client attestation
* additional gateway, device, network, scope, audience, and tool-level controls are required for regulated environments
* arbitrary dynamic client registration may be inappropriate in enterprise environments
* a hardened enterprise profile may be needed for sensitive MCP servers

---

## Concrete asks

At minimum, it would help if VS Code provided:

* an enterprise-oriented MCP auth mode or policy surface
* a way to constrain or manage approved MCP OAuth client behavior
* improved support for stronger token-binding or client-assurance patterns
* clearer admin guidance for secure MCP deployment in regulated environments
* better visibility and lifecycle management for MCP OAuth credentials and dynamically registered clients

---

## Non-goals / clarification

This issue is not saying that OAuth native-app flows are universally broken.

This issue is not saying that loopback redirects are invalid.

This issue is not saying that PKCE is useless.

This issue is not claiming that VS Code currently has a traditional exploitable vulnerability.

This issue is saying that the baseline public native-client OAuth model is not sufficient for every MCP deployment, especially in regulated enterprise environments where approved-client assurance matters.

---

## Why I am filing this in VS Code

VS Code is one of the most important MCP clients.

Enterprise adoption of MCP will likely depend on whether clients can support stronger authentication and policy models than baseline public native OAuth.

If the right long-term solution also requires changes in the MCP specification, I am happy to file a related proposal there as well. However, VS Code is an appropriate place to start because this affects real-world MCP deployability today.

Thanks.

title: MCP OAuth: VS Code does not evict a stale dynamically-registered client / expired token on HTTP 401 (remote server behind APISIX + Keycloak)
document:
MCP OAuth: VS Code does not evict a stale dynamically-registered client / expired token on HTTP 401 (remote server behind APISIX + Keycloak)

- VS Code Version: Visual Studio Code - Insiders 1.125.0-insider
- OS Version: Windows 11 Enterprise, 10.0.26100 (Build 26100)

> MCP OAuth (RFC 9728 / 8414 / 7591 discovery + dynamic client registration + token caching) is handled by VS Code core, not by an extension — so this reproduces with all extensions disabled.

## Summary

When VS Code connects to a **remote HTTP MCP server** protected by OAuth, it caches the dynamically-registered `client_id` **and** the access token. Once that cached credential becomes invalid (token expires, or the client/registration changes server-side), VS Code keeps sending the **stale** token, gets **HTTP 401**, and does **not** automatically refresh the token, evict the cached client, or re-run discovery/registration/authorization. The MCP server stays stuck in a failed (401) state until the user manually clears the dynamic auth state (Sign Out / "forget cached tools" / `Authentication: Remove Dynamic Authentication Providers`).

A plain HTTP client (PowerShell) that mints a **fresh** bearer token on every run, hitting the **same** gateway endpoint, never reproduces the problem — which points at VS Code's credential cache/refresh handling rather than the server.

Likely the same root cause as #263990 / #250960 / #270383 — filing with an APISIX + Keycloak (OIDC) reproduction and the side-by-side "fresh-token HTTP client works" evidence.

## Environment / setup (anonymized)

- Remote MCP server (`"type": "http"`, streamable HTTP/SSE) behind an **APISIX** gateway; **Keycloak** as the OAuth Authorization Server (OIDC). MCP server = Resource Server.
- Discovery / flow endpoints exposed by the gateway:
 
- `/register` returns a **fixed, pre-provisioned public client** (`client_id: vscode-mcp-public-client`, `token_endpoint_auth_method: none`, authorization_code + PKCE, redirect_uris `http://127.0.0.1:*` and `https://vscode.dev/redirect`). The client is pre-created in Keycloak.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure a remote `"type": "http"` MCP server that requires OAuth and advertises RFC 9728 / 8414 / 7591 discovery (gateway + IdP as above).
2. In VS Code Insiders, connect to it and complete the OAuth flow in the browser. Confirm tools are discovered and tool calls succeed. (In our case: `Discovered 33 tools`.)
3. Invalidate the cached access token **without changing the configuration** — any of: let the access token expire, re-login/rotate the client on the IdP, or restart the server so previously-issued tokens are rejected.
4. Trigger another MCP request from VS Code (use a tool, or restart the server entry).
5. Observe that VS Code re-sends the stale token, receives 401, and the server stays failed.

## Expected behavior

On a 401 from a previously-authorized MCP server, VS Code should self-heal: (1) try the stored `refresh_token`; (2) if that fails, evict the cached token **and** the cached dynamic client registration; (3) re-run discovery → DCR → authorization (prompting only if interaction is needed); (4) retry the request — without the user manually clearing caches.

## Actual behavior

VS Code keeps sending the stale token, the gateway keeps returning 401, and the server is shown as failed/unavailable. Recovery requires **manual** action: Sign Out / "forget cached tools", or `Authentication: Remove Dynamic Authentication Providers`, then restart the server. After that the OAuth flow re-runs and everything works — until the cached credential next goes invalid.

## Logs (anonymized, trace level)

Request made while the cached token was still valid — `status:200`, valid JSON-RPC result (included to show the server is healthy, and to highlight the header note below):

**Representative failing sequence** — *reconstructed from our environment and masked. The line format is taken verbatim from VS Code's own trace output (only `status`/`method`/`id` differ); the behavior shown matches what #263990 documents (stale token re-sent, 401 logged, no refresh or re-registration). If/when the verbatim capture is available it should replace this block.*

## Workaround

`Authentication: Remove Dynamic Authentication Providers` (or Sign Out + clear cached tools), then restart the MCP server — forces a fresh DCR + OAuth flow and restores the connection.

## Additional notes (full disclosure of gateway-side quirks)

Two aspects of our gateway are non-standard; we're fixing them, but want to confirm whether either interacts with VS Code's auth state machine:

1. **`WWW-Authenticate` is returned on every response, including HTTP 200** (statically injected alongside `x-accel-buffering: no`, as in the trace), not only on 401. RFC 7235 says it belongs with a 401. Does VS Code treat this header on a non-401 response as an auth signal?
2. **`/register` returns a fixed client** (same `client_id` every time) rather than real per-client registration. Could an idempotent DCR response affect VS Code's ability to evict/re-register when the cached token later becomes invalid?

Even setting these aside, the core ask stands: VS Code should not get **permanently** stuck on a stale cached token/client and require manual cache clearing.

## Possibly related / duplicate

- #263990 — VS Code does not automatically refresh expired OAuth tokens.
- #250960 — VS Code does not evict the client when it can't be authorized; no way to delete a cached client.
- #270383 — MCP OAuth fails to retry after server restart when scopes are unchanged.
- #269379 — No clean way to remove stale MCP OAuth credentials.
- #256198 — Missing documented method for clearing the MCP cache.

title: GHCP: memoryInstructions are injected into the system prompt even when the memory tool is disabled
document:
GHCP: memoryInstructions are injected into the system prompt even when the memory tool is disabled

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: v0.54.2026061702
- VS Code Version: 1.126.0-insider (commit 226cd5d573076e1d3b260d21409538fbc67cb059)
- OS Version: macOS (Darwin arm64 25.5.0)
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): agent mode
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): Claude Opus 4.x
- Logs: Not attached — system prompt inspected via Chat Debug

#### Summary

When the `memory` tool is disabled (via `github.copilot.chat.tools.memory.enabled: false`, or by unchecking it in the tools picker), the `` block is still injected into the agent system prompt. The instructions tell the model to consult, read, and write memory files — but the tool needed to do so is not available. The model then attempts to look for memory files (often falling back to `read_file`/`create_file` on a `/memories/` path that does not exist), producing confusing behavior and wasted turns.

The `` block should be conditional: include it only when the `memory` tool is actually enabled and provisioned.

#### Steps to Reproduce

1. Disable the memory tool: set `github.copilot.chat.tools.memory.enabled: false` (or uncheck it in the agent tools picker).
2. Start a new Agent mode chat.
3. Inspect the system prompt via Chat Debug.
4. Observe the full `` block is present even though the tool is disabled.

#### Expected behavior

When the `memory` tool is disabled, `` (and the `` / `` / `` context blocks) are omitted from the system prompt. The model is not instructed to use a capability it does not have.

#### Actual behavior

`` is always injected regardless of whether the `memory` tool is enabled. The model is told to "consult your memory files," "check your memory for relevant notes," and "record what you learned," and it tries to act on this — looking for memory files that are unreachable because the tool is off.

For reference, the injected block:

#### Related issues

- #298822 / #308374 / #298821 — "memory tool not registered → memoryInstructions still present" (closed as completed). The same class of mismatch, but those were about the tool never being registered/shipped; this report is specifically about the tool being **disabled by the user via setting** while the instructions remain.
- #309245 — `vscode/memory` tool never provisioned for GPT-5.4 while instructions are present (open). Same root symptom (instructions present, tool unavailable), different trigger.

#### Suggested fix

Gate the rendering of `` (and the memory context blocks) on the enablement/provisioning state of the `memory` tool, so the prompt and the available tools stay in sync.

[Codespaces] Copilot model selector not populated until first message is sent

## Summary
When starting a GitHub Codespace, the Copilot plugin UI does not show all available models in the model selector dropdown until a dummy message is sent.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a fresh GitHub Codespace
2. Open Copilot chat
3. Look at the model selector dropdown
4. Observe that models are missing or the dropdown is incomplete
5. Send a dummy message (e.g., "hi")
6. After the response, the model selector now shows all available models

## Expected Behavior
The model selector should be fully populated with all available models immediately when Copilot chat opens, without requiring any interaction.

## Actual Behavior
The model selector is empty or incomplete until the first message is sent to Copilot.

## Workaround
Send a dummy message like "hi" to trigger model loading.

## Environment
- GitHub Codespaces
- VS Code (web or desktop client)
- Latest Copilot extension

title: [Codespaces] Copilot agent sessions and chat history lost when Codespace resumes from sleep
document:
[Codespaces] Copilot agent sessions and chat history lost when Codespace resumes from sleep

## Summary
When a GitHub Codespace resumes after being suspended due to inactivity, all Copilot agent sessions and full conversation context are lost.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a GitHub Codespace
2. Create multiple Copilot chat sessions or agent conversations
3. Leave the Codespace idle until it goes to sleep (default: 30 minutes)
4. Resume the Codespace
5. Reopen Copilot chat
6. Observe that all previous chat history, agent sessions, and conversation context are gone

## Expected Behavior
Copilot chat history and agent sessions should persist across Codespace sleep/wake cycles, similar to how other VS Code extensions persist their state.

## Actual Behavior
All Copilot chat history, agent sessions, and full context are lost when the Codespace resumes.

## Impact
This is disruptive during development workflows, as users lose important agent context and conversation history that was needed for ongoing work.

## Environment
- GitHub Codespaces
- VS Code (web or desktop client)
- Latest Copilot extension

title: Flexible "Centered Layout"
document:
Flexible "Centered Layout"

There is option in View > Appearance called "Centered Layout", it works perfectly while no sidebars are open and vscode is in fullscreen mode, but the moment you open anything alongside it, Centered Layout just becomes annoying to deal with...

When you open sidebar, the "Centered Layout" moves content to the center of free space that is left after sidebar opens instead of adjusting itself and hold the center position of entire window. Same goes when you have more than one window opened and try to split-screen.

The solution: "Centered Layout" option should add minimum width for content, so after resizing, it doesnt lose 50% of view area for un-needed centering. Also it should be centered to the middle of window and not middle of 'free space left'.

title: Deprecate built-in chat "Edit" mode
document:
Deprecate built-in chat "Edit" mode

## Summary

Edit mode (`ChatModeKind.Edit`) is one of the three built-in chat modes (Ask / Edit / Agent). It is already in a "deprecated but supported" state:

- The mode picker only surfaces Edit when Agent mode is disabled by enterprise policy.
- The setting `chat.editMode.hidden` (default `true`, policy `DeprecatedEditModeHidden`) is now vestigial — it is read in only one place (`chatNewActions.ts`), and the mode picker doesn't read it at all (it uses `agentModeDisabledViaPolicy`).

This issue tracks **fully removing `chat.editMode.hidden` and not allowing Edit to be selected from the agent dropdown**.

Ref https://code.visualstudio.com/updates/v1_110#_new-deprecations-in-this-release

## Design notes

- When Agent is disabled by enterprise policy, the picker can still fallback to the Edit experience.

title: How to Fix VS Code Menu Bar Missing (terminal, help)
document:
How to Fix VS Code Menu Bar Missing (terminal, help)

When I launch VS Code, the full menu bar (`File`, `Edit`, `Selection`, `View`, `Go`, `Run`, `Terminal`, `Help`) is displayed correctly for a few seconds. Once the UI finishes loading, the menu bar is recalculated and the `Terminal` and `Help` entries disappear from the visible area.

This behavior occurs consistently on every startup. It appears to be a layout issue where the title bar or Command Center expands after initialization, reducing the available space for menu items. Instead of adapting or shrinking appropriately, the rightmost menu entries are pushed out of view.

Key observations:

* The issue occurs immediately after startup without any manual window resizing.
* It happens regardless of the initial window size.
* The menu items are visible briefly during startup, then disappear once the UI finishes loading.
* This suggests the menu itself is being rendered correctly initially and is later affected by a layout recalculation.

### Video Evidence

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fc1b8c30-1097-4016-ba09-3d3b4d042674

### Comparison

On the same machine, VS Code for the Web (`https://vscode.dev`) displays the complete menu bar correctly and does not exhibit this behavior.

How to Fix VS Code Menu Bar Missing (terminal, help)

title: Feature Request for Markdown file review windows
document:
Feature Request for Markdown file review windows

Why don't we develop a Markdown review window similar to the one found in the Antigravity IDE? With the growing popularity of "vibe coding," the need to frequently review and revise Markdown files within projects has increased, making a high-quality review window particularly important.
In this window, we can quickly add comments and review the changes AI makes to our Markdown files, rather than constantly relying on line numbers to tell Copilot what modifications we want to make to a specific line; I believe this will significantly improve the user experience.

title: Custom agent frontmatter is not automatically loaded on VSCode startup.
document:
Custom agent frontmatter is not automatically loaded on VSCode startup.

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: Built-in
- VS Code Version: 1.124.2 (Universal)
- OS Version: macOS 26.5.1 (25F80)
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): agent
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): GPT-5.4 (but happens with other models as well)

Problem:

After restarting VS Code, selecting a custom agent does not reliably load the metadata configured in its frontmatter into the chat UI.

The chat window keeps using the model and tools that were already active in the chat session instead of switching to the model and tools configured for the selected agent. Agents with `user-invocable: false` are still shown in the UI.
It seems that this does not only affect the UI, but the whole chat functionality, in particular this also affects subagents.

The configuration only starts working after opening the corresponding agent file and saving it again without making any changes. After that, the configured model and tools are applied correctly and non user invocable agents disappear.

0. Enable `allowInvocationsFromSubagents`
1. Create one or more custom agents and subagents with frontmatter that defines description, model, and tools.
2. Restart VS Code.
3. Open the chat view and select one of the configured agents.
4. Observe that the chat UI does not update to the model and tools defined in the selected agent's frontmatter.
5. Ask the agent to trigger a subagent.
6. Observe in Debug View that the subagent request uses the model and tools currently active in the chat window instead of the ones configured in the selected subagent.
7. Open the corresponding `.agent.md` file.
8. Save the file again without making any changes.
9. Observe that after saving, the agent metadata is loaded and the configured model/tools are then applied.

Expected:

Selecting an agent or subagent after restarting VS Code should immediately apply the frontmatter-defined description, model, and tools.

Actual:

After restart, custom agent frontmatter is not automatically loaded. Easily visible by non user-invocable agents still appearing in the UI, but also causing sideeffects to agents that are selected and subagents that are indirectly called.

Logs:

This log snippet shows that the description of the `research` and `scout` agents was not loaded:

(Taken from Debug Chat Panel - subAgent Tool call - Requests - System)

In other logs, for example `tool/runSubagent-...`, the model used for the subagent does not match the model configured in the subagent frontmatter.

Example frontmatter configuration:

Observed in logs:

The same logs also show that the tools used are the ones active in the chat window at that moment, not the tools configured in the subagent frontmatter.

title: tcsh/csh shell integration
document:
tcsh/csh shell integration

### Summary

The [VS Code Terminal Shell Integration](vscode-file://vscode-app/c:/Users/matanco1/AppData/Local/Programs/Microsoft%20VS%20Code/6a44c352bd/resources/app/out/vs/code/electron-browser/workbench/workbench.html) documentation currently lists the following supported shells:

* Linux/macOS: bash, fish, pwsh, zsh
* Windows: Git Bash, pwsh

**tcsh** (and its ancestor csh) are not supported.

This is a significant gap for users working in Hardware companies/EDA/HPC environments, academic institutions, and legacy Unix systems where tcsh is the default and often mandatory shell.

---

### The main issue

The lack of shell integration with tcsh/csh causing the agents to:
1. Do extra iterations when using shell tool (exec), using wrong syntax (EOF, 2>&1, multiline strings etc.)
2. Not communicate with the environment correctly - not knowing if command finished and the exit status
3. High token spending and high costs.

title: "Reserved for response" ignores `maxOutputTokens` for extension-contributed models (hard-coded to 8192)
document:
"Reserved for response" ignores `maxOutputTokens` for extension-contributed models (hard-coded to 8192)

# Problem

The Copilot context window widget shows **"Reserved for response"** as ~8192 tokens for all extension-contributed models, regardless of the `maxOutputTokens` value declared by the provider.

**Expected:** "Reserved for response" reflects the actual `maxOutputTokens` reported by the `LanguageModelChatProvider` (e.g., 32768, 65536, etc.).

**Actual:** Always ~8192 tokens, making the reservation bar a tiny sliver even for models configured with large output budgets.

## Root Cause

In the Copilot Chat extension's internal endpoint wrapper, `ExtensionContributedChatEndpoint` ignores the provider's declared `maxOutputTokens` and returns a hard-coded value:

**File:** `extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/vscode-node/extChatEndpoint.ts`

This value flows into `toolCallingLoop.ts` where it's used as the `outputBuffer` for usage reporting:

Which then renders in `chatContextUsageWidget.ts` as the "Reserved for response" bar.

## Why This Matters

- Extension providers **do** report `maxOutputTokens` on `LanguageModelChatInformation` — but Copilot's internal wrapper never reads it
- For models with large output budgets (32K–128K), the UI misleads users into thinking only ~8K is reserved
- This is particularly misleading for reasoning/thinking models where output can legitimately be very large
- The `maxOutputTokens` value **is** used correctly for the actual vLLM `max_tokens` parameter — only the **display** is wrong

## Context

We've already solved the related issues in our extension (vLLM-2-Copilot):
- ✅ Token usage reporting via `LanguageModelDataPart` with `'usage'` MIME type and snake_case keys (not blocked by `isApiUsage()`)
- ✅ `prompt_tokens` / `completion_tokens` / `total_tokens` displayed correctly in the context widget
- ✅ `provideTokenCount` works for prompt budgeting

The only remaining gap is the `outputBuffer` / "Reserved for response" display.

## Proposed Fix

Option A (preferred): Read `maxOutputTokens` from the model information when constructing `ExtensionContributedChatEndpoint`:

Option B: Expose `maxOutputTokens` on the `LanguageModelChat` API surface so Copilot can access it without reaching into provider internals.

## Environment

- VS Code: 1.120+

title: Accessibility: Webview body element has invalid role="document" (WCAG 4.1.2 violation)
document:
Accessibility: Webview body element has invalid role="document" (WCAG 4.1.2 violation)

- VS Code Version: 1.124.2 (Universal)
- OS Version: MacOS 26.5.1 (25F80) and Windows 11

1. Create any VS Code extension with a webview (or use any existing extension with webviews)
2. Open the webview in VS Code
3. Open browser DevTools (Help > Toggle Developer Tools)
4. Inspect the webview iframe's inner document
5. Observe the `` element has `role="document"` attribute

**Expected Behaviour:**
The `` element should not have an explicit ARIA role. The body element's implicit semantic role is already understood by assistive technologies.

**Actual Behaviour:**
VS Code's webview infrastructure automatically adds `role="document"` to the body element of all webviews.

**Impact:**
- **WCAG Violation**: 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (Level A) 
- **Reference**: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/name-role-value.html
- **Assistive Technology Impact**: Screen readers like JAWS may misinterpret the structural context of the entire webview, causing incorrect navigation behaviour.
- **Scope**: Affects all extensions using webviews and all users relying on screen readers

**Proposed Fix:**
Remove the `role="document"` attribute from webview body elements. The body element should rely on its implicit semantic role for proper accessibility.

title: Feature request: Display cache_write_price in the Cost column of the Models management UI
document:
Feature request: Display cache_write_price in the Cost column of the Models management UI

The **Cost (Credits per 1M Tokens)** column in the Models management UI currently displays **Input**, **Cache** (read), and **Output** costs. However, the CAPI `token_prices` response also includes a `cache_write_price` field that is not surfaced anywhere in the UI.

**Example CAPI response fragment:**

**Requested feature:**

Add a **"Cache write"** entry alongside the existing cost items (In, Cache, Out) in the Cost column so that users can see the full per-model pricing breakdown directly in the UI.

- Show `cache_write_price` (and its long-context variant) from the CAPI response in the same format as the other cost fields (e.g. "Cache write: 625").
- Include it in hover tooltips (e.g. "Cache write: 625 credits per 1M tokens").
- When the value is missing or null, display a consistent fallback matching the behavior for other absent costs.
- Support both default and long-context pricing rows in the model picker hover table.

**Why this matters:**

Cache write cost can be significantly higher than cache read cost (e.g. 625 vs 50 credits). Without this information in the UI, users cannot fully evaluate model pricing and may underestimate actual usage costs. The data is already returned by the API — it just needs to be displayed.

**Screenshot of current Cost column (Cache write is missing):**

title: GitHub authentication callback fails after browser authorization ("No auth flow succeeded"), preventing Marketplace access
document:
GitHub authentication callback fails after browser authorization ("No auth flow succeeded"), preventing Marketplace access

## #VS Code Version

ElectronBuildId: 14159160

## OS

Windows 11 Home 23H2

## Issue Type

Bug

## Description

I am unable to sign in to access the Extensions Marketplace.

The GitHub authentication flow starts normally. My browser opens and I can reach the "Authorize Visual Studio Code" page. After selecting my GitHub account and authorizing Visual Studio Code, the browser redirects back to VS Code, but authentication never completes successfully.

VS Code reports that authorization is incomplete and eventually displays:

> No auth flow succeeded

As a result:

* Marketplace remains locked.
* Extensions cannot be searched or installed.
* GitHub Copilot commands are unavailable.
* Authentication repeatedly loops.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open VS Code.
2. Open the Extensions view.
3. Click "Sign in to access Marketplace".
4. Browser opens GitHub authentication.
5. Select GitHub account and click "Authorize Visual Studio Code".
6. Browser redirects back to VS Code.
7. Authentication does not complete and eventually reports "No auth flow succeeded".

## Expected Result

Authentication completes successfully and Marketplace access is granted.

## Actual Result

Authentication fails with "No auth flow succeeded" and Marketplace remains inaccessible.

## Attempted Fixes

* Reinstalled VS Code.
* Deleted `%APPDATA%\Code\User\globalStorage`.
* Reloaded the window.
* Used Chrome as the browser.
* Repeated authorization multiple times.
* Browser login succeeds, but VS Code does not appear to process the callback successfully.

## Additional Notes

The issue is consistently reproducible. The browser authentication itself succeeds, but the callback to VS Code appears to fail, preventing Marketplace access and extension installation.

title: Webviews render unstyled in Firefox and Safari when resources are served cross-origin
document:
Webviews render unstyled in Firefox and Safari when resources are served cross-origin

- VS Code Version: reproduced on `main`
- OS: server: Linux; browser: Firefox / Safari

### Problem

When the webview content frame and its resources are served from different origins — the `vscode-resource` virtual host, the normal architecture (e.g. `*.vscode-webview.net` for content vs `*.vscode-resource.vscode-cdn.net` for resources) — extension stylesheets do not load in **Firefox** and **Safari**. The webview renders with no styling (toolbars/inputs appear as unstyled, stacked elements). **Chromium is unaffected.**

### Root cause

Extension `` elements have no `crossorigin` attribute, so the browser fetches them in **no-cors** mode. The webview service worker answers with an opaque cross-origin response. Firefox and Safari refuse to apply a stylesheet from an opaque response; Chromium is lenient and applies it anyway.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Serve the workbench so that webview resources resolve to a different origin than the content frame (i.e. `vscode-resource-base-authority` is set, as in a standard cross-origin webview deployment).
2. Open the workbench in Firefox or Safari.
3. Activate an extension that contributes a webview with an external stylesheet.
4. The webview content is unstyled. The same setup in Chrome is styled.

### Expected

Webview stylesheets load and apply in Firefox and Safari, as they do in Chromium.

### Notes

The service worker already serves these resources with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`, so the resources are CORS-eligible — the links just aren't requested in CORS mode. A fix is prepared (mark webview-resource stylesheet links `crossorigin` and re-fetch, Firefox/Safari only) and can be submitted as a PR.

CPU flags difference observed:
Ubuntu 24.04 flags end around:

Ubuntu 26.04 additionally exposes SME/SME2-related flags:

This looks like @vscode/vsce-sign or a dependency/runtime path is selecting an instruction based on exposed ARM CPU features and then hitting SIGILL under Ubuntu 26.04 in Lima on Apple M4.

title: Agents window ("Open in Agents"): preserve source subfolder as working directory when a session uses worktree isolation (monorepo)
document:
Agents window ("Open in Agents"): preserve source subfolder as working directory when a session uses worktree isolation (monorepo)

## Feature

> **Context:** this is specifically about the **Agents window** (the dedicated "Open in Agents" companion window), starting a session via **Copilot CLI** with **Worktree** isolation, launched from a **subfolder of a git repository** (a monorepo).

When such a session is started from a **subfolder of a git repository** and **worktree isolation** is selected, the session should keep that source subfolder as its **working directory** inside the newly created worktree — i.e. `/` — instead of resetting to the worktree (repository) root.

Ideally this is the default; at minimum, expose a setting such as `chat.agentHost.worktree.preserveWorkingDirectory` to opt in.

## Repository layout

User opens `packages/mobile-app` as the session folder and starts a session with worktree isolation. The worktree is created at the repository level:

## Flow comparison

| Step | Current behavior | Expected behavior |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1. Start session in | `packages/mobile-app` | `packages/mobile-app` |
| 2. Before worktree (Files panel) | shows subproject ✅ | shows subproject ✅ |
| 3. Instructions loaded (pre-worktree) | subproject instructions ✅ | subproject instructions ✅ |
| 4. Worktree created → re-anchored to | repo root ❌ | `/packages/mobile-app` ✅ |
| 5. `workingDirectory` becomes | `` ❌ | `/packages/mobile-app` ✅ |
| 6. Files panel (post-worktree) | whole repo ❌ | subproject ✅ |
| 7. Instructions loaded (post-worktree) | repo-root instructions ❌ | subproject instructions ✅ |

## Problem

git worktrees are **repository-level**: a new worktree is created at the repo root and contains the whole repository tree. With worktree isolation, VS Code re-anchors the session to the **worktree root** and drops the original subfolder path. The session folder becomes `{ root: , workingDirectory: }`, losing the relative subpath (e.g. `packages/mobile-app`).

This breaks two things that both key off the working directory:

1. **Custom instructions loading** — Instructions are discovered relative to the working directory (`/.github/copilot-instructions.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `.claude/CLAUDE.md`). Discovery does not walk **down** into subfolders, so after the worktree is created the agent loads the **repo-root** instructions and the **subproject's** instructions are never loaded.

2. **`worktreeCreated` tasks** — These are discovered from `/.vscode/tasks.json`, where `sessionFolder = gitRepository.workingDirectory ?? root`. That resolves to the worktree root, so a `tasks.json` in the subproject is never found.

Net effect: there is **no clean, non-invasive way** to keep a worktree-isolated session anchored to a subproject. Adding root-level instructions/tasks would affect **every** session in the monorepo, which is unacceptable for a large shared repo.

## Steps to reproduce

1. In the **Agents window** ("Open in Agents"), open a subfolder of a monorepo as the session folder (e.g. `packages/mobile-app`). The Files panel shows the subfolder; the subproject's instructions load.
2. Start a new **Copilot CLI** session with **Worktree** isolation.
3. After the worktree is created, the session is re-anchored to the **repository root** — Files panel shows the whole repo, working directory is the worktree root.

## Expected behavior

The session keeps the source subfolder as its working directory inside the new worktree (`/packages/mobile-app`). The subproject's instructions load automatically, per-subproject `worktreeCreated` tasks work, and no root-level config leaks into unrelated sessions.

## Proposed behavior

1. On worktree isolation from a subfolder, compute the relative subpath from the repo root and set `workingDirectory` to `/`.
2. Use that working directory consistently for both custom-instructions discovery and `worktreeCreated`/session task discovery.
3. If changing the default is risky, gate it behind `chat.agentHost.worktree.preserveWorkingDirectory` (default `true` when launched from a subfolder).

## Related issues

- #311148 — Agents App: Support multi-root workspaces with cross-repo change tracking and worktrees
- #316250 — Copilot CLI agent panel: git worktree creation path defaults to inside workspace folder

## Environment

- VS Code: 1.125.0 (also observed on 1.124)
- OS: macOS (Apple Silicon)

title: Graceful handover when AI credits are exhausted mid-task
document:
Graceful handover when AI credits are exhausted mid-task

**Problem**
When using GitHub Copilot agent mode in VS Code, the session can terminate abruptly when AI credits are exhausted.
Example messages:

This is particularly likely to occur mid-task during workflows such as multi-step refactoring or iterative agent workflows.

The assistant may stop in the middle of a task with no clear indication of what was completed. The user cannot easily determine what work remains. There is no guidance on where to pick up from

This is especially disruptive given the shift to usage-based billing, where hitting limits is now a normal and expected state rather than an edge case.

**Steps to Reproduce**

Open VS Code with GitHub Copilot enabled
Start a long-running Copilot chat or agent workflow such as:

"Refactor this repository and replace usage of library X with Y"

Allow the session to run until AI credits are exhausted
Observe that the session terminates with a quota message

**Expected Behavior**
Instead of stopping immediately, Copilot should provide a structured “handover summary” before terminating the session.

**Actual Behavior**
Copilot stops abruptly and shows a credit limit message.
Todo items and the displayed trace provide some high level sense of what was done vs what is outstanding.

**Proposed Solution**
Introduce a lightweight “handover phase” that triggers when the system detects that the session is about to end due to usage or credit limits.
This phase should generate a structured summary containing:

Completed work: A clear list of tasks or steps that were finished.

Work in progress: Tasks that were partially completed.

Interrupted step: A precise description of where execution stopped, including file names or operations if applicable.

Possibly it could include next steps.

This could be implemented using a small reserved token budget for termination scenarios

**Example Scenarios**
1. File processing interruption
Copilot is reading a file or set of files and stops mid-way
Expected: summary of which files or sections were processed, which were not, and which has been partially processed.
2. Refactoring workflow
Copilot is applying changes across multiple functions
Expected: list of files and functions already refactored, those yet to be refactored, and the file, function and line at which updating was interupted. 
3. Repository analysis
Copilot is scanning modules to detect issues
Expected: partial findings and modules not yet analyzed
4. Debugging session
Copilot is tracing a bug through multiple call paths
Expected: explored paths and suggested next debugging steps

**Why This Matters**
With usage-based billing, session termination due to limits is predictable and frequent.
A structured handover would:

 - reduce repeated work
 - improve trust in Copilot as a collaborator
 - make agent-style workflows more practical for longer tasks, particularly for users with enterprise limited token budgets

**Additional Context**
Current behavior feels like losing a collaborator mid-task without a handover.
A simple summary at termination would significantly improve continuity and usability.

title: Fcitx5 Hangul preedit text is discarded when clicking another editor position on native Wayland
document:
Fcitx5 Hangul preedit text is discarded when clicking another editor position on native Wayland

## Description

When using Fcitx5 Hangul in VS Code running natively on Wayland, active Hangul preedit text is discarded when I click another position in the editor.

The composed Korean text is displayed with an underline, indicating that it is still in the IME preedit state.

If I press Space before clicking, the text is committed and does not disappear. However, if I click another editor position while the underline is still visible, the entire preedit text disappears instead of being committed.

The issue does not occur when VS Code is launched through XWayland with:

The issue also does not occur in GNOME Text Editor under the same desktop session and with the same Fcitx5 Hangul input method.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Log in to a GNOME Wayland session.

2. Use Fcitx5 with the Hangul input method.

3. Start VS Code normally using its native Wayland backend.

4. Open any editable file.

5. Type a Korean word, for example:

 

6. Do not press Space, Enter, or an arrow key. Keep the text in the underlined preedit state.

7. Click another position in the editor.

## Actual Behavior

The underlined Hangul preedit text is discarded and disappears.

## Expected Behavior

The active preedit text should be committed before the caret moves to the clicked position.

This is how it behaves in GNOME Text Editor and when VS Code is running through XWayland.

## Comparison

* Native Wayland VS Code: issue occurs
* VS Code launched with `--ozone-platform=x11`: issue does not occur
* GNOME Text Editor under Wayland: issue does not occur
* Pressing Space before clicking: issue does not occur

## Environment

* OS: Fedora Linux 44

* Desktop: GNOME 50

* Session type: Wayland

* Input method framework: Fcitx5

* Input method engine: Fcitx5 Hangul

* VS Code version: 1.124.2

* Fcitx5 version: 5.1.19

* Fcitx5 package versions:
fcitx5-5.1.19-1.fc44.x86_64
fcitx5-hangul-5.1.9-1.fc44.x86_64

## Extensions

The issue also occurs when VS Code is started with all extensions disabled:

## Additional Information

The issue appears to be specific to the native Wayland IME path.

A screen recording is attached showing:

1. The Hangul preedit text disappearing in native Wayland VS Code
2. The correct behavior in VS Code running through XWayland
3. The correct behavior in GNOME Text Editor

title: Add a font family setting for code blocks in Markdown Preview
document:
Add a font family setting for code blocks in Markdown Preview

Add a font family setting for code blocks in Markdown Preview, so that a user can choose fonts for code blocks in Markdown Preview different from fonts used in code editors.

This feature is required because some users like to write tree graphs or other figures in Markdown code blocks, which require characters to be aligned. This is often not a problem as long as a user use a monospaced font. However, a user from China, Japan, Korea, and some other countries may want to write characters of their language, e.g., Chinese characters and Hangul letters, and then they'll have a trouble—those characters are too wide to be aligned with English letters.

A solution is to use instead a carefully designed “monospaced” CJK font in which a Chinese character is of exactly two English letters' width, so users can still adjust their graphs to make lines aligned. However, such kind of fonts usually “squeeze” English letters, so they looks not so good especially when writing code making up of merely ASCII characters. Since VS Code renders Markdown code blocks with `editor.fontFamily` fonts, a user who wants to align lines in graphs in Markdown code blocks must look at those squeezed English characters when writing code, which is very uncomfortable. A separate font setting can solve this problem.

I noticed that there's a settings `markdown.preview.fontFamily` that controls the font family used in the main text of Markdown Preview. I expect there to be another setting like `markdown.preview.codeBlockFontFamily`.

title: Agents window: Allow customization of branch name prefix for worktree branches
document:
Agents window: Allow customization of branch name prefix for worktree branches

### Description

1. Open VS Code
2. Open Agents window
3. Begin a new session by selecting Worktree isolation (via the dropdown to the bottom right of the Copilot new session dialog), entering a prompt, and submitting the prompt.

**Expected behavior:**
Copilot honors instructions in my AGENTS.md file for how new branches should be named (e.g. by prefixing branches with the name of the current user, like `/`).

**Actual behavior:**
Every branch created by the Agents window in Worktree isolation mode begins with `agents/`. Copilot tells me that this prefix is hardcoded in VS code.

title: Run in terminal tool returns 'Failed to retrieve command output' when output exceeds scrollback
document:
Run in terminal tool returns 'Failed to retrieve command output' when output exceeds scrollback

## Problem

When a command's output exceeds the terminal's scrollback buffer (`terminal.integrated.scrollback`, default 1000 lines), the `run_in_terminal` tool returns "Failed to retrieve command output" instead of returning whatever is available in the buffer.

## Expected behavior

The tool should return the full scrollback buffer contents (i.e. the last N lines that fit in scrollback) rather than failing entirely. Even partial output is more useful than an error message.

## Repro

1. Set `terminal.integrated.scrollback` to a low value (e.g. 100)
2. Have the agent run a command that produces more lines than the scrollback (e.g. `seq 1 5000`)
3. Tool result says "Failed to retrieve command output"

## Notes

This is separate from the large output truncation/file-write handling in #321640 — that PR handles the case where output is successfully retrieved but exceeds the inline size limit. This issue is about output never being retrieved at all.

title: MCP tool input validation rejects anyOf schemas - blocks Union-typed parameters
document:
MCP tool input validation rejects anyOf schemas - blocks Union-typed parameters

**VS Code Version:** 1.124.2 (6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c, x64, Windows)

## Problem

When an MCP server exposes a tool with a parameter typed as a union (e.g. `Union[str, dict]` in Python/Pydantic), the resulting JSON Schema uses `anyOf`:

VS Code's MCP client-side validation rejects **all** inputs to these parameters with:

The tool call never reaches the MCP server. The same tool works correctly with Claude Desktop's MCP client.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create an MCP server (Python + FastMCP) with a tool that has a `Union[str, dict]` parameter:
 
2. Call the tool from VS Code's agent/chat with a dict value for `fields_json`
3. Observe the `anyOf` validation error — the call never reaches the server

## Expected Behavior

The MCP client should pass the input through to the server when it matches **any** branch of the `anyOf` schema (a dict matches `{"type": "object"}`).

## Actual Behavior

The client rejects the input at validation time, reporting it doesn't match any branch.

## Workaround

Change all union-typed parameters to `Any`, which generates `{}` (unconstrained) in JSON Schema. This sacrifices type documentation to bypass the validation bug.

title: Accessibility: Built-in VSCode webviews  fails with WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.2 Page Titled (Level A) - Missing <title> element in <head> element
document:
Accessibility: Built-in VSCode webviews  fails with WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.2 Page Titled (Level A) - Missing <title> element in <head> element

**WCAG Requirement:**

https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/page-titled

**Steps to Reproduce:**

Open VSCODE
Select Extension
Run accessibility checker on it

**Rationale:**
This requirement ensures descriptive titles are provided. Descriptive titles help all users orient themselves when navigating through content or when moving between multiple applications or documents.

Screen readers announce titles before other content. If titles are missing, screen reader users must explore more content to determine the purpose. Conversely, if titles are identical for multiple pages, or repeat the same information before providing unique information, a user will hear repetitive content before understanding context.

The title should:

Succinctly identify the subject
Make sense when read out of context, for example by a screen reader, in a site map, list of search results, window title, or on a browser tab title
If the page or document belongs to a collection, the title should:

List the most unique identifying information first
Be unique within the collection

title: Context window indicator shows higher fill immediately after compacting conversation than actual
document:
Context window indicator shows higher fill immediately after compacting conversation than actual

ElectronBuildId: 14159160

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Have an ongoing session that fills a noticeable portion of the context window
2. Trigger a compact of the conversation (button or `/compact`)
3. Observe the context window indicator immediately after compacting completes.

## Expected Behavior
Immediately after a compact, the indicator should reflect the reduced context size, showing a lower fill level than before compacting.

## Actual Behavior
Immediately after compacting, the indicator shows a 2x higher fill level than before the operation. The value is only corrected after the conversation continues, at which point the indicator updates to the correct, lower size.

## Impact
Misleading context-usage feedback right after a compact, which is precisely when the user expects to see freed-up space. This can cause users to believe the compact had no effect or made things worse.

Video:
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6c29f74d-a3ef-4d53-8f5b-b9260b542ea9

title: Add a setting `forceFirstExecutionInSandbox`
document:
Add a setting `forceFirstExecutionInSandbox`

* by default it is `false`
* when set to `true`, we always programmatically first try `run_in_terminal` inside the sandbox and present the "elevation" to the user only when the command fails.
* implementation:
 * don't touch the tool description
 * when the LLM uses `requestUnsandboxed: true`
 * inside the `run_in_terminal` we actually run it inside the sandbox
 * we ignore `requestUnsandboxed: true` ==> if the exit code is 0 then we continue
 * if the exit code is non zero, then we show the "Allow" question to the user.
