vs code 1.124.2 breaks extensions

- VS Code Version: 1.124.2
- OS Version: Windows 10 64bit

## Description

hi, i've noticed that some extensions stop working when upgrading to vs code `1.124.2`. one of these extensions is `shardulm94.trailing-spaces`. this extension highlights and trims whitespaces on save and since upgrading to `1.124.2` the highlighting capability together with trimming stopped working. if you look in the extension host output when pressing `ctrl + s` you will get the following output:

some people have posted issues on the extension's github page but the project seems to be abandoned. can someone have a look at the changes that are causing this? the last version of vs code where it worked is `1.122.1` and the problem occurs both in `1.123` and `1.124` in all patches.

hope this helps,
thanks

## Steps to Reproduce

- download latest version of vs code
- download `Trailing Spaces` extension
- open a text file
- add some white spaces at the end of any line in the file
- the white spaces should be highlighted in red and when pressing `ctrl + s` the spaces should be trimmed
- this doesn't happen; instead you get the stacktrace in the extension host output window

title: Chat: system notification XML tags rendered raw in conversation
document:
Chat: system notification XML tags rendered raw in conversation

## Problem

When the Copilot agent runtime sends system notifications (e.g., background agent completion), the `` XML tags are rendered as raw text in the chat conversation instead of being hidden or styled appropriately.

## Expected behavior

System notifications wrapped in `...` tags should either be:
- Hidden from the visible conversation (they are metadata for the model, not the user)
- Or rendered as a styled, dismissible notification element

## Actual behavior

The raw XML tags appear verbatim in the chat output, e.g.:

## Context

These notifications are injected by the agent runtime as non-billable system messages to inform the model about async events (shell command completions, background agent results). They are not intended to be user-facing content.

title: Add Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite to BYOK known models whitelist
document:
Add Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite to BYOK known models whitelist

### Summary

Request adding `models/gemini-3.1-flash-lite` to the BYOK known models whitelist at https://main.vscode-cdn.net/extensions/copilotChat.json so it can be used via the native Gemini provider with full thinking + tool calling support.

### Motivation

Google's free tier Gemini API offers 500 RPD (Requests Per Day) for `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`, which is significantly higher than other models. This makes it the most practical choice for developers using BYOK with the free tier, enabling sustained coding sessions without hitting rate limits.

- Rate Limits: https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/rate-limits
- Model Specs: https://docs.cloud.google.com/gemini-enterprise-agent-platform/models/gemini/3-1-flash-lite

### Current Workaround Limitations

1. **`customendpoint` vendor**: Can connect via Google's OpenAI-compatible endpoint, but fails with thinking models due to missing `thought_signature` support (required by Gemini thinking models for function calling).

2. **Native `gemini` vendor**: Correctly handles `thought_signature` but filters models against the remote whitelist (`copilotChat.json`), which currently does not include `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`.

### Current Gemini Whitelist

The current `Gemini` section in https://main.vscode-cdn.net/extensions/copilotChat.json:

Click to expand

title: Accessibility: Built-in VSCode webviews fails for Name, Role, Value (Level A) SC 4.1.2 (WCAG 2.2)
document:
Accessibility: Built-in VSCode webviews fails for Name, Role, Value (Level A) SC 4.1.2 (WCAG 2.2)

**WCAG Requirement:**

https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/name-role-value

**Steps to Reproduce:**

1. Open VSCODE
2. Select Extension 
3. Run accessibility checker on it

**Description:**
When we run accessiblity checker on any built in VSCODE webviews, it fails for "The ARIA role 'document' is not valid for the element "

Below is the example for built-in webview in VSCODE:

**Rationale:**
The intent of this success criterion is to ensure that Assistive Technologies (AT) can gather appropriate information about, activate (or set) and keep up to date on the status of user interface controls in the content.

When standard controls from accessible technologies are used, this process is straightforward. If the user interface elements are used according to specification the conditions of this provision will be met. (See examples of Success Criterion 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value below)

If custom controls are created, however, or interface elements are programmed (in code or script) to have a different role and/or function than usual, then additional measures need to be taken to ensure that the controls provide important and appropriate information to assistive technologies and allow themselves to be controlled by assistive technologies.

What roles and states are appropriate to convey to assistive technology will depend on what the control represents. Specifics about such information are defined by other specifications, such as [WAI-ARIA](https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria/), or the relevant platform standards. Another factor to consider is whether there is sufficient [accessibility support](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/name-role-value#dfn-accessibility-supported) with assistive technologies to convey the information as specified.

A particularly important state of a user interface control is whether or not it has focus. The focus state of a control can be programmatically determined, and notifications about change of focus are sent to user agents and assistive technology. Other examples of user interface control states are whether or not a checkbox or radio button has been selected, or whether a collapsible tree view or accordion is expanded or collapsed.

title: Agents window: auxiliary bar shows Files instead of Changes when changes exist / Files pane hidden
document:
Agents window: auxiliary bar shows Files instead of Changes when changes exist / Files pane hidden

## Problem

In the Agents window, when the right (auxiliary) sidebar is opened, it sometimes shows the **Files** panel instead of **Changes** in cases where the user never chose Files:

- An **untitled** session whose workspace has changes always shows Files (never consults `hasChanges`).
- After the Files pane has been **hidden/unpinned**, it gets force-reopened.

> **Note:** Persisting the user's *explicit* auxiliary-bar choice (e.g. clicking the Files tab) across session switches and restarts is intended behavior and is preserved — it is not part of this bug.

## Root cause

The logic lives in `LayoutController._syncAuxiliaryBarVisibility()` in `src/vs/sessions/contrib/layout/browser/sessionLayoutController.ts`.

Original decision order:

1. Bail if no session / no workspace.
2. If the session is **untitled** → always open Files (never consults `hasChanges`). ← bug
3. If a `savedState.auxiliaryBarActiveViewContainerId` is set → open that container unconditionally, even if its pane was hidden/unpinned. ← bug for the hidden-pane case
4. Else: `hasChanges ? Changes : Files`.

For the hidden-pane case: hiding the Files tab only unpins the composite. `openViewContainer(...)` → `openComposite(...)` does not consult pinned/hidden state, and the Files container is registered with `hideIfEmpty: false`, so nothing keeps it hidden once it is opened programmatically.

## Repro

**Untitled session with changes:**
1. In an untitled session whose workspace has changes, open the auxiliary bar.
2. ❌ Files is shown (the `isUntitled` branch short-circuits before `hasChanges`). Expected: Changes.

**Hidden Files pane:**
1. Right-click the Files tab → Hide.
2. Trigger anything that flips `hasChanges`, or switch sessions.
3. ❌ Files reappears. Expected: it stays hidden; the bar falls back to Changes.

## Expected

- Untitled sessions with changes should prefer **Changes**.
- If the user has hidden/unpinned the Files pane, it should not be force-opened (fall back to Changes).
- A fresh session (no saved choice) with changes should default to **Changes**.
- The user's explicit auxiliary-bar choice should be remembered across session switches and restarts.

title: read_file reads only 2000 characters
document:
read_file reads only 2000 characters

After the change last year, the read_file reads only 2000 characters without any way how to change this limit: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/26129ae2c53b97ea9dd4f95bd695f255adaaa0f7/extensions/copilot/src/extension/tools/node/readFileTool.tsx#L77

 While I understand the reasoning behind this change, there are valid scenarios where larger lines are needed.

These scenarios include (but are not limited) to output of important MCP tools such as Azure DevOps MCP where larger details, user stories or implementation requirements often have more than 2000 characters. The MCP returns JSON where the content of every fields is one line and truncating details causing wrong context to be passed to copilot.

There are workarounds, but they are not easy to implement (e.g. adding postprocessing task to MCP to split the response from string to array of strings to split long text to multiple lines).

I think this is a bug as the idea behind this limitation was unstructured text (long tokens) or binary files, but structured text returned from MCPs was overlooked.

title: Bug: Prompt cache expires during active agent sessions, causing 10x cost spikes on gaps > 5 min
document:
Bug: Prompt cache expires during active agent sessions, causing 10x cost spikes on gaps > 5 min

## Bug Description

VS Code Copilot advertises prompt caching as a cost-saving feature. However, the cache
**silently expires during active agent sessions** whenever two consecutive LLM calls are
more than ~5 minutes apart .

The next LLM call after any such gap pays **100% of input_tokens** at full price:
- System prompt + all instructions + skills + workspace context
- Entire conversation history
- All previous tool call results

This is typicallydozens of k-Tokens - and can nor range up to 1 M Token! — charged at full input price instead of
the cached price (~10%). The user receives no warning, no indicator, and has no way
to know the cache has expired.

**Expected behavior:** The cache stays warm for the duration of an active agent session.

**Actual behavior:** The cache expires silently mid-session, billing the user for full
input tokens on every call after any inter-call gap > ~5 min.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Start an agent session with large workspace context (>50k tokens loaded via instructions/skills)
2. Trigger a tool call that has a natural wait time > 5 min: `run_in_terminal` with a build, test suite, or `npm install`
3. After the terminal command completes, inspect the next LLM call via agent debug logs
4. Observe: `input_tokens_no_cache` equals the full context size — cache was not used

### This is NOT a "user is idle" problem

The agent is actively working. The machine is not blocked. Examples of legitimate gaps:

- `run_in_terminal` waiting for a 10-min build
- `get_terminal_output` polling a long test suite
- Large `read_file` or `semantic_search` on a big codebase
- `vscode_askQuestions` waiting for user input (secondary scenario)

and:
- `manage_todo_list` as an indicator for: end of a long session 
it comes very often with a 100% spike if input tokens!!

---

## Two Distinct Cost Spike Patterns (from debug log analysis)

### Pattern 1 — Inter-call gap spikes

Any two consecutive LLM calls with `time_delta > 300s` → cache miss → full input price on next call.

Visible in Agent debug logs as isolated high-cost turns after terminal/build waits.
cached input tokens = 0
new input tokens: same height as previous cached ones

`manage_todo_list` is a pure bookkeeping tool — no code execution, no user output.
It has **zero inherent cost** by design.

Yet it consistently shows the **highest `input_tokens_no_cache` values** in session data,
because it is called in connection with long sessions where:
1. The context is already fully loaded (large `input_tokens` total)
2. A prior long session already expired the cache
3. Now, the next call after manage_todo_list shows the spike!

### Pattern s — VS Ask Questions

the same pattern of sudden drops of cached input tokes together with similar amount of **new** input tokens is visible after VS Ask Question tool calls:

the tools is waiting for User input. if the user waits 301 s ++, they have to pay 10x for the input tokens.

---

## Proposed Fix

### Primary: Cache Keepalive Ping

While the agent is waiting between tool calls (terminal running, file reads, etc.),
VS Code Copilot sends a minimal no-op LLM request every N minutes:

This resets the TTL at near-zero cost (1–2 output tokens; input served from cache ≈ 10% price).
The ping is **completely invisible** to the user — no message appears in chat.

Note: This fix requires no changes to the API contract, no new user-facing UI,
and no changes to tool definitions. It is purely an internal timing concern.

### Proposed Setting

**Trigger condition:**
> Any time elapsed since last LLM call exceeds `intervalSeconds` AND total pings
> since last real user-triggered call is less than `maxPings`.

This covers both "agent waiting for terminal" AND **vscode_askQuestions blocking** with a
single mechanism — no special-casing needed.

max Pings = 2-3:
2-3 pings x 5min -> should be enough for the user to detect the vscode_askQuestions dialog.
AND: significant cost saver compare to 10x.

### Secondary (Optional): Traffic light
maxPings=3.
With every bridging LLM call, a new notification is sent to the user - with a traffic light running from green to red.
After red traffic light, no additional bridging calls are sent - and the user has another 299s till they have to pay 10x for the next LLM call.

---

## Impact Estimate

Based on session analysis across ~50 agent sessions (VS Code debug logs):

| Scenario | Cost relative to baseline |
|---|---|
| Dense work, no pauses > 5 min | 1× (cache always warm) |
| One gap > 5 min mid-session | 3–5× for that turn |
| Long session with 10+ bridging calls after a gap | 8–15× cumulative |
| With keepalive ping (proposed) | ~1.1× (3 ping calls max) |

**Estimated reduction: 30–60% per-session LLM cost** for typical interactive workflows
involving builds, file reads, or multi-step agent tasks.

---

## Evidence & Reproducibility

The correlation between `time_delta_to_prev > 300s` and `input_tokens_no_cache` spikes
is statistically clear and reproducible from VS Code Copilot debug logs.

## Affected Tools (highest frequency in gap-induced spikes)

| Tool | Reason for high frequency |
|---|---|
| `manage_todo_list` | Called after long coding sessions - cahce is expired |
| `run_in_terminal` | Long-running commands create inter-call gaps |
| `vscode_askQuestions` | Blocks on user think-time |

title: Extension host OOM crash: glob.ts compiles an arbitrarily long watcher pattern into a pathological regex (regExp.test blows the V8 heap)
document:
Extension host OOM crash: glob.ts compiles an arbitrarily long watcher pattern into a pathological regex (regExp.test blows the V8 heap)

- VS Code Version: VS Code 1.124.2 (commit 6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c)
- OS Version: macOS 26.5.1 (arm64)

Note: local username scrubbed to `userx` in all captured strings.

## Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: No (end-to-end), but the crash is reproducible WITH extensions disabled

The end-to-end crash is *triggered* by a language extension (gopls / vscode-go) supplying a bad watch pattern, so disabling all extensions removes the trigger. **However, the defect itself is in VS Code core** (`vs/base/common/glob.ts`) and is reproducible with no extensions at all by feeding the attached `pattern` directly to the `glob` API (see Steps to Reproduce) — `glob.parse(pattern)` + a single `match(path)` OOMs the host. So this is **not** an "extension bug" to forward to a publisher: the core defect is that `glob.ts` compiles an arbitrary, untrusted, multi-kilobyte string into a regex and runs it on every file event with no length or complexity guard, so a single malformed pattern from *any* extension can OOM-kill the entire extension host.

- VS Code Version: 1.124.2 (6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c)
- OS Version: macOS 26.5.1, arm64
- Insiders: the faulting code path (`glob.ts` `parseRegExp` / `regExp.test`) is unchanged on `main`, so this is expected to reproduce on the latest Insiders as well.

## Summary

A file-watcher **glob pattern** that is not a real glob — in my case the multi-line error output of a failed `go list` (~6.5 KB, dozens of `missing go.sum entry ...` lines) — gets compiled by `glob.ts` into an **8,691-character regex** containing **459 `[/\\]` path-separator character classes**. On the very next file-system event, `extHostFileSystemEventService.$onFileEvent` runs `regExp.test(path)` against that regex, which exhibits catastrophic backtracking / unbounded memory growth and **OOM-crashes the extension host**.

Because this happens inside the extension host, it silently kills any in-flight extension work. In my case it repeatedly cancelled in-flight GitHub Copilot Chat requests (they surfaced as `"Canceled"`), which is how I found it — but the editor-level defect is independent of Copilot.

The root problem is **defense-in-depth**: `glob.ts` trusts the pattern string completely. A malformed pattern should fail safe (be rejected / produce a non-matching matcher), never OOM the host.

## Steps to Reproduce

I have preserved the exact captured `pattern` and compiled `regExp` as a minimal repro. The shortest faithful reproduction:

How it arises organically (real-world trigger):

1. Open a Go workspace where `go list ./...` fails (e.g. missing `go.sum` entries for an imported module).
2. gopls / vscode-go surfaces that **failure string** and it ends up registered as a **file-watch glob pattern** (a 6.5 KB multi-line string — clearly not a glob).
3. `glob.ts` compiles it into the pathological regex above.
4. Save any `.go` file → `$onFileEvent` → `regExp.test(path)` → extension host OOM.

## Smoking gun (extension-host debugger)

Running `Developer: Show Running Extensions` with a profile attached and reproducing the crash, the debugger paused with the banner **"PAUSED BEFORE OUT OF MEMORY EXCEPTION"**. Faulting frame and call stack (top-down):

At the breakpoint:

- `path` = the `.go` file being saved (a path under my workspace)
- `regExp` = an **8,691-character** regex
- `pattern` = the **6,562-character** source string it was compiled from
- the regex contains **459 `[/\]`** separator classes

(The attached sanitized repro reproduces the same shape at 6,512 / 8,628 bytes and 495 separator classes.)

The `pattern` begins (sanitized — real package names replaced):

The compiled `regExp` begins (sanitized):

## Expected behavior

`glob.ts` should fail safe against pathological / non-glob patterns and never be able to OOM-crash the extension host:

- Reject or truncate patterns above a sane length (a 6.5 KB watch glob is never legitimate).
- Cap regex complexity (e.g. number of separator classes / alternations) and fall back to a literal/non-matching matcher when exceeded.
- Optionally guard `regExp.test` with a complexity or step budget.

A malformed watch pattern from an extension should at worst log a warning and not match — it must not be able to take down the host.

## Actual behavior

`glob.ts` compiles the 6.5 KB string into an 8,691-char regex; the first `regExp.test(path)` on a file event causes catastrophic backtracking and OOMs the extension host, silently killing all in-flight extension work.

## Attachments

Two files reproduce it deterministically (paths **sanitized**; structure/size preserved):

- `pattern.txt` — the ~6.5 KB non-glob source string (failed `go list` output)
- `regExp.txt` — the ~8.6 KB compiled pathological regex

(Drag these two files into the GitHub issue before submitting. They are sanitized: the local username is `userx` and all internal package paths are generic placeholders — only the failed-`go list` **structure** remains.)

## Notes on the upstream trigger (separate issue, FYI only)

The *source* of the bad pattern is gopls / vscode-go registering a failed `go list` error string as a watch glob. That is a second, separate bug I may file against `golang/vscode-go`. **This** issue is specifically about VS Code core (`glob.ts`) not being robust against such a pattern — that hardening protects against any extension (Go, .NET, or otherwise) leaking an oversized string into a watch pattern.

title: Remove built-in Copilot code review from SCM view and code selection
document:
Remove built-in Copilot code review from SCM view and code selection

### Description

Remove the built-in Copilot code review feature offered in two surfaces:

1. **SCM view** — Review of staged, unstaged, and uncommitted changes
2. **Code selection** — Inline review of currently selected code in the editor

### Motivation

The non-agentic version of Copilot Code Review (CCR) that powers this experience is being deprecated. As a replacement, the agentic `/review` command (via the Copilot SDK) will be used, surfaced in the same UI to avoid customer disruption.

Key reasons:
- The `/review` command produces better review results based on benchmarks
- The `/review` command is actively improved, while the non-agentic CCR hasn't been updated since late 2025

cc @ria-gopu

title: Feature Request: Native Azure DevOps Pull Request and Inline Comments Support
document:
Feature Request: Native Azure DevOps Pull Request and Inline Comments Support

### Problem
When working on repositories hosted in Azure DevOps (Azure Repos), there is no first-class, built-in support in VS Code for viewing and managing Azure DevOps pull requests and their inline review comments. Existing capabilities are either GitHub-focused (the built-in GitHub Pull Requests experience) or provided by third-party extensions that vary in quality and maintenance. 

Furthermore, because third-party extensions require handling highly sensitive enterprise access tokens, corporate security and compliance teams often restrict or block their installation. This creates severe context switching, friction, and governance roadblocks for contributors and reviewers of ADO-owned repositories who are forced to use the web browser.

### Proposed Feature
Add native Visual Studio Code support for Azure DevOps pull requests and inline review comments. The feature should provide an integrated PR workflow similar in parity to the built-in GitHub Pull Requests experience, including:

- **PR explorer**: List and filter PRs for the current repository/remote (open, assigned, reviewed, draft, etc.).
- **Open PR details**: View PR description, changes, checks/build status, and linked work items.
- **Inline review comments**: Display inline comments in diff/editor views, allow creating new review comments, reply to threads, mark threads as resolved, and apply suggested changes where applicable.
- **Create/manage PRs**: Create a PR from a branch, set reviewers, add work items, and manage PR state (abandon/complete/reopen).
- **Authentication**: Native integration with VS Code's built-in Microsoft Authentication Provider to support Azure DevOps authentication (Azure AD + PAT) and secure multi-account/session management.
- **Instance Support**: Support both Azure DevOps Services (dev.azure.com) and on-premises Azure DevOps Server instances.
- **Notifications and status updates**: Surface CI/build status and PR updates in the editor/status bar and provide quick actions to navigate to PR or open related checks/logs.

### How it would be used (examples)
- While working on a feature branch in an ADO-owned repo, open the PR explorer to see incoming review requests and start reviewing a PR. Open a file diff and respond to inline comments, or add a new inline comment directly in the editor. Resolve comment threads and let the author apply suggested edits.
- Create a PR from a branch without leaving VS Code, add reviewers and link relevant work items, and then monitor build/check status from the PR details view.

### Why this is valuable
- **Reduces Context Switching**: Eliminates constant toggling between VS Code and the Azure DevOps web UI or external tools.
- **Enterprise Compliance**: A first-party, officially signed Microsoft solution satisfies corporate data governance teams who block third-party tools.
- **Speeds Up Code Review**: Smooths out iteration times for teams using Azure Repos/ADO.
- **UX Parity**: Restores ecosystem alignment with the GitHub PR experience that many VS Code users already rely on daily.

### Notes & Related
- **Related/close**: Issue 214017 appears to be notebook/Python-specific; this request targets any repository hosted on Azure DevOps globally.
- **Native Preference**: Built-in or first-party extension support ensures the feature is reliably maintained alongside core VS Code releases and provides a consistent UX across platforms (Windows, macOS, Linux).

### Implementation Suggestions (non-blocking)
- Reuse patterns and UI affordances from the GitHub Pull Requests experience where it makes sense (PR tree view, review panel, comment threading) to provide a familiar UX.
- Provide an opt-in setting to enable/disable Azure DevOps PR integration for users who prefer alternative workflows.

### Acceptance Criteria
- Users can securely authenticate to Azure DevOps via the native Microsoft identity provider and list PRs for the current repository.
- Users can open a PR’s changes and see inline comments at the correct locations in both unified and side-by-side diffs.
- Users can post/reply to inline review comments and resolve threads from within the VS Code editor lines.

title: Feature Request: Let extensions add custom options to the main "Session Target" dropdown menu
document:
Feature Request: Let extensions add custom options to the main "Session Target" dropdown menu

I’m really enjoying the new Agents Window and how it tracks tasks, but I’ve hit a bit of a wall with the main Session Target dropdown menu (the one that lets you pick between Local, Cloud, and Copilot CLI). Right now, this menu seems locked down to third-party extensions.If you are building a custom local coding agent or using an independent CLI harness (like pi-cli or something inspired by Databricks' Omnigent meta-harness approach), the only option is to register it as a sub-tool under an .agent.md file.The problem with this is "Double Model Invocation." The default Copilot model has to wake up first just to parse my prompt and type the terminal command, which then fires my actual local agent model. Running two LLMs back-to-back adds annoying latency, wastes tokens, and can sometimes mess with the custom system prompts inside my CLI.What I'd love to see:It would be awesome if extensions could add a custom option directly to that top-level dropdown menu. Selecting it would basically turn the Agents Window into a direct pass-through string pipeline. It would just grab my raw prompt and active session_id, and hand it straight to my local CLI binary—completely skipping the parent model interpretation phase.This would give developers the best of both worlds: the beautiful UI and task-tracking history of the new Agents Window, but with the freedom to use their own zero-overhead local execution engines underneath. 

I really hope this is considered.

Ty Ron

title: Agents window: Browsers should be isolated to the active session
document:
Agents window: Browsers should be isolated to the active session

Currently in the Agents window:
- "Shared" browsers are shared with all agents
- Agents trying to open the same URL open in another session, will prompt to share or reuse that
- Browsers opened by a background session will be opened in the active session

Ideally, browser tabs are always scoped to the particular session where they were opened.

title: Copilot Custom Endpoint ignores model temperature options, causing Moonshot/Kimi models to fail
document:
Copilot Custom Endpoint ignores model temperature options, causing Moonshot/Kimi models to fail

Summary

When using GitHub Copilot BYOK Custom Endpoint in VS Code with an OpenAI-compatible Chat Completions endpoint, Copilot appears to ignore or override the configured model temperature.

This breaks Moonshot/Kimi models such as kimi-k2.7-code, because Moonshot rejects any temperature value other than 1.

The same endpoint, API key, and model work correctly when called directly with curl using "temperature": 1.

Environment

* VS Code: 1.124.2 (Universal)
* GitHub Copilot extension: 0.41 (2026-03-25)
* OS: macOS
* Provider: BYOK Custom Endpoint
* API type: chat-completions
* Endpoint: https://opencode.ai/zen/go/v1/chat/completions
* Model: kimi-k2.7-code

Configuration

chatLanguageModels.json:

{
 "name": "OpenCode Go - Chat Completions",
 "vendor": "customendpoint",
 "apiKey": "${input:chat.lm.secret.xxxxxxxx}",
 "apiType": "chat-completions",
 "models": [
 {
 "id": "kimi-k2.7-code",
 "name": "Kimi K2.7",
 "url": "https://opencode.ai/zen/go/v1/chat/completions",
 "toolCalling": true,
 "vision": false,
 "maxInputTokens": 262144,
 "maxOutputTokens": 16000,
 "streaming": true,
 "modelOptions": {
 "temperature": 1,
 "top_p": 1
 }
 }
 ]
}

I also tried removing modelOptions, setting temperature at different levels, and disabling toolCalling, but the request still fails with the same temperature-related error.

Actual behavior

Using the model from Copilot Chat fails with:

Reason: Request Failed: 400 {"error":{"message":"Error from provider (Moonshot AI): invalid temperature: only 1 is allowed for this model","type":"invalid_request_error"}}

The stack trace points to the Copilot extension:

/extensions/copilot/dist/extension.js

Expected behavior

Copilot Custom Endpoint should either:

1. Respect the configured temperature from modelOptions, or
2. Provide a documented way to configure request body parameters such as temperature, top_p, etc., or
3. Allow disabling temperature entirely for providers/models that require it.

For this model, the final request body must include:

"temperature": 1

or omit any incompatible default temperature.

Direct API call works

The same endpoint and model work correctly outside VS Code:

curl -i https://opencode.ai/zen/go/v1/chat/completions \
 -H "Authorization: Bearer " \
 -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
 -d '{
 "model": "kimi-k2.7-code",
 "messages": [
 {
 "role": "user",
 "content": "Answer just with: working"
 }
 ],
 "temperature": 1,
 "stream": false,
 "max_tokens": 1000
 }'

Response:

{
 "model": "kimi-k2.7-code",
 "choices": [
 {
 "message": {
 "role": "assistant",
 "content": "working"
 },
 "finish_reason": "stop"
 }
 ]
}

This confirms that:

* The API key is valid.
* The endpoint works.
* The model ID is valid.
* Moonshot/Kimi accepts the request when temperature is exactly 1.

Additional comparison

Another model from the same custom endpoint, glm-5.1, works in VS Code/Copilot with the same provider configuration.

This suggests the issue is not with the endpoint or API key, but with Copilot sending a default temperature value that Moonshot/Kimi rejects.

Why this matters

Some OpenAI-compatible providers/models have strict parameter requirements. In this case, Moonshot/Kimi rejects any temperature other than 1.

Currently, BYOK Custom Endpoint does not appear to provide a reliable way to control or disable the default temperature sent by Copilot.

Related context

The Custom Endpoint provider appears to be the new BYOK provider for OpenAI-compatible, Responses API, and Messages API endpoints.

A configurable request-body option for Custom Endpoint models would make this provider compatible with stricter APIs such as Moonshot/Kimi.

title: Add missing unit test coverage for prefixedUuid in uuid.ts
document:
Add missing unit test coverage for prefixedUuid in uuid.ts

### Description

While reviewing the UUID utility module, I noticed that the `prefixedUuid` helper exported from `src/vs/base/common/uuid.ts` currently has no direct unit test coverage in `src/vs/base/test/common/uuid.test.ts`.

The existing `UUID` test suite covers `generateUuid` and validates generated UUIDs using `isUUID`, but it does not include any tests for the namespace prefixing behavior implemented by `prefixedUuid`.

### Proposed Solution

I would like to add a small test case to verify that `prefixedUuid` behaves as expected by checking that:

1. The returned string starts with the provided namespace followed by a hyphen.
2. The total length of the generated string matches the expected value based on the namespace length and UUID length.
3. The portion of the string following the namespace prefix is a valid UUID.

This would provide direct coverage for `prefixedUuid` and help ensure its behavior remains correct if the implementation changes in the future.

I already have the test prepared and would be happy to submit a pull request if this issue is accepted. Could a maintainer please assign this issue to me?

title: [COGS] Investigate Todo/Task tool use with CLI SDK
document:
[COGS] Investigate Todo/Task tool use with CLI SDK

With the move to CLI SDK, we need to investigate how the Todo/Task tool use impacts the models resolution rates and token usage. 
We learnt that it negatively does so for larger models, and delegating TODO management to a smaller background agent can reduce cogs (See Background TODO Agent exp). 

As a result we need to:
1. investigate with evals whether turning off the TODO tool can reduce cost while not negatively impacting the resolution rate
2. If savings are seen, implement a background todo agent in the CLI SDK world to reduce cost for the user for the relevant scenarios

title: Chat: Enabled Plugins setting description is too enterprise-focused for general use
document:
Chat: Enabled Plugins setting description is too enterprise-focused for general use

## Problem

The `chat.plugins.enabledPlugins` setting works perfectly well for individual users and workspace-level configuration, but its description is heavily overloaded with enterprise/policy language that makes it confusing and intimidating for regular users.

Current description:

> Enterprise-managed plugin enablement. Keys are plugin IDs in `@` form (resolved to Copilot CLI install paths); values enable (`true`) or disable (`false`) the plugin. Discovered alongside the path-keyed entries in Chat: Plugin Locations. When set by policy, also restricts which marketplace-discovered plugins are allowed to load (only IDs mapped to `true` here pass the gate).

## Suggestion

The setting description should be simpler and user-focused. The enterprise/policy gating behavior is a secondary concern and could be mentioned briefly or in a `markdownDescription` link, rather than leading the description.

Something like:

> Enable or disable Copilot Chat plugins. Keys are plugin IDs in `@` format; values are `true` (enabled) or `false` (disabled). When managed by enterprise policy, this also controls which marketplace plugins are allowed to load.

This keeps the setting approachable for users who just want to toggle plugins on/off in their user or workspace settings, while still noting the policy behavior for admins.

title: [AHP/CLI: COGS] Compaction Improvements
document:
[AHP/CLI: COGS] Compaction Improvements

**Priority:** P2

Our approach adds transcripts post-compaction for reference and lookup; CLI uses checkpoints instead. Transcripts allow models to reference previous tool calls and prevent token-wasteful loops after compaction.

Also ensure that we always have a warm cache before we kick off compaction (even at a new turn boundaries).

title: [AHP:COGS]: Cache Checkpoint Gap
document:
[AHP:COGS]: Cache Checkpoint Gap

**Priority:** P3

Our implementation uses one additional cache checkpoint compared to the SDK (which has 3). This extra checkpoint is good to have.

document:

**Priority:** P1

Opus48 on CLI uses 200k more tokens per turn than VSCode chat. 
Additional insight: VSCode users average fewer turns per session than CLI users, so there may be system prompt differences which might affect our workloads. Plan to review and run evals.

title: [AHP/CLI:COGS] Client Side Tool Search & Tools Grouping Service
document:
[AHP/CLI:COGS] Client Side Tool Search & Tools Grouping Service

**Priority:** P0

Upstream issue:https://github.com/github/copilot-mcp-core/issues/1818 — Bridging the gap between VS Code and Copilot CLI/SDK tool search

## Model support

Both Anthropic and OpenAI natively support tool search, surfaced through the `supports.tool_search` capability flag on `IChatModelCapabilities`:

- [`extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/common/endpointProvider.ts` → `IChatModelCapabilities`](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/common/endpointProvider.ts#L33-L60) (see `supports.tool_search`)

## SDK-side buy-in to allow client-supplied tool search

Our tool search tool is complex and depends on the client-side embeddings service, so we've gotten buy-in from the SDK / CLI folks to let clients pass in their own tool search rather than relying solely on the built-in one.

On our side, the client will update our embeddings service to also index on the SDK tools, keeping client-side tool search accurate as the SDK tool set evolves.

---
**Context:** We're migrating to using the CLI in AHP as the default. Tracking this item given the COGS impact.

title: Agent terminal gets stuck on interactive package manager prompts (needrestart, debconf, pipe buffering)
document:
Agent terminal gets stuck on interactive package manager prompts (needrestart, debconf, pipe buffering)

## Summary

The agent terminal tool fails to handle two classes of interactive prompts, causing consistent eval failures on terminalbench2 tasks that copilot-cli passes.

## Problem 1: needrestart/debconf TUI prompts

When `apt-get install` triggers needrestart, a "Which services should be restarted?" TUI prompt appears. None of the `detectsHighConfidenceInputPattern()` or `detectsLikelyInputRequiredPattern()` patterns in `outputMonitor.ts` match this format. The `idleSilence` timer fires after 60s, the tool returns "moved to background", and the agent gives up.

**Example**: `nginx-request-logging` — agent ran `apt-get install -y nginx`, needrestart prompted, agent waited 5 minutes then sent an empty `send_to_terminal` and stopped. copilot-cli finished the same task in 33 seconds.

## Problem 2: Pipe buffering hides prompts

When the model pipes an interactive command through `tail`/`head`/`grep`, the pipe buffers all output until the upstream closes. Interactive prompts (like opam `[Y/n]`) never reach the terminal buffer, so the `OutputMonitor` cannot detect them.

**Example**: `compile-compcert` — agent ran `opam install coq -y 2>&1 | tail -20`. The opam depext `[Y/n]` prompt was buffered by `tail` and never visible to the output monitor. The `[Y/n]` pattern *would* match `detectsHighConfidenceInputPattern` if it reached the terminal — the bug is the pipe hiding it.

## Fix

1. Set `DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive` in the copilot agent terminal env vars (in `toolTerminalCreator.ts`), alongside existing `GIT_PAGER`, `GIT_EDITOR`, etc. This prevents apt/dpkg from ever opening interactive prompts.

2. Add system prompt guidance telling the model to never pipe interactive commands through filters.

## Impact

Found via terminalbench2 eval analysis comparing vscode-copilot-cli vs copilot-cli. These two issues account for 2 of 12 consistent regressions (~13% score gap).

title: Agent turn hangs indefinitely when a streaming response stalls (no idle timeout in messagesApi/responsesApi SSE read loop)
document:
Agent turn hangs indefinitely when a streaming response stalls (no idle timeout in messagesApi/responsesApi SSE read loop)

## Summary

claude-opus agent turns hang for the full harness wall-clock cap (eval: `X_AGENT_STILL_RESPONDING`, 0 output) when a single `/v1/messages` (Anthropic Messages API) streaming response stalls — the upstream/proxy returns HTTP 200 headers but never streams a terminating body and never closes the socket, and the client's SSE read loop in `processResponseFromMessagesEndpoint` has **no idle timeout**, so the turn never resolves or rejects. Found via terminalbench2 eval runs where it was the dominant error mode (~18 of 30 errored instances across 3 `vscode-copilot-cli` runs). The same unguarded read loop exists in the OpenAI **Responses API** path, so this is not Anthropic-specific in principle.

## Where it hangs

[`extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/node/messagesApi.ts`](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/node/messagesApi.ts) → `processResponseFromMessagesEndpoint`:

No inactivity guard. When the body delivers 200 headers but no SSE bytes (and never ends), this loop blocks forever: no `message_stop` is parsed, the `AsyncIterableObject` never calls `feed.emitOne(...)` or `feed.reject(...)`, and the `ToolCallingLoop` awaiting the turn hangs until an outer cap (the eval harness's 60-min limit; in product, effectively indefinitely / until user cancel) kills it.

The **identical pattern** is in `extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/node/responsesApi.ts` → `processResponseFromChatEndpoint` (line ~907), so the OpenAI Responses path shares the vulnerability.

## Evidence (from eval `GitHub Copilot Chat.log`)

A healthy turn logs `[messagesAPI] message 0 returned. finish reason:[stop]` → `ccreq …| success | claude-opus…` → (final turn) `[ToolCallingLoop] Stop hook result: shouldContinue=false` → clean exit.

A hung turn:

| Instance | Signature | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| circuit-fibsqrt | 3 turns OK, 4th `/v1/messages` never returns | last `ccreq …success…[stop]` @ `20:50:54`; capi-proxy sends req `[10]` @ `20:50:57` with **no** matching `[messagesAPI] message returned` / `ccreq`; silence → OTel flush `21:50:42` (60-min cap) |
| regex-chess | 2 turns OK then hang | last return `21:08:27` → cap flush `22:08:14` |
| train-fasttext | 36 turns OK then hang | last return `22:30:41` → flush `22:36:46` (hung at min ~54) |
| winning-avg-corewars | 60 turns OK then hang | last return `22:40:18` → flush `22:42:54`; one completed turn took `133,191ms` |

The hang strikes an intermittent turn (2nd to 61st), confirming it is a per-turn streaming stall, not a prompt/content issue.

### Affected eval runs (terminalbench2, vscode-copilot-cli)

| Run | Model | Resolved / Failed / Err | timeout-hangs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 27478622081 | opus-4.6 | 48 / 28 / 13 | 9 |
| 27511500953 | opus-4.6 | 52 / 27 / 10 | 6 |
| 27511511587 | opus-4.7 | 60 / 23 / 5 | 3 |

Same instances `PASS` on the headless Copilot CLI control runs (different harness): 27479099364 (0 err), 27512067473 (1 err).

## Why GPT-5.x was not hit

GPT-5.x routes through `useResponsesApi` → `processResponseFromChatEndpoint` (OpenAI Responses upstream), which is selected **before** the Messages path; only models advertising `Messages` in `supported_endpoints` (Anthropic) hit `processResponseFromMessagesEndpoint`. The streaming stall in these runs occurred on the Anthropic `/v1/messages` upstream, which OpenAI models don't use. **This is routing luck, not structural immunity** — the Responses path has the same unguarded read loop and would hang identically if its upstream stalled the same way.

## Ruled out

- **OTel consent modal** — present on resolved instances too; a universal end-of-run screenshot artifact, not the cause.
- **"Retry storm" / repeated `x-request-id`** — that id is a per-session id, constant across all `/v1/messages` calls in a session; resolved instances repeat it 28–37× and pass.
- **HMAC 401 near end-of-hang** — a background model-metadata refetch firing ~55 min *after* the wedge; effect, not cause.

## Proposed fix

1. **Add a streaming idle timeout** to both `processResponseFromMessagesEndpoint` (`messagesApi.ts`) and `processResponseFromChatEndpoint` (`responsesApi.ts`). Arm a watchdog that resets on each chunk; if no chunk arrives within ~120s, `response.body.destroy(new Error('stream idle timeout'))` so the `for await` throws and `feed.reject(...)` fails the turn fast, letting the existing fetch/retry path recover instead of hanging. Also `feed.reject(...)` if the stream ends without a `message_stop`/completion having been emitted.
2. **Wire the request `CancellationToken` through** — `processResponseFromChatEndpoint` and `processResponseFromMessagesEndpoint` are currently called without the `cancellationToken` that `defaultChatResponseProcessor` receives; pass it so user/turn cancellation can abort a stuck body read, and add a per-turn wall-clock deadline to catch dribbling keep-alive streams that never terminate.
3. **Upstream/proxy** — investigate why streaming responses can return 200 headers and then never stream a body or close; the client timeout is the safety net, not the root remedy.

## Links

- Code: `extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/node/messagesApi.ts` (`processResponseFromMessagesEndpoint`); `extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/node/responsesApi.ts` (`processResponseFromChatEndpoint`)
- Eval analysis cache: `~/.agent/eval-cache/opus46-cli-compare/timeout-rootcause.md`
- Related (eval-side, supersede — modal claim disproven): microsoft/vscode-copilot-evaluation#4863

title: “Autopilot (Preview)” text has insufficient color contrast in Light 2026 theme:A11y_VisualStudioCode_Permissions levels in Copilot CLI_Contrast
document:
“Autopilot (Preview)” text has insufficient color contrast in Light 2026 theme:A11y_VisualStudioCode_Permissions levels in Copilot CLI_Contrast

## GitHub Tags
#A11yTCS;#Visual Studio Code Client; #SH_Integrated browser features_Win32_JUNE2026;#DesktopApp;#FTP;#Win32;#A11ySev2;#A11yMAS;#WCAG1.4.3;

## Issue Subcategory 
Low contrast text 

## User Experience 
Users with low vision or impaired contrast sensitivity cannot read the text. This is one of the most common accessibility barriers on the web.

## Repro Steps 
Open VS Code > Copilot chat > Permission Picker combo box > Observe the issue: “Autopilot (Preview)” text has insufficient color contrast in Light 2026 theme

## Actual Result 
The “Autopilot (Preview)” text does not meet the minimum required color contrast ratio of 4.5:1 against its background in the Light 2026 theme, making it difficult to read.

## Expected Result 
The text should meet a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 (for normal text) against the background to ensure readability for all users.

## How to Fix 
Darken the text color or lighten/darken the background until the ratio is met. Use [WebAIM Contrast Checker](https://webaim.org/resources/contrastchecker/) to verify. Audit all color themes including dark mode.

## Environment details 
Application Name: VS Code 

Edition Windows 11 Enterprise
Version 25H2
OS build 26200.8390

## Note

- Please do not close this bug. This bug should only be closed by TCS, C+AI Accessibility tester after bug verification.
- "[Check out Accessibility Insights!](https://nam06.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https://accessibilityinsights.io/&data=02%7c01%7cv-stfe%40microsoft.com%7cb67b2c4b646d4f9561a208d6f4b5c39b%7c72f988bf86f141af91ab2d7cd011db47%7c1%7c0%7c636965458850501301&sdata=mxhokIKNMb22llsjXHLgU3XZibj1Qfx37rpY4PU2sfE%3D&reserved=0) - Identify accessibility bugs before check-in and make bug fixing faster and easier."

title: Selecting 200K context size for Claude Sonnet 4.6 has maxPromptTokens: 936000 (1M)
document:
Selecting 200K context size for Claude Sonnet 4.6 has maxPromptTokens: 936000 (1M)

ElectronBuildId: 14159160

When selecting Claude Sonnet 4.6 in the chat model picker with the context size set to **200K** (the Default option), the actual request sent to the model uses the **1M** context window instead.

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. Open the Chat view and select **Claude Sonnet 4.6** as the model.
2. In the context size dropdown, select **200K** (marked as Default).
3. Send a message.
4. Open the request log and inspect the request metadata.

**Expected behavior:**
With the **200K** context size selected, `maxPromptTokens` + `maxResponseTokens` should reflect a ~200K total context window (e.g. `maxPromptTokens` of roughly 136000 with `maxResponseTokens` of 64000).

**Actual behavior:**
The request metadata shows a 1M-sized context window:
- `model`: `claude-sonnet-4.6`
- `maxPromptTokens`: `936000`
- `maxResponseTokens`: `64000`

This corresponds to the **1M** option, not the selected **200K**

## GitHub Tags
#A11yTCS;#Visual Studio Code Client; #SH_Integrated browser features_Win32_JUNE2026;#DesktopApp;#FTP;#Win32;#A11ySev2;#A11yMAS;#WCAG1.4.1;

## Issue Subcategory 
Links distinguished only by color 

## User Experience 
Users with color deficiencies cannot distinguish inline links from surrounding body text. They may miss important navigation options entirely. 

## Repro Steps 
Open VS Code > Copilot chat > Permission Picker combo box > Bypass approvals > “chat.permissions.default" link > Observe the issue: Links distinguished only by color 

## Actual Result 
The link “chat.permissions.default” is visually distinguished only by color (blue text) and does not have any additional non-color visual indicator such as underline or styling.

## Expected Result 
Links should have a non-color visual indicator (e.g., underline, bold text, or icon) in addition to color so that they are distinguishable from surrounding text for all users.

## How to Fix 
Add text-decoration: underline to inline links, or provide another non-color visual cue (bold, border-bottom). Ensure the link color also meets 3:1 contrast against surrounding text.

Supplementary information is not programmatically associated

Screen reader users may not hear important helper text, requirements, or error context associated with a form field, so they may not understand how to complete it correctly.

title: API: expose panel/sidebar/auxiliaryBar visibility state to extensions
document:
API: expose panel/sidebar/auxiliaryBar visibility state to extensions

## Problem

There is no public Extension API to query whether the sidebar, panel (bottom), or auxiliary bar are currently visible. The internal `IWorkbenchLayoutService` has exactly what's needed — `isVisible("workbench.parts.sidebar")` and `onDidChangePartVisibility` — but neither is bridged to the extension host.

As a result, extensions that need to save and restore layout state (e.g. a "maximize editor" toggle) have no reliable way to read the current visibility of these three parts. The only workaround is to parse VS Code's internal SQLite database (`state.vscdb`) directly, which is fragile and reads stale data mid-session.

The layout buttons in the VS Code title bar already track this state correctly because they run in the renderer process with direct access to the layout service. Extensions cannot do the same.

## Proposed API

Add to `vscode.window`:

## Use Case

A "maximize editor" extension needs to:
1. Read current visibility of all three parts before hiding them
2. Restore only the parts that were originally visible

Without this API, if the user hides the bottom panel manually before pressing maximize, the extension has no way to know — and incorrectly restores it on unmaximize.

This is a general-purpose API gap: any extension that interacts with workbench layout (focus modes, presentation modes, Zen-like toggles) hits the same wall.

## Current Workaround (and why it's bad)

Read `~/.config/Code/User/workspaceStorage//state.vscdb` directly via a custom SQLite B-tree parser. Problems:
- Only accurate at window open (VS Code flushes the DB on close, not on toggle)
- Relies on undocumented internal key names (`workbench.sideBar.hidden`, etc.)
- Breaks if VS Code changes its storage format

## Prior Art

- Context keys `sideBarVisible`, `panelVisible`, `auxiliaryBarVisible` already exist internally and are kept in sync via `onDidChangePartVisibility`
- These are used in `when` clauses in `package.json` but cannot be read at runtime by extension code
- Exposing them (or wrapping `isVisible()`) would be a small bridge with high utility

title: "Data Science" profile template: files.exclude patterns match dotfile names, not extensions
document:
"Data Science" profile template: files.exclude patterns match dotfile names, not extensions

The template's files.exclude uses **/.csv, **/.parquet, **/.pkl, **/.xls. In VS Code glob syntax **/.csv matches a file literally named .csv, not files with a .csv extension, so these four entries are accepted but never exclude the intended data files. They should be **/*.csv, **/*.parquet, **/*.pkl, **/*.xls.

title: Copilot semantic_search returns incorrect file paths for directory names that contain a dot
document:
Copilot semantic_search returns incorrect file paths for directory names that contain a dot

The built-in Copilot `semantic_search` tool returns incorrect file paths for directory names that contain a dot. Results from dotted directories (e.g. `MyProject.Common`, `MyProject.Api`) are mangled, so the returned path points at a non-existent location on disk. The model then cannot find the result files, and the clickable `filepath:` links rendered in chat are dead.

I use the troubleshoot command and let the agent read the session log to confirm the details of the issue.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Codebase search must be enabled, workspace must be indexed. Workspace has a repo on github.
1. Open a workspace with directories with dots in the names, e.g. a repo rooted at `C:\repos\MyProject` that contains directories named `MyProject.Common`, `MyProject.Api`, `MyProject.Tests`, etc.
1. In Chat agent mode, run a query that triggers the `semantic_search` tool with a prompt likely to match code that lives under one of the `MyProject.*` dotted folders.
1. Inspect the `filepath:` lines returned in the tool result and in the fenced code blocks.

## Expected behavior

The tool returns the on-disk path verbatim, e.g.: `C:\repos\MyProject\MyProject.Common\Settings\MyFile.cs`

## Actual behavior

The directory name is magled and the part after the full stop is removed: `C:\repos\MyProject\MyProject\Settings\MyFile.cs` which cannot be opened. Most results whose first path component after the workspace root contains a dot is affected in the same way: `MyProject.Common` -> `MyProject`, `MyProject.Api` -> `MyProject`, etc.

Directories named `MyProject.Tests` seemed to be unaffected.

## Environment

- OS: Windows 10 (10.0.26200)
- VS Code: 1.124.2, commit `6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c`, Electron 42.2.0, Node 24.15.0
- Copilot: built-in extension (no separate version)

title: Chat: GitHub Copilot quota/billing gates the entire Chat surface even when a separate (BYOK/subscription) provider is selected
document:
Chat: GitHub Copilot quota/billing gates the entire Chat surface even when a separate (BYOK/subscription) provider is selected

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: bundled with VS Code (no longer versioned separately)
- VS Code Version: 1.124.2 (Universal), commit 6928394f91b684055b873eecb8bc281365131f1c — also reproduced on 1.123.x
- OS Version: macOS (Darwin arm64 25.5.0)
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): agent mode
- Selected model: a **Claude** provider backed by a separate Anthropic/Claude subscription (BYOK/subscription), switched from the default `Local` (GitHub Copilot) provider
- Logs: not attached

1. Configure Chat with two providers: `Local` (GitHub Copilot) and a `Claude` provider authenticated with a *separate* Anthropic/Claude subscription (a different account/subscription, not just a different model).
2. Reach the Copilot limit, or let Copilot billing lapse/expire, so the `Local` provider returns a quota/billing error.
3. In the provider/model picker, switch from `Local` to `Claude`.
4. Send a message.

---

### Summary

The first-party **Chat** UI supports multiple providers — the default `Local` (GitHub Copilot) provider and a separate `Claude` provider backed by my own Anthropic/Claude subscription. When the Copilot entitlement is exhausted/expired, I expect to hit the limit on `Local` — that's correct. But **switching the provider to `Claude` does not let me keep working**: the Copilot quota/billing state appears to gate the whole Chat surface, so an independently-funded provider that has plenty of available usage is also blocked.

### Expected

Requests route through the selected `Claude` provider's subscription (which has available usage), and Chat keeps working. The Copilot limit should only affect the Copilot-backed provider — switching providers should be a graceful fallback.

### Actual

Chat stays blocked by the Copilot quota/billing state regardless of the selected provider. The only ways to keep working are to leave the first-party Chat entirely (Anthropic's standalone Claude Code extension, or the Claude CLI), which defeats the purpose of the integrated provider picker.

### Additional observation (possible cause)

Even with the `Claude` provider selected, my Chat debug history shows requests going through a **`Claude Copilot Proxy`**. That suggests the `Claude` provider's traffic is still routed through GitHub Copilot infrastructure rather than directly to Anthropic — which would explain why the Copilot quota/billing gate applies regardless of which provider is selected. If the gate is enforced at that proxy layer, switching providers can't serve as a fallback by design.

### Impact

- A healthy, separately-paid subscription is unusable precisely when it's needed most (Copilot exhausted/expired).
- Users are pushed out of the integrated editor Chat experience for a billing reason unrelated to the provider they actually selected.
- The provider picker implies provider-level isolation that doesn't hold for entitlement/quota.

### Related issues (different scenarios; this one is specifically about provider-switch fallback)

- #320227 — Copilot Free tier: BYOK models lose tool access after downgrade.
- #316404 — Claude (Max) subscription hits phantom Copilot session/rate limits; a comment there describes requests routing through the Copilot API gateway.
- #314952 — Feature request: allow Claude Agent to run through an Anthropic subscription instead of Copilot quota.
- #311136 — Copilot Chat doesn't work with the Claude Code Agent.

### Question for maintainers

Is gating the Chat surface on Copilot entitlement — even when a non-Copilot provider is explicitly selected — intended? Given the `Claude Copilot Proxy` observation above: can the `Claude` provider talk to Anthropic directly (bypassing the Copilot proxy) when an alternate subscription is configured, or can the Copilot limit at least be scoped to the Copilot provider so an explicitly-selected alternate-subscription provider can serve as a working fallback?

title: Inline rename for chats in the chat composite bar (Agents window)
document:
Inline rename for chats in the chat composite bar (Agents window)

Adds inline renaming of a chat directly inside its tab in the chat composite bar (Agents window), mirroring how the session title is renamed inline in the session header.

- **Double-click** a tab — or use the context-menu **Rename** — to reveal a themed input pre-filled with the chat title.
- **Enter** commits the rename; **Escape** or **blur** cancels.
- Clicking another tab while editing cancels the edit and switches to that tab.

Implemented in PR #321384.

### Verification steps
1. Open the Agents window with at least two chats so multiple tabs appear in the chat composite bar.
2. **Double-click** a tab — confirm an input appears in-place, pre-filled with the current title and fills the tab width (label, status indicator and close button hidden). Type a new name and press **Enter** — confirm the tab shows the new name.
3. Right-click a tab and choose **Rename** — confirm the same inline input appears and committing with **Enter** renames the chat.
4. Start a rename, then press **Escape** — confirm the edit is cancelled and the original title is retained. Repeat, but click outside the input (blur) — confirm the edit is also cancelled.
5. Start a rename on one tab, then click a *different* tab — confirm the edit cancels and focus switches to the clicked tab.
6. Rename a chat, then reload the Agents window — confirm the new name persists.
7. Verify in both Dark and Light themes that the rename input is themed correctly, and that long titles render without breaking the tab layout.

Refs #321384.

title: VS Code does not reload modified .ipynb files when stored on external drives and edited on another machine
document:
VS Code does not reload modified .ipynb files when stored on external drives and edited on another machine

When working with .ipynb files stored on an external SSD and switching between two PCs, VS Code does not reload the updated notebook when reopening it on the first machine. Instead, it restores the old in‑memory notebook state.

To reproduce: 
1. Open a .ipynb on PC1. 
2. Close the VS Studio sesion with the .ipynb open, but saved on the external SSD
2. Modify and save it on PC2 (same file on external SSD). 
3. Return to PC1 and reopen VS Code.

I expected that VS Code detects the file changed on disk and reloads the new version, but VS Code restores the previous notebook state from memory and ignores the updated file on disk.

However, files.autoReload works for .py files but not for .ipynb; disabling hotExit and restoreWindows avoids the issue, but it is only a workaround, I think

Request: 
Please add an option for .ipynb files to always reload from disk when they change, or disable notebook state restoration.

title: Custom Editor feature does not work with Virtual Documents
document:
Custom Editor feature does not work with Virtual Documents

Several commands for Custom Editor (e.g., "Reopen Editor With..." (`workbench.action.reopenWithEditor`) and `reopenActiveEditorWith`) do not work with virtual documents created by TextDocumentContentProvider API.

When a new virtual document is opened via `vscode.window.showTextDocument` API or `vscode.open` command, the default text editor is used, regardless of the "editor.associations" setting. Also, "Reopen Editor With..." command has no response for the opened document.

Using `vscode.openWith` command, new virtual documant can be opened with a custom editor. In this case, "Reopen Editor With..." command works. However, once after switching to the default editor, there seems to be no way to switch back to the custom editor.

The following is a partial code of the extension that creates a markdown-style virtual document. The code is based on generator-code "New Extension (TypeScript)"-type template. One can reproduce the situation with this extension. This dummy extension assumes Built-in markdown preview editor is used as a custom editor but the problems will occur even when another custom editor is used.

Virtual Document is a helpful feature for extension authors in several scenarios, such as showing generated content, since no physical file is created and save dialog is not shown when being closed. The above problems will limit the use of virtual documents in those scenarios. It would be great if the problem is fixed.

title: 1.125.0 Endgame
document:
1.125.0 Endgame

[Endgame guide](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog/blob/main/guidelines/endgame-guide.md)

# Before Monday 6AM Zurich time

`@team`:
- Update endgame availability 
- Test plan items created 
- Milestone only has TPIs. All other issues need to be closed
- All closed issues in milestone labeled `on-testplan`, `bug`, or `verification-needed`
- Verification steps added to `bug` and `verification-needed` issues

# Monday morning (Zurich)

`@endgame-champ`:
- [x] Assign TPIs
 - [x] Create TPI's for all verification needed bugs. Visit `Testing and Verification` on tools site, and generate groupings, then create TPIs. The TPI section below will update with all of the generated items. 
- [x] Pause automatic releases (this also ensures chat-pre-release isn't released) after 10am.
- [x] Run the [Start Endgame](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-engineering/actions/workflows/start-endgame.yml) workflow
 
 - PRs opened with auto-merge enabled — ensure they get reviewed and merged Monday morning so Insiders picks up the next milestone version:
 - [x] Version bump (`microsoft/vscode`): https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/321382
 - [x] Endgame notebook milestone bump (`microsoft/vscode`): https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/321381
 
 - If you need to run these steps manually, see the [Branching guide](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog/blob/main/guidelines/endgame-guide.md#branching).

# Monday morning (Redmond and Zurich)

`@team`:
- [Test plan items](https://github.com/issues?q=org%3Amicrosoft%20milestone%3A%221.125.0%22%20is%3Aissue%20is%3Aopen%20label%3Atestplan-item). All TPIs must be finished by noon. This includes verification TPIs.
- Make sure your TPIs are being tested and ping folks if to confirm they will finish by noon.

# Monday afternoon (Redmond and Zurich)

`@team`:
- Fix essential issues. Default to pushing to next iteration
- Release notes 

# Monday EOD Redmond time

`@team`:
- All issues including late fixes, are verified ([query](https://github.com/issues?q=org%3Amicrosoft+milestone%3A%221.125.0%22+is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed+reason%3Acompleted++label%3Abug+-label%3Averified+-label%3Aon-testplan+-label%3A*duplicate+-label%3Aduplicate+-label%3Ainvalid+-label%3A*as-designed+-label%3Aerror-telemetry+-label%3Averification-steps-needed+-label%3Az-author-verified+-label%3Aunreleased+-label%3A*not-reproducible+-label%3A*out-of-scope+sort%3Aupdated-asc))
- Release notes written

`@endgame-champ`:
- [x] Create new builds from `release/` branch so we can finish verification. Not required to contain OSS Tool changes.
- [x] Run the [OSS Tool Workflow](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-engineering/actions/workflows/oss-tool.yml) to update `ThirdPartyNotices.txt` for release. ([detailed docs / manual fallback](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-build-tools/tree/main/distro-tools))
 - [x] On Failure: an agentic workflow auto-dispatches to fix missing licenses. PR link is in the `safe_outputs summary`
 - [x] Verify each new entry in `cglicenses.json` has the correct license + owner and merge the PR ([example])(https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/317212)), see [docs](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-build-tools/tree/main/distro-tools) for more details.
 - [x] Trigger the OSS Tool Workflow again.
 - [x] On Success: Review and merge the generated PRs.
 - [x] Note: The [cherry-pick workflow](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-engineering/actions/workflows/cherry-pick.yml) auto-generates cherry-pick PRs from `release/*` into main, those must also be merged. ([example](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/317220))

# Tuesday by noon CET

`@team`:
- Fix essential issues. Default to pushing to next iteration
- Release notes 

`@endgame-champ`:
- [x] Generate "Thank you" section of the release notes in the `vnext` branch of the `vscode-docs` repo. 
 - [x] [Acknowledge third-party pull requests](https://tools.code.visualstudio.com/acknowledgement) in release notes. 
 - [x] Acknowledge [issue trackers](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-internalbacklog/wiki/Community-Triage---Credits#community-triage---role-based) from the community (do the copy-paste variant) - 
- [x] Build and release insiders from release/
- [x] Build stable from release/ and release to MSFT folks
- [x] Reenable automatic releases
 - A build is scheduled for 7:00 PM Zurich time (`19:00 GMT+2`); make sure automatic releases are reenabled before then so we get an Insiders build from `main` on Tuesday evening.

# Tuesday afternoon/Wednesday morning

- In extreme cases, accept candidate changes
 - [x] Build stable from `release/`. Do *not* bump the VS Code version.
 - [x] Release stable to MSFT folks

# Wednesday
`@endgame-champ`:
- [x] Stable starts rollout to everyone (start percentage: `20%`, end time UTC: `6AM next Wednesday`)
- [x] [Deploy vscode.dev](https://dev.azure.com/monacotools/Monaco/_build?definitionId=276) (Branch `release/x.y`, Deployment Target `prod`, needs peer approval)
- [x] [Publish @vscode/chat-lib](https://dev.azure.com/monacotools/Monaco/_build?definitionId=683) (Branch `release/x.y`, Release Version `auto`, needs peer approval)

title: Test: Agent Feedback with Agent Host Providers
document:
Test: Agent Feedback with Agent Host Providers

Refs: #321254

- [x] anyOS @federicobrancasi
- [x] anyOS @dileepyavan

Complexity: 4

[Create Issue](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/new?template=blank&body=Testing+%23321338%0A%0A&assignees=benibenj)

---

The agent host now exposes **server-side feedback ("comments") tools** (`addComment`, `listComments`, `deleteComments`, `resolveComments`) and a built-in **`act-on-feedback`** skill. When you submit feedback on a session's changes, the agent loop uses the skill + tools to read your comments, make the requested edits, and mark the comments resolved. This works for any (codex not yet) agent-host harness, verify it for **both Copilot CLI and Claude**.

## Setup

1. Open a folder with some real code (a Git repo is ideal so changes are easy to see).
2. Make sure **both providers run through the agent host** and appear in the Agents window's new-session picker:
 - **Claude**: set `github.copilot.chat.claudeAgent.enabled: true` and `chat.agents.claude.preferAgentHost: true`
 - **Copilot CLI**: ensure the Copilot CLI agent-host provider is enabled/available.

## Test: run the whole flow once per harness (Copilot CLI, then Claude)

1. Open the **Agents window** and start a new session with the chosen provider.
2. Ask the agent to make a code change.
3. **Open the session's changes** (the changed files / diff).
4. In a changed file, **select a range of code** and **add a feedback comment** describing what to change
 - Verify the comment renders as an agent-feedback comment anchored to that range.
5. Add **2–3 more comments** on different selections/files
6. Click **Submit Feedback**

### Verify

- [ ] The agent invokes the built-in **`act-on-feedback`** skill and calls the **`listComments`** tool to retrieve your comments (visible as a tool call in the session).
- [ ] The feedback tools run **without a confirmation prompt** (they're auto-approved).
- [ ] The agent makes edits that actually address each comment.
- [ ] When done, the agent calls **`resolveComments`** and the addressed comments **disappear from the UI**.
- [ ] The resulting code changes match what each comment asked for.

title: Feature Request: Respect metered network connections on Linux (NetworkManager)
document:
Feature Request: Respect metered network connections on Linux (NetworkManager)

# Feature Request: Respect metered network connections on Linux (NetworkManager)

## Problem

VS Code performs automatic update checks and extension update downloads regardless of whether the active network connection is marked as metered. On Linux, NetworkManager exposes this information system-wide via `connection.metered`, which applications are expected to respect to reduce background network activity on limited connections (mobile hotspots, tethered phones, satellite links, expensive roaming).

VS Code does not detect or respect this setting. In my opinion, the Auto-Update should be disabled when on an metered network.

## Expected Behavior

When the active connection is metered, VS Code should:

- suspend automatic update downloads, **or**
- offer a setting to disable update activity on metered connections

The auto check for new updates should be still possible, because it should not use huge amount of data. 
This should apply to VS Code application updates, extension update checks, and extension auto-downloads.

## Linux Integration

NetworkManager exposes the metered status via:

Values: `yes`, `no`, `unknown`. GNOME, KDE, Manjaro, Ubuntu, and Fedora all surface this in their network settings UI.

## Suggested Implementation

Detect `connection.metered` on Linux and pause background update activity automatically. Alternatively, expose a user setting:

title: Advanced Autopilot goal evaluation receives "Start implementation" instead of the actual user request from Plan mode
document:
Advanced Autopilot goal evaluation receives "Start implementation" instead of the actual user request from Plan mode

## Summary
When `chat.autopilot.advanced.enabled` is true, after Plan mode generates a plan, clicking "Implement with Autopilot" starts a new Autopilot session. The goal evaluation step only receives `Original user request: Start implementation` instead of the actual user requirement from the Plan turn.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Enable `chat.autopilot.advanced.enabled`
2. Ask Copilot Chat a question (e.g., "Add version and release date columns to the report")
3. Click "Plan" → let Plan agent respond with a plan
4. Click "Implement with Autopilot"
5. After Autopilot finishes, check the goal evaluation prompt

## Expected
Goal evaluation prompt should contain the actual user request from the Plan turn.

## Actual
Goal evaluation prompt only contains:

## Impact
The goal evaluation LLM has no context about what the user wanted, so it blindly returns YES, making the feature useless.

title: Bug: Advanced Autopilot goal evaluation only gets "Start implementation" as original request
document:
Bug: Advanced Autopilot goal evaluation only gets "Start implementation" as original request

When `chat.autopilot.advanced.enabled` is true, after Plan mode generates a plan, clicking "Implement with Autopilot" starts a new Autopilot session. The goal evaluation step at the end sends the LLM a prompt like:

The user's actual requirement is only documented in the Plan agent's response from the previous conversation turn. The Autopilot evaluation session only sees `Start implementation` as the original request, so the goal evaluation LLM has no idea what the user actually wanted — it's guessing blindly and almost always returning YES, making the feature useless.

**Suggested fix:** Include the actual user request from the Plan mode turn in the goal evaluation prompt.

title: Freeze when opening any dialog V1.123+
document:
Freeze when opening any dialog V1.123+

**Edit from maintainer**: 
Tracking upstream at electron/electron#51988
Known workaround: configure `"window.dialogStyle": "custom"`

---

- VS Code Version: 1.123.0 and 1.124.0 (1.122.1 does NOT reproduce the issue)
- OS Version: CachyOS (Arch Linux based), kernel 7.0.12-1-cachyos, tested on both Wayland and X11 (--ozone-platform=x11)

1. Open VS Code (does not matter if a project is opened)
2. Trigger any action that opens a native confirmation dialog via DialogService#prompt, e.g.:
 - Help > About
 - Source Control > Sync (with git.confirmSync enabled)
 - Source Control > Discard Changes on a modified file
3. The entire window immediately becomes unresponsive (after a while turns grey/blank) as soon as the dialog is requested, and never recovers. VS Code must be force-killed.

What I expected: the dialog opens normally and the window remains responsive.

What actually happens: the whole window freezes the instant the dialog is requested.

Additional notes:
- Reproduced with extensions fully disabled (--disable-extensions), confirming this is not extension-related.
- Reproduced on both Wayland and XWayland (--ozone-platform=x11), so it does not appear to be Wayland-specific.
- Bisected via local package cache: 1.122.1-1.1 works correctly. 1.123.0-1.1 and 1.124.0-1.1 both reproduce the freeze.
- Git extension log (Git.log) shows the operation reaching the dialog and then nothing further:
 `DialogService#prompt This action will pull and push commits from and to "origin/"`
 immediately followed by repeated native crashpad errors:
 `[ERROR:third_party/crashpad/crashpad/snapshot/elf/elf_dynamic_array_reader.h:64] tag not found`
 `[ERROR:third_party/crashpad/crashpad/util/process/process_memory_range.cc:75] read out of range`
- Attempting to attach gdb/strace to the frozen main process (code/code.mjs) causes it to crash with SIGSEGV immediately on PTRACE_ATTACH/PTRACE_SEIZE, preventing further inspection this way.
- No additional errors appear in Dev Tools Console (Help > Toggle Developer Tools) before the freeze; the last console output is the DialogService#prompt trace line above.

Workaround: downgrading to 1.122.1 resolves the issue completely.

title: Copilot Chat destroys edits to files done by other chats, and those reverts are not recorded in edit history (timeline)
document:
Copilot Chat destroys edits to files done by other chats, and those reverts are not recorded in edit history (timeline)

- Copilot Chat Extension Version:
built-in
- VS Code Version:

ElectronBuildId: 14159160

- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode):
Agent in Chat panel

- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet):
any model

**Steps to Reproduce:**

1. Open a new Copliot chat (1), make a prompt, attach context, and (optionally) run prompt to make an edit
2. Open another chat (2), can be Copilot or Codex or Claude etc, make edit via this chat
3. Return to chat (1) and either modify original prompt (which will trigger 'Revert edits' scenario) OR start a new prompt
4. Observe all edits done by chat (2) disappear. Sometimes Copilot warns user before reverting, but sometimes it just **silently** reverts ALL edits it hasn't seen before. Insanity.

**Expected behaviour:**
Particular chat should revert ONLY its own edits, and not edits done by the user or other chats.

Moreover, _sometimes_ such Copilot-induced reverts ARE NOT recorded in Timeline (VSCode file's edit history), and are EXTREMELY hard to track down and to re-revert back. I am facing this scenario right now: been trying to get back my codebase into a working state, after Copilot w/o any confirmations erased all edits done by Codex. Copilot did it on the first prompt, and w/o any warnings... What I had was:
- a Copilot chat (with the prompt typed-in and context attached, but no edits applied yet) that had been open before Codex chat
- Codex applied some edits
- as soon as I submitted the typed-in prompt to Copilot, all Codex' edits to dozens of files that were not even part of the Copilot's prompt, were SILENTLY gone. 

I learned this only after deploy to prod rendered previous version of my project (because Copilot wiped out all changes related to the current version).

Hopefully, the repro below makes the scenario above more obvious:
