title: Extract shared "segment separator" helper to prevent fused assistant text across SDK integrations
document:
Extract shared "segment separator" helper to prevent fused assistant text across SDK integrations

## Background

We've now shipped the same fix to the same bug pattern twice:

- **OpenAI Responses API** ([#312173](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/ `lastTextDeltaOutputIndex` in `extensions/copilot/src/platform/endpoint/node/responsesApi.ts`312173)) 
- **Copilot CLI** ([#319727](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/pull/ `lastEmittedAssistantMessageId` in `extensions/copilot/src/extension/chatSessions/copilotcli/node/copilotcliSession.ts`319727)) 

In both cases the symptom in the Agents window chat was the same: consecutive assistant text items fused into one run-on paragraph (e.g. `"...wiring:Now add..."` instead of two paragraphs).

The Claude Code SDK path (`extensions/copilot/src/extension/chatSessions/claude/common/claudeMessageDispatch.ts`) likely has the same latent multiple `text` content blocks in one `SDKAssistantMessage` (or consecutive messages in one turn) are forwarded via `stream.markdown(item.text)` with no but no user-visible report has come in yet.separator bug 

## Why this keeps happening

1. **Impedance mismatch.** Every model SDK models a turn as discrete items (`content_block`s, `output_index`-tagged events, `messageId`-tagged messages). `vscode.ChatResponseStream.markdown(...)` is a purely concatenative sink. The natural way to write the handler (`for (item) stream.markdown(item.text)`) silently fuses items.
2. **Hand-rolled each time.** No shared helper exists, so each new integration reinvents the same ~5-line state or forgets to.machine 
3. **Hides in dev testing.** Only surfaces when the model emits *multiple* text items in one turn; short answers don't trigger it. There is no invariant test that would catch the omission at PR review or CI time.

## Proposal

Extract a tiny, sink-agnostic segment-boundary helper, then refactor both existing call sites onto it. Sketch:

Call sites become:

The value isn't saving 5 lines per it's:site 
- **Naming the operation** so the intent (`onSegment(...)`) is visible at every call site, making a missing call easier to spot during review.
- **One place for the invariant test** that locks in the behavior so the pattern can't regress silently.
- **Lower friction** for the next SDK integration to do the right thing by default.

## Scope of work

1. Add `MarkdownSegmentSeparator` (location probably `extensions/copilot/src/util/common/`) with focused unit tests covering: first emission (no separator), same key repeated (no separator), key change (separator), `undefined`-key legacy fallback (no separator), and `reset()`.TBD 
2. Refactor `responsesApi.ts` `OpenAIResponsesProcessor` to use the helper (replace `lastTextDeltaOutputIndex`).
3. Refactor `copilotcliSession.ts` to use the helper (replace `lastEmittedAssistantMessageId` + `maybeEmitMessageSeparator`).
4. **Audit and fix the Claude Code SDK path** (`claudeMessageDispatch.ts` `handleAssistantMessage`) using the same helper, keyed on `:`. This is the third site likely affected; fixing it preemptively is cheap once the helper exists.
5. Migrate the existing tests in `responsesApi.spec.ts` and `copilotcliSession.spec.ts` to remain passing; add a Claude Code SDK test.

## Non-goals

- Changing the separator string (`\n\n` is correct and markdown-safe between blocks).
- Touching the `chunkMessageIds`/`assistantMessageChunks` dedup logic in `copilotcliSession. that's pre-existing and out of scope.ts` 
- Auto-detecting fusion at the chat-rendering layer (heuristic would break legitimate intra-word streaming like `"writ"` + `"ing"`).

## Acceptance

- All three integrations use `MarkdownSegmentSeparator`.
- Existing tests still pass; new tests assert no regression on each site.
- A future new SDK integration that forgets to call `onSegment(...)` is caught either by review (intent is now explicit) or by a written test pattern this issue establishes.

## References

- Original fix for OpenAI Responses API: #312173
- Original fix for Copilot CLI: #319727
- Most recent screenshot of the user-visible bug: in #319727

title: Add a configurable delay for inline suggestions
document:
Add a configurable delay for inline suggestions

I would like the Copilot Inline Suggestions to have an optional configurable delay before requesting an inline suggestion generation after the user stops typing.
I thought the "Eagerness" setting would affect this, but it does not.

The suggestions are normally good if the task is easy - but if you're not sure yet about exactly what you want to do, suggestions pop up too soon, and often with irrelevant content, which gets very distracting.
I don't like using "Snooze" in this case, because I would still like to have shorter, one-line suggestions, but not too fast.

If I could set, for example, a 2 second delay after I stop typing before getting the suggestion, I feel it could fix the problem.
(also, possibly, making "Low" eagerness even less eager, like not adding more lines of code while typing just a single line? I haven't felt a difference between "High" and "Low" eagerness...)

title: [agent-sandbox] Regression: allowRead paths denied due to symlink resolution on macOS
document:
[agent-sandbox] Regression: allowRead paths denied due to symlink resolution on macOS

**Disclaimer:** part of this issue report has been prepared with the help of AI (Github Copilot/Claude Opus), however I have verified the logic and content behind it

- VS Code Version: 1.123.0 (6a44c352bd24569c417e530095901b649960f9f8)
- OS Version: macOS 26.5 (Darwin arm64 25F71)

**Steps to Reproduce:**

1. Have symlinked git dotfiles (a common pattern for dotfile managers):
 
 Where `~/.gitconfig` contains `[include] path = ~/.gitaliases`.

2. Configure `chat.agent.sandbox.fileSystem.mac` to allow both the source and target symlink paths:
 
 (technically only the source should be required after #317198)

3. Enable the sandbox (`"chat.agent.sandbox.enabled": "on"`).

4. Ask the agent to run `git status` (or otherwise read those symlinked files on the allow list) in the terminal.

**Expected:** git succeeds — `~/.gitaliases` and `~/.gitconfig` are explicitly in `allowRead`.

**Actual:** git fails with:

**Suggested root cause — regression from #317198:**

PR #317198 ("Handle sandbox path resolution across platforms") resolves symlinks via `realpath` and **replaces** the configured path with the canonical target.

The generated sandbox settings JSON then contains only the resolved target instead of the configured symlink paths (`~/.gitaliases`, `~/.gitconfig`):

macOS Seatbelt (used by the sandbox) evaluates rules against the **path as passed to the syscall**, not the resolved vnode. When git calls `open("/Users/me/.gitaliases")`, the sandbox checks:

1. `(deny file-read* (subpath "/Users/me"))` — matches → deny
2. `(allow file-read* (subpath "/Users/me/.dotfiles/git/gitaliases.symlink"))` — does NOT match the opened path → no effect
3. Last matching rule → **denied**

**Direct reproduction using the packaged sandbox runtime:**

title: Agent tool `read/problems` on Windows is sensitive to drive letter case. Not stable enough.
document:
Agent tool `read/problems` on Windows is sensitive to drive letter case. Not stable enough.

### Problem
On Windows, when Copilot agent uses the `read/problems` tool, the file path generated by the agent uses a lowercase drive letter (e.g., `c:\`). As a result, the tool fails to find problems for the file. If I change the drive letter to uppercase (e.g., `C:\`), the tool works as expected and finds problems.

### Expected behavior
The file object should be validated and normalized (e.g., standardize to uppercase drive letter or otherwise) within the tools layer. The agent should not have to care about drive letter case. Ideally, the agent's output would not need to adjust for this platform-specific detail.

### Steps to reproduce
1. On Windows, run an agent query that uses `read/problems` with a path like `c:\path\to\file`
2. The tool reports "no problems found"
3. Change the path to `C:\path\to\file` and rerun – now problems are correctly found

### Suggestion
- Tools layer should handle drive letter normalization and validation, so that agents are not required to output platform-specific casing.
- Agent should not need to care about drive letter casing when generating tool input.

### Additional info
- VS Code version: 1.123.0 (latest up to now)
- OS: Windows 11 Pro
- Copilot/Agent version: latest

**Labels:** `bug`, `agent-skills`, `agents`, `agent-behavior`

title: Default Approvals do not prompt for subsequent send_to_terminal inputs after starting an interactive shell, allowing destructive commands without confirmation
document:
Default Approvals do not prompt for subsequent send_to_terminal inputs after starting an interactive shell, allowing destructive commands without confirmation

VS Code: 1.123.0

Copilot Chat version from debug log: 0.51.0
Permission level: Default Approvals

Summary
When using Copilot Chat Agent with Default Approvals, direct terminal commands correctly require confirmation for every tool invocation . However, after approving the launch of an interactive shell (run_in_terminal: pwsh -NoLogo), subsequent **send_to_terminal** inputs into that shell run without additional confirmation, including destructive commands such as Remove-Item.

1. In Copilot Chat Agent, set permission level to Default Approvals.
2. Ask the agent to start an interactive PowerShell shell: pwsh -NoLogo
Confirm the pwsh -NoLogo tool invocation when prompted.
3. Ask/send the following input into that interactive shell: 
 New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path .\copilot-approval-repro-20260604
4. Ask/send the following input into the same shell: 
 Remove-Item -LiteralPath .\copilot-approval-repro-20260604 -Force

Actual Behavior
Only the initial pwsh -NoLogo command required confirmation. The subsequent New-Item and Remove-Item inputs sent via send_to_terminal ran without explicit confirmation. In particular, Remove-Item deleted the test directory without a fresh approval prompt. The same behaviour happens for example in context of ssh user@ssh-server.

Expected Behavior
Under Default Approvals and interactive terminal shells, each send_to_terminal invocation should require confirmation unless explicitly auto-approved by user settings. It should not be implicitly covered by approving the startup of an interactive shell.

Control Test
Running the same operations outside an interactive shell as separate terminal tool invocations does require confirmation each time (probably because each one is called wih "run_in_terminal" instead of "send_to_terminal":
 Test-Path -LiteralPath .\copilot-approval-direct-20260604
 New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path .\copilot-approval-direct-20260604
 Remove-Item -LiteralPath .\copilot-approval-direct-20260604 -Force
Observed result: the user had to confirm all three direct terminal invocations.

title: JS/TS crash notification should suggest checking TypeScript version after accepting workspace TS prompt
document:
JS/TS crash notification should suggest checking TypeScript version after accepting workspace TS prompt

- VS Code Version: 1.123.0
- OS Version: macOS Darwin x64 25.4.0
- Workspace TypeScript Version: 4.9.5
- VS Code Bundled TypeScript Version: 6.0.3
- Framework: Angular 15.2.10
- Workspace setting:

## Summary

VS Code initially used its bundled TypeScript version and showed a prompt asking whether to use the workspace TypeScript version.

After accepting that prompt, VS Code switched to the workspace TypeScript SDK:

In this workspace, that resolves to TypeScript 4.9.5.

Immediately after switching, the JS/TS language service became unusable and crashed repeatedly. The final crash notification suggested that the problem may be caused by a plugin contributed by `GitHub.copilot-chat`.

That guidance was misleading in this case:

- disabling `GitHub.copilot-chat` did not fix the issue
- reproducing with all installed extensions disabled still crashes when workspace TypeScript 4.9.5 is selected
- switching back to `TypeScript: Select TypeScript Version -> Use VS Code's Version` fixes the issue

This issue is about VS Code's crash recovery guidance, not about claiming that Copilot caused the crash.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a workspace with TypeScript 4.9.5 installed locally.
2. Open a TypeScript file.
3. Accept VS Code's prompt to use the workspace TypeScript version.
4. Confirm that TS Server is loaded from:

5. Observe the JS/TS language service crash repeatedly.
6. Disable `GitHub.copilot-chat` and reload.
7. Observe that the crash still happens.
8. Launch VS Code with installed extensions disabled and force the same workspace TypeScript SDK.
9. Observe that the crash still happens.
10. Switch to `TypeScript: Select TypeScript Version -> Use VS Code's Version`.
11. Observe that the JS/TS language service works.

## Actual Result

The JS/TS language service crashes repeatedly and eventually stops restarting.

The notification points to a contributed plugin:

However, the crash also reproduces with installed extensions disabled when the active TS Server is workspace TypeScript 4.9.5.

Crash log excerpt from the extension-disabled reproduction:

Relevant stack frames:

## Expected Result

When the JS/TS language service crashes shortly after the user accepts the workspace TypeScript version prompt, the crash notification should include TypeScript SDK/version selection in the recovery guidance.

For example, it could suggest:

- the active TS Server is running from `node_modules/typescript/lib`
- the selected workspace TypeScript version may be old or incompatible
- try `TypeScript: Select TypeScript Version -> Use VS Code's Version`
- check the `typescript.tsdk` setting

The notification may still mention contributed plugins when relevant, but it should not only point the user toward extensions when the selected workspace TypeScript version is a strong recovery candidate.

## Reproduction Matrix

| Mode | Active TypeScript | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Normal extensions | Workspace TypeScript 4.9.5 | Crashes repeatedly |
| Copilot Chat disabled | Workspace TypeScript 4.9.5 | Still crashes |
| Installed extensions disabled | Workspace TypeScript 4.9.5 | Still crashes |
| Installed extensions disabled | VS Code bundled TypeScript 6.0.3 | Works |
| Normal extensions | VS Code bundled TypeScript 6.0.3 | Works |

## How I Verified the Extension-Disabled Cases

I used isolated VS Code profiles so the normal profile state would not affect the result.

Workspace TypeScript 4.9.5 reproduction settings:

Bundled TypeScript control settings:

The extension-disabled workspace TypeScript run logged:

and then failed on `updateOpen`.

The extension-disabled bundled TypeScript run logged:

and `updateOpen` completed successfully without `Debug Failure` or repeated `TSServer exited` messages.

## Why this belongs in microsoft/vscode

The underlying `Debug Failure` may be a TypeScript 4.9.5 server bug.

However, the VS Code user experience is the issue here:

1. VS Code prompted me to switch to the workspace TypeScript version.
2. After accepting that prompt, IntelliSense / JS/TS language service became unusable.
3. The crash notification pointed me toward Copilot/plugin troubleshooting.
4. The actual recovery path was switching back to VS Code's bundled TypeScript version.

So the request is for better crash diagnostics and recovery guidance in VS Code.

## Additional Notes

In the extension-disabled reproduction, the TS Server arguments did not include Copilot/global TypeScript plugins. The workspace TypeScript 4.9.5 server still failed on `updateOpen`.

I can provide redacted TS Server logs if needed. The raw logs include local workspace paths and may include source-related details.

title: Some Azure Foundry models do not load Skills in VS Code Copilot Chat
document:
Some Azure Foundry models do not load Skills in VS Code Copilot Chat

* Copilot Chat Extension Version:

* Logs:

Result:

* GPT-5.1 → Skills are loaded correctly
* GPT-5.2 → Skills are not loaded
* GPT-5.4-mini → Skills are not loaded

title: [Remote-SSH] Extension icons/fonts (SVG/WOFF2) blocked by CORS policy after recent update (vscode-managed-remote-resource)
document:
[Remote-SSH] Extension icons/fonts (SVG/WOFF2) blocked by CORS policy after recent update (vscode-managed-remote-resource)

### Bug Description
After updating to the latest version of VS Code, many extension icons and fonts (e.g., CMake Tools, Todo Tree, GitLens, Gerrit) are completely missing when working in a Remote-SSH workspace. 

Checking the Developer Tools console reveals that the underlying `vscode-managed-remote-resource://` protocol is being blocked by a strict CORS policy. This appears to be a regression bug related to webview or remote resource security policies.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Update VS Code to the latest version.
2. Connect to a remote Linux machine using the `Remote - SSH` extension.
3. Open a remote project.
4. Observe the Activity Bar and Editor UI: specific extension icons (like Todo Tree, CMake) are missing or broken.
5. Open Developer Tools (`Cmd+Option+I`) and look at the Console.

### Logs / Error Messages
Here are the exact errors from the DevTools console:

Access to image at 'vscode-managed-remote-resource://window:1/home/.../.vscode-server/extensions/gruntfuggly.todo-tree-0.0.226/resources/todo-tree-container.svg?authority=ssh-remote%2Bdev&scheme=vscode-remote' from origin 'vscode-file://vscode-app' has been blocked by CORS policy: Cross origin requests are only supported for protocol schemes: chrome, chrome-extension, chrome-untrusted, data, http, https, vscode-file, vscode-remote-resource, vscode-webview.

Failed to load resource: net::ERR_FAILED

Access to font at 'vscode-managed-remote-resource://window:1/home/.../.vscode-server/extensions/ms-vscode.cmake-tools-1.23.52/res/product-icons.woff2?authority=ssh-remote%2Bdev&scheme=vscode-remote' from origin 'vscode-file://vscode-app' has been blocked by CORS policy.

### Environment
* **Local OS:** macOS
* **Remote OS:** Linux (SSH)
* **Extensions affected:** Todo Tree, CMake Tools, GitLens, Gerrit, etc.

Remote OS version: Linux x64 5.4.0-216-generic

title: Debug: Silent data loss when stored breakpoints/watch expressions are corrupted
document:
Debug: Silent data loss when stored breakpoints/watch expressions are corrupted

### Problem

In `src/vs/workbench/contrib/debug/common/debugStorage.ts`, five methods load debug data from storage using `JSON.parse()`, but all five use **empty catch blocks** — silently swallowing any parsing or construction errors:

- `loadBreakpoints()` — line 83 — Stores user breakpoints
- `loadFunctionBreakpoints()` — line 94 — Stores function breakpoints
- `loadExceptionBreakpoints()` — line 105 — Stores exception breakpoints
- `loadDataBreakpoints()` — line 116 — Stores data breakpoints
- `loadWatchExpressions()` — line 127 — Stores watch expressions

If stored data gets corrupted (sync conflicts, disk errors, crash mid-write, or a bug in serialization), **every single user breakpoint, function breakpoint, exception breakpoint, data breakpoint, and watch expression is silently dropped** with zero indication to the user or developer. The data simply vanishes.

This class already has `ILogService` injected via the constructor (`this.logService`), but it is unused in the catch blocks.

### Why this matters

Users may lose their entire debugging setup without any notification. This is especially painful for complex debug configurations with many breakpoints. The user has no way to know that their data was corrupted — they'll just see all breakpoints gone and assume they were never set.

### Fix

Replace empty catch blocks with `this.logService.error()` calls that record the failure. This ensures:

1. The error is visible in the developer console and logs
2. Users/developers can diagnose why breakpoints disappeared
3. The behavior is unchanged (returns empty array on failure)
4. The fix is minimal, low-risk, and uses existing infrastructure

### File

`src/vs/workbench/contrib/debug/common/debugStorage.ts`

title: Performance: Cache getComputedStyle result in shadowCaretRangeFromPoint (mouse hot path)
document:
Performance: Cache getComputedStyle result in shadowCaretRangeFromPoint (mouse hot path)

### Problem

In `src/vs/editor/browser/controller/mouseTarget.ts:1136-1142`, the `shadowCaretRangeFromPoint` function calls `getComputedStyle(el, null)` **six separate times** to retrieve individual CSS font properties (`font-style`, `font-variant`, `font-weight`, `font-size`, `line-height`, `font-family`) and then concatenates them into a font shorthand string.

Each `getComputedStyle()` call forces a **synchronous style recalculation**, which is expensive. Since this function runs on every mouse move over the editor viewport's shadow DOM content, the overhead is multiplied across every mousemove event in the editor.

### Fix

Cache the `getComputedStyle()` result once in a local variable, then call `.getPropertyValue()` on the cached result:

### Impact

- **6x reduction** in forced style recalculations on every mouse move over editor content
- No functional change — identical output, same behavior
- Low-risk, one-line addition, single file change

### File

`src/vs/editor/browser/controller/mouseTarget.ts` — `shadowCaretRangeFromPoint` function

### Related

This is a well-known browser performance best practice — MDN recommends caching `getComputedStyle()` results when reading multiple properties: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Window/getComputedStyle#notes

title: 2026 themes: XML attribute names and values use the same foreground color
document:
2026 themes: XML attribute names and values use the same foreground color

**Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?:** Yes

**VS Code Version:** 1.123.0
**OS Version:** Windows 11

**Steps to Reproduce:**

1. Open any XML/MSBuild file (e.g. a `Directory.Build.props` containing ``).
2. Select the **Dark 2026** color theme.
3. Compare with **Dark Modern** or **Dark+**.

**Expected:** Tag names, attribute names, and attribute string values are visually distinguishable, as they are in Dark Modern / Dark+.

**Actual:** In Dark 2026 (or Light 2026), the following TextMate scopes all render with the same foreground color, making XML/MSBuild hard to read:

- `entity.name.tag.localname.xml`
- `entity.other.attribute-name.xml` / `entity.other.attribute-name.localname.xml`
- `string.quoted.double.xml`

Verified via **Developer: Inspect Editor Tokens and Scopes** — the scopes are distinct, but the theme's `tokenColors` does not assign them different foregrounds.

**Suggested fix:** In `extensions/theme-defaults/themes/2026-dark.json` (and `2026-light.json` for parity), add `tokenColors` entries that distinguish XML tag names, attribute names, and quoted strings — mirroring how `dark_modern.json` / `dark_plus.json` handle these scopes, but using Dark 2026's palette.

Adding this to my settings.json fixes it for me on Dark 2026:

title: Reasoning/thinking parameters not forwarded to extension-contributed model providers
document:
Reasoning/thinking parameters not forwarded to extension-contributed model providers

### Environment

- **Copilot Chat Extension Version:** (current main)
- **VS Code Version:** (current main)
- **OS Version:** cross-platform
- **Feature:** agent mode (tool calling loop with reasoning/thinking)
- **Selected model:** extension-contributed models (custom providers via VS Code Language Model API)

### Steps to Reproduce:

1. Register a custom language model provider via `vscode.lm.createLanguageModelChatProvider`
2. Configure the model with `reasoningEffort` in the model configuration
3. Use the model in Copilot agent mode
4. Observe that `provideLanguageModelChatResponse` in the extension provider never receives `enableThinking` or `reasoningEffort` in `modelOptions`

### Expected behavior:

When the Copilot agent enables thinking for a request, `enableThinking` and `reasoningEffort` should be forwarded through `modelOptions` to the underlying extension-contributed model provider, so the provider can apply them to the actual API call.

### Actual behavior:

`extChatEndpoint.makeChatRequest2()` silently drops `modelCapabilities` from `IMakeChatRequestOptions`. The `modelOptions` passed to `languageModel.sendRequest()` only contain internal tracing IDs (`_capturingTokenCorrelationId`, `_otelTraceContext`), never `enableThinking` or `reasoningEffort`.

### Root cause:

`extChatEndpoint.ts` deconstructs `IMakeChatRequestOptions` but never reads `modelCapabilities`. The fix forwards `enableThinking` and `reasoningEffort` into `modelOptions` so they reach the provider via the VS Code API.

### Impact:

- **Extension-contributed models**: Never receive reasoning/thinking settings
- **BYOK models**: Work correctly (use `OpenAIEndpoint`, which reads `modelCapabilities`)
- **Cloud models**: Work correctly (use `OpenAIEndpoint`/`responsesApi`)

title: Hard to discover no-code themes in Marketplace search
document:
Hard to discover no-code themes in Marketplace search

This post is intended to draw attention to the inconvenience described in the Problem section. The feature alternative I share in the Suggestion section is offered only as a starting point. The Context is to prevent misinterpretation. 

**Context**

- Some themes don't execute code; some do.
- In [#222024](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/222024), @sandy081 classified such extensions as "no-code" and proposed a feature to warn users before updating an extension from a no-code version to one that contains code-executing logic. This feature produces messages such as:
 > The update for \ extension introduces executable code, which is not present in the currently installed version. Please \[review the extension] and update it manually.
- Officially distinguishing themes that execute code from those that don't is a welcome step, but the problem deserves greater support. 
- Organizational VS Code users are often required to seek approval before installing software on their work machines. This measure is typically enforced to mitigate supply chain attacks or potential failures, so it is usually non-negotiable.
- For users whose organizations impose such restrictions, a no-code theme is almost always preferred since authors can rarely justify the decision to ship code with themes.
- I expect organizational users with such constraints to make up a significant proportion of VS Code users.

**Problem**

The users who are restricted to no-code themes cannot quickly find a suitable option.

**Suggestion**

One possible direction:

- Implement changes in both the Marketplace and VS Code.
- VS Code should let users apply filters to narrow theme search results to no-code options.
- The Marketplace should maintain a record of each theme extension's no-code status.
- The Marketplace API should support "no-code" filter in search queries.

**Workaround**

I navigate from the extension's page to the theme's repository and check whether folders include `src` or similar.

**Considerations**

I believe using the internal term "no-code" in user-facing contexts would carry a negative connotation. Adopting a term like "simple themes" might instead give authors more room to explain to visitors why their extension is not classified as such.

title: Terminal auto-approve settings ignored on macOS but work on Windows (1.122.1)
document:
Terminal auto-approve settings ignored on macOS but work on Windows (1.122.1)

## Environment

- **VS Code version:** 1.122.1 (also reproduced on VS Code Insiders latest)
- **OS (broken):** macOS (arm64, Apple Silicon)
- **OS (working):** Windows (same VS Code version, same settings)
- **Copilot Chat extension:** latest

## Problem

Terminal auto-approve settings are completely ignored on macOS. Every terminal command triggered by Copilot agent mode shows the "Run zsh command?" confirmation dialog, regardless of settings. The identical configuration works correctly on Windows - commands auto-approve without any dialog.

## Settings (identical on both machines)

**User settings (`settings.json`):**

**Workspace settings (`.vscode/settings.json`):** Same terminal auto-approve object, plus `chat.permissions.default: "autoApprove"`.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open VS Code on macOS
2. Configure the settings above (both user and workspace level)
3. Reload window
4. Open Copilot agent mode chat
5. Ask: "Run `echo test`"
6. **Expected:** Command runs without any confirmation dialog
7. **Actual:** "Run zsh command?" dialog appears requiring manual click

## Diagnostic data

Queried the VS Code state database (`globalStorage/state.vscdb`):

All three prerequisite flags are correctly set in storage. No enterprise MDM profiles are installed (`profiles list` returns empty). No VS Code profiles configured. Workspace trust is enabled and the folder is trusted.

## Additional context

- No enterprise policies (`~/Library/Application Support/Code/User/policies/` does not exist)
- `security.workspace.trust.enabled: true` with workspace trusted
- Tested with simple commands (`echo "test"`, `ls`) that are explicitly in the approve list
- Also tested with env-prefixed commands (`FDR_ENV=qa echo "test"`) where `FDR_ENV` is in the approve list
- Reproduced on both VS Code Stable 1.122.1 and VS Code Insiders (latest as of June 3, 2026)
- Works perfectly on Windows with the same settings and same VS Code version

This appears to be a macOS-specific code path issue where the terminal auto-approve check either fails to read the settings correctly or applies additional restrictions not present on Windows.

title: Github Copilot Chat will not complete activation

title: Remote-SSH: Connection times out on macOS due to listen EINVAL
document:
Remote-SSH: Connection times out on macOS due to listen EINVAL

- VS Code Version: 1.123.0
- Remote-SSH Version: remote-ssh@0.123.0
- OS Version: macOS Sequoia / Sonoma (darwin arm64)
- Remote OS Version: WSL2 (Ubuntu 22.04.2 LTS)

### Context / Scenario
The issue occurs when attempting to establish a Remote-SSH connection from a macOS client to a WSL2 instance running on a remote machine. 

Local SSH authentication is configured via SSH Public Key (Key-based authentication). Although the SSH key authentication itself succeeds and successfully logs into the remote WSL instance, the connection is prematurely terminated and times out due to a local macOS system limitation regarding UNIX domain socket path lengths.

### Steps to Reproduce:

1. Open VS Code on macOS.
2. Ensure the system default `$TMPDIR` is relatively long (standard macOS sandbox/user path template under `/var/folders/.../T/`).
3. Attempt to connect to a remote host using the Remote-SSH extension.
4. The connection logs show successful remote authentication, but the local helper process immediately throws a `listen EINVAL` error and times out after a few seconds.

---

### Core Log Context
Here is the raw sequence from the Remote-SSH output log showing the initialization, the socket failure, the successful SSH publickey auth, and the ultimate timeout:

```text
[01:00:43.083] SSH Resolver called for host: wsl-remote
[01:00:43.088] Acquiring local install lock: /var/folders/x6/4s02g9sx0hbg3kpb6973708c0000gn/T/vscode-remote-ssh-22ba18ea-install.lock
...
[01:00:43.106] askpass server listening on /var/folders/x6/4s02g9sx0hbg3kpb6973708c0000gn/T/vscode-ssh-askpass-0cd1ad7e276cbbc4e4dbb86a937493564c243c06.sock
[01:00:43.107] Spawning local server with {"serverId":1,"ipcHandlePath":"/var/folders/x6/4s02g9sx0hbg3kpb6973708c0000gn/T/vscode-ssh-askpass-f51bb763c655498631fceb8c48a21c27762546fc.sock", ...}

[01:00:43.164] > local-server-1> Running ssh connection command: ssh -v -T -D 51619 -o ConnectTimeout=15 wsl-remote
[01:00:43.166] stderr> local-server-1> listen EINVAL: invalid argument /var/folders/x6/4s02g9sx0hbg3kpb6973708c0000gn/T/vscode-ssh-askpass-f51bb763c655498631fceb8c48a21c27762546fc.sock

[01:00:43.353] stderr> Authenticated to ([]:22) using "publickey".
[01:00:43.391] > Welcome to Ubuntu 22.04.2 LTS (GNU/Linux 6.6.114.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2 x86_64)
...
[01:00:43.610] Server delay-shutdown request failed: connect EINVAL /var/folders/x6/4s02g9sx0hbg3kpb6973708c0000gn/T/vscode-ssh-askpass-f51bb763c655498631fceb8c48a21c27762546fc.sock - Local (undefined:undefined)
[01:00:45.610] Server delay-shutdown request failed: connect EINVAL /var/folders/x6/4s02g9sx0hbg3kpb6973708c0000gn/T/vscode-ssh-askpass-f51bb763c655498631fceb8c48a21c27762546fc.sock - Local (undefined:undefined)
...
[01:00:48.167] > local-server-1> Timed out
[01:00:48.177] Local server exit: 0

title: Increase maximum allowed scm (git) gutter width
document:
Increase maximum allowed scm (git) gutter width

Currently, the gutter width can be set to a maximum of 5 in the `settings.json`:

Is there a (mandatory) technical reason to allow a max width of 5? I, myself, find even 5 rather hard to spot and would really like to increase this to 8 or even 10 to make the git gutter icon easier to spot.

This here is an example for the **maximum** size of 5 for the gutter icon of a modified line (yellow icon):

The default icon would be much thinner. 5 is easy to spot if you are currently focused on a specific function/class but when you scroll over code it is easy to miss still.

Thus, I propose an increased maximum gutter size such that "blind people" like myself can set it to 8 or even 10.

title: Remote SSH: since update, "Settings up SSH Host [hostname]" appears in toast notification every 5 seconds.
document:
Remote SSH: since update, "Settings up SSH Host [hostname]" appears in toast notification every 5 seconds.

Since updating to the latest VSCode version `1.123.0`, when connected to a remote host with Remote-SSH, every five seconds I get the toast message, `Settings up SSH Host [hostname]`. Then it resolves. My active terminals remain open, but freeze / pause every five seconds as focus is stolen. Really annoying!

I have tested this across three different remote hosts since the update, and the same behavior occurs on all three remote hosts. I have tried deleting the `.vscode-server` directory on the remote, forcing a fresh download, and the same behavior continues.

- VS Code Version: 1.123.0 (Universal)
- OS Version: MacOS Tahoe 26.5

1. Upgrade to the latest VSCode version
2. Connect to a Remote host via SSH

The [builds](https://update.code.visualstudio.com/1.123.0/linux-x64/stable) on the website are for `1.123.0`, the [GitHub release](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/releases/tag/1.123) is for `1.123.0`, but the [git tag](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/releases/tag/1.123) is for `1.123`.

title: API: Per-panel minimumWidth for webview panels
document:
API: Per-panel minimumWidth for webview panels

# API proposal: per-panel `minimumWidth` on `WebviewPanelOptions`

*Companion API proposal to #311289 (notebook editor scroll state) — both came out of building the [`notebook-minimap`](https://github.com/EricPutney/vscode-notebook-minimap) extension.*

## Problem

The workbench enforces a default minimum editor width of **220 px** (`DEFAULT_EDITOR_MIN_DIMENSIONS.width`) that applies uniformly to every editor pane, including webview-hosted panels created via `vscode.window.createWebviewPanel`. For webview content that's usable at narrower widths, there's no way to opt below this floor.

Concrete cases where 220 px is wrong:

- **Editor-beside minimaps** — the built-in text-editor minimap is ~80–120 px wide. An extension-side minimap that lives in a webview column has to be 220 px minimum, so the user can't drag it to the same narrow proportion.
- **Thin outline / scrollbar widgets** — review panels, progress bars, diff ribbons that only need a thin column.
- **Notification / log ribbons** beside content.

Workarounds extension authors currently resort to:

- Rendering empty padding in the webview to hide the wasted width (cosmetic, doesn't recover the space).
- Moving the panel to the Secondary Side Bar — different UX, different placement semantics, no longer colocated with the editor content it's about.
- Patching VS Code locally (what `notebook-minimap` has been doing).

## Proposed API

Add a single optional field to the existing `vscode.WebviewPanelOptions` interface, behind a proposal flag so stable-API extensions don't gain access until the proposal stabilizes:

## Example usage

Extension also adds the proposal to its `package.json`:

An opted-out extension that sets `minimumWidth` throws at `createWebviewPanel` time via `checkProposedApiEnabled`.

## Use cases beyond minimaps

- **Narrow inline-review widgets** adjacent to code (≤ 120 px, just enough for a comment count + hover-to-expand).
- **Scroll-position indicators** for long documents that present as a thin strip beside the editor.
- **Progress / activity ribbons** for long-running extension tasks, rendered as a slim column rather than a full notification panel.
- **Colorpicker / palette accessories** that an extension wants docked beside a file but not taking 220 px of horizontal space.
- **Collaboration presence strips** — avatars, cursor indicators — in pair-programming extensions.

## Implementation

Six-file threading, each edit small:

1. **`src/vscode-dts/vscode.proposed.webviewPanelMinimumWidth.d.ts`** — new proposed-API declaration.
2. **`src/vs/workbench/api/common/extHost.protocol.ts`** — add `minimumWidth?: number` to `IWebviewPanelOptions` (internal wire type, not API-gated).
3. **`src/vs/workbench/api/common/extHostWebviewPanels.ts`** — `serializeWebviewPanelOptions` now takes `(extension, options)` and calls `checkProposedApiEnabled(extension, 'webviewPanelMinimumWidth')` when `options.minimumWidth !== undefined`. Call site in `createWebviewPanel` passes `extension`.
4. **`src/vs/workbench/contrib/webview/browser/webview.ts`** — `WebviewOptions` (main-thread struct) gains the optional field.
5. **`src/vs/workbench/api/browser/mainThreadWebviewPanels.ts`** — `reviveWebviewOptions` forwards the field.
6. **`src/vs/workbench/contrib/webviewPanel/browser/webviewEditor.ts`** — `override get minimumWidth()`: returns `this.input.webview.options.minimumWidth` when set, else `DEFAULT_EDITOR_MIN_DIMENSIONS.width`. Only affects the specific `WebviewEditor` pane whose input carries the option; other editors keep their own minimums.

Persistence is automatic — the existing `$registerWebview.panelOptions: webviewInput.webview.options` path already serializes the full `WebviewOptions` for window-reload backup, so `minimumWidth` rides along with no extra plumbing.

Runtime cost: one extra field read per pane dimension query. Negligible.

## Alternatives considered

- **Global setting to lower `DEFAULT_EDITOR_MIN_DIMENSIONS.width`** — affects all editors (text editors included). Most text-editor content actually needs the 220 px floor for legibility; lowering globally produces unusable splits.
- **Override `WebviewEditor.minimumWidth` unconditionally** — affects *every* webview panel, including Markdown preview / image viewer / custom editor webviews that may legitimately need the 220 px minimum. Too broad.
- **Move to `contributes.views.*` with the secondary side bar API** — different UX. Side-bar views have their own width affordances and live outside the editor group; users can't place them "beside" a specific document column.
- **Work around with padding** — doesn't recover the wasted space, doesn't match the visual of a truly thin panel.

A per-panel opt-in is the minimum-surface answer: extensions that know their webview is legible below 220 px opt in; everyone else keeps the existing behavior unchanged.

## Design notes

- **`readonly minimumWidth?: number`** on an existing stable interface rather than a new options-bag type — matches precedent for `enableFindWidget` and `retainContextWhenHidden`. Requires `?` for back-compat on `WebviewPanelOptions`.
- **Gated at `serializeWebviewPanelOptions`** rather than at `createWebviewPanel` directly so the check runs once per panel creation, close to where the field is read, with access to the calling extension's descriptor.
- **Declared behind a proposal even though the field is a trivial optional** — follows the process guideline that all new API surface starts proposed regardless of size, so it gets one iteration of real-world feedback before becoming permanent.
- **Not exposed on `WebviewView`** (the sidebar counterpart) — sidebar view widths are governed by a different layout path (user drag + persisted per-view), so the semantics aren't identical. Leave that to a separate proposal if there's demand.

## Validation

A Mocha suite following `extensions/vscode-api-tests` conventions exercises the API end-to-end:

- `createWebviewPanel` accepts `minimumWidth: N` without throwing.
- Back-compat: omitting the field continues to work.
- Multiple values (1, 50, 100, 220, 500) are accepted.

Not exercised from within the test extension (which has the proposal enabled): the opt-out-extension-throws path. That path is the `checkProposedApiEnabled` gate in `extHostWebviewPanels.ts::serializeWebviewPanelOptions` and is best covered by a narrow unit test alongside the PR.

## Back-compat

Fully additive. Existing extensions that don't set `minimumWidth` see the current 220 px floor unchanged. Wire-format addition is a single optional field — old ext hosts running against a new renderer (or vice versa) silently ignore it.

## Prior art / sample consumer

[`notebook-minimap`](https://github.com/EricPutney/vscode-notebook-minimap) requests `minimumWidth: 50` to let the user drag the minimap column down to roughly the width of a built-in text-editor minimap. Before this proposal, the extension shipped with a local VS Code patch doing exactly the change described here.

## Reference implementation

A drop-in implementation is available at [`EricPutney/vscode:proposal/webviewPanelMinimumWidth`](https://github.com/EricPutney/vscode/tree/proposal/webviewPanelMinimumWidth) for reviewers who want to skim the code during API-shape discussion. Will be opened as a PR once this issue gets an ack from the API owner.

## Narrower scope vs. the other three proposals

Intentionally the smallest of the four API proposals I'm opening alongside the notebook-minimap work — a single optional field, a well-localized implementation, and no open design trade-offs. If the API call prefers to land this separately from the scroll/theming proposals, it's independent of all three.

title: API: Theme-resolved token-category colors on ColorTheme
document:
API: Theme-resolved token-category colors on ColorTheme

# API proposal: theme-resolved foreground colors on `ColorTheme`

*Companion API proposal to #311289 (notebook editor scroll state) — both came out of building the [`notebook-minimap`](https://github.com/EricPutney/vscode-notebook-minimap) extension.*

Related: #32813 (general ask for programmatic access to a theme's `tokenColors`) and [#319753](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/319753), companion request for API access to document tokens.

## Problem

Extensions that render UI outside a `TextDocument` but want colors matching the editor's theme — synthetic labels, legends, category pills, decorative separators, heading bars, outline chips — have no way to ask for "the keyword color" or "the string color" of the active theme.

## Proposed API

Values are resolved from representative TextMate scopes (most-specific first, fallback to general):

| category | scopes probed |
| --------- | --- |
| keyword | `keyword.control`, `keyword` |
| string | `string` |
| number | `constant.numeric`, `constant` |
| comment | `comment` |
| function | `entity.name.function`, `support.function` |
| type | `entity.name.type`, `support.type`, `storage.type` |
| variable | `variable` |
| operator | `keyword.operator`, `keyword` |
| constant | `constant.language`, `constant` |

`default` is `editor.foreground`.

Updated via the existing `window.onDidChangeActiveColorTheme` event — no new event needed.

## Example usage

A minimap that renders markdown heading bars in the theme's own keyword color:

## Implementation

Main-thread `MainThreadTheming.onDidColorThemeChange` now resolves the 9 probes through the existing `ColorThemeData.resolveScopes` method (cast through `unknown` because it's on the impl class, not the `IColorTheme` interface) and ships the result as a second argument to `$onColorThemeChange` alongside the existing `type` string. `ExtHostTheming` stores it on the `ColorTheme` instance it hands back via `window.activeColorTheme`.

Runtime cost: 9 `resolveScopes` calls per theme change event, each a shallow lookup over the theme's tokenColors rules. Negligible.

## Back-compat

Additive on a stable type. The `tokenColors` field is optional (`?`) so implementations predating the proposal simply don't carry it. No wire-format or event shape changes.

## Prior art / sample consumer

[`notebook-minimap`](https://github.com/EricPutney/vscode-notebook-minimap) uses `tokenColors.keyword` / `string` / `number` / `comment` for non-document UI rendering (markdown heading bars, output stream distinguishers). Alongside `documentTokenColors`, which covers actual document content.

## Reference implementation

A drop-in implementation is available at [`EricPutney/vscode:proposal/themeTokenColors`](https://github.com/EricPutney/vscode/tree/proposal/themeTokenColors)

## Relationship to `documentTokenColors`

The two proposals serve different needs:

- `documentTokenColors` in [#319753](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/319753) addresses "what would the editor paint for *this text in this document*?"
- `themeTokenColors` addresses "what does the active theme think a *category of thing* looks like, independent of any document?"

title: API: Expose document tokenization with theme-resolved token colors
document:
API: Expose document tokenization with theme-resolved token colors

# API proposal: expose document tokenization with theme-resolved colors

*Companion API proposal to #311289 (notebook editor scroll state) — both came out of building the [`notebook-minimap`](https://github.com/EricPutney/vscode-notebook-minimap) extension.*

Related: #32813 — long-standing ask for programmatic access to a theme's `tokenColors`. That issue is about reading the theme's rules; this proposal is about getting the resolved per-token foreground for a specific document (i.e. after tokenization + scope matching).

## Problem

Extensions that render tokenized text outside the editor e.g. minimaps can't match the editor's syntax highlighting.

## Proposed API

## Example usage

A minimap that paints each character in the color the editor would:

## Implementation

Sourced from `TextModel.tokenization.getLineTokens(lineNumber)` + `TokenizationRegistry.getColorMap()` — the same pipeline the editor uses to paint text. For documents whose `ITextModel` isn't loaded (notebook cells load lazily), the main-thread implementation uses `ITextModelService.createModelReference` to force resolution, releasing the reference after.

Tokenization cost: TextMate grammars typically tokenize at ~50k lines/sec, so a 1000-line doc ≈ 20 ms. Callers should cache per document version.

## Back-compat

Fully additive: new namespace function, new result types. No changes to existing language / document / tokenization APIs.

## Prior art / sample consumer

[`notebook-minimap`](https://github.com/EricPutney/vscode-notebook-minimap) uses this API per-cell to paint each character in the editor's resolved color; tokens stream via an internal `setCellTokens` message as they land.

## Reference implementation

A drop-in implementation is available at [`EricPutney/vscode:proposal/documentTokenColors`](https://github.com/EricPutney/vscode/tree/proposal/documentTokenColors).

title: editor.inactiveLineHighlightBackground does not work when navigating problems panel and global or local search results
document:
editor.inactiveLineHighlightBackground does not work when navigating problems panel and global or local search results

**Issue**
The background color set in **editor.inactiveLineHighlightBackground** 
or the border color set in **editor.lineHighlightBorder**
does not work ( ie., the relevant line in the editor does not show the selected color but the default faint grey background color only )
when
- navigating the list of errors or warnings in the problem pane
- navigating the results in the global search side bar
- moving through results of ineditor search widget

the colour shows properly when clicked on panel or side bar, but we start to navigate in problem panel or side bar items - 
then the settings colour disappears and the default faint grey colour is used.

**editor.LineHighlightBackground** also does not work when focus is moved back to editor by triggering **workbench.action.focusActiveEditorGroup** after navigating.
this colour comes into effect only after pressing any cursor keys or inputting a character

Please fix the issue - as the faint grey colour is very difficult to see
making difficult to spot in which line error/ warnings/ matches are present when navigating errors or search results 
causing eye strain and fatigue

- VS Code Version: 1.122..1
- OS Version: Linux Mint 21.3

1. set the following in settings.json ( bright colours choosen for easier visibility )

2. Use a codebase that has errors and warnings or purposefully induce them
3. Navigate errors & warning from problems panel 
4. also try navigating results of global or local search

__Results__
__general highlight__
normal line highlight works fine ( red brown colour )

clicking on problems panel shows inactive line colour properly ( green ) 

__navigating problems panel__
on navigating errors, green background highlight to be seen - but faint grey colour is used as background

same with navigating warning/ info messages

when editor is back in focus triggered **workbench.action.focusActiveEditorGroup** through keyboard shortcut
reb-brown colour highlight should have been seen( **editor.lineHighlightBackground** not seen ), rather only the same faint grey colour seen, ( cursor is visible properly though )

__navigating global search results sidebar__
same issue when navigating global search results ( editor.inactiveLineHighlightBackground not seen )

now active back in focus ( editor.lineHighlightBackground not seen )

__navigating local search results ineditor widget__
( navigating local search results ( editor.inactiveLineHighlightBackground not seen )

now active back in focus ( editor.lineHighlightBackground not seen )

------
please consider fixing the issue as spotting faint grey on black background is causing eye strain and fatigue
Thanks.

title: Warn users when they are breaking the cache
document:
Warn users when they are breaking the cache

We need to warn users when they are breaking the cache:
* If they swtich a model in a long agentic turn (context used > 50%)
* They come back to a session after 1h for Anthropic, 24h for OpenAI

We could use our tips UI to show this warning.

Bhavya - starting with you, but feel free to assign back to me.

This is important so we educate our users on how to get more out of their copilot usage.

title: "Files" view in Agent Window errors out after one prompt
document:
"Files" view in Agent Window errors out after one prompt

Window: Agents

2. Open the Agent window

title: VS Code crashes on startup with TypeError when http.noProxy is a string
document:
VS Code crashes on startup with TypeError when http.noProxy is a string

- VS Code Version: 1.123.0 (stable), also reproduced on 1.124.0-insider (arm64)
- OS Version: macOS (darwin, arm64)

1. Create a minimal `User/settings.json` with only:
 
2. Launch VS Code with a clean user data dir:
 
3. VS Code crashes immediately in the main process with no user-visible error, only a console trace:

 

`http.noProxy` is typed as `string[]`, but when a plain string is provided instead of an array, the setting is silently accepted without validation and causes an unrecoverable crash in the main process. The `|| []` fallback in the code guards against `undefined`/`null` but does not handle other invalid types like a string. A misconfigured setting should not result in a hard crash — a schema validation warning or graceful fallback would prevent this.

title: [major] v1.123 breaks model-selection ability when resubmitting prompt
document:
[major] v1.123 breaks model-selection ability when resubmitting prompt

Regression: v1.123 breaks the ability to change the selected model with resubmitting a prompt. In this version the model used when resubmitting a prompt is no longer the model selected in the active editing field for the resubmitted prompt, but apparently erroneously tied to the "new prompt" selected model.

1. Submit a prompt in chat with Claude Opus 4.8, such as "Sing me a song." Wait for the response to complete.
 - Note in the response summary that the model used was Claude Opus 4.8.
 - Note also that the field for a _new_ prompt also indicates Claude Opus 4.8, the same model that was just used.
2. Click the submitted prompt to edit it. Do not change the text, but change the model to GPT-5.5 and resubmit the prompt. Wait for the response to complete.
3. Look at the model that was used for the resubmitted prompt.

**Expected:** GPT-5.5 was used for the resubmitted prompt.

**Actual:** Claude Opus 4.8 was used for the resubmitted prompt.

**Conclusion:** Even though the model was was changed to GPT-5.5 when resubmitting the prompt, GitHub Copilot ignored the newly selected model and resubmitted the prompt with the _originally_ selected model.

**Workaround:** Apparently the resubmitted prompt is no longer tied to the originally selected model, but to the selected model of the field for the _new_ prompt. So you must change the model in the "new prompt" field before attempting to edit a submitted prompt.

**Discussion:** Obviously this interrupts the workflow if you want to resubmit a prompt with a different model. Remembering to update the _new prompt_ field is counterintuitive. It adds extra steps, and when you forget it burns credits. In addition this is an obvious regression; it worked before, and with the current bug the model selection UI when resubmitting a prompt serves no purpose, as it is broken.

GitHub Copilot Markdown paste discards HTML tag from inline code

The v1.122 series (and maybe before) has a facility to copy and paste the LLM output as Markdown. That's nice, but it has some bugs.

1. Coax the LLM to output the sentence:
 > The HTML has an `` in the source code.
2 Copy that sentence from the LLM output and paste it into a the prompt field, which now accepts Markdown.

**Expected**

> The HTML has an `` in the source code.

**Actual**

> The HTML has an `` in the source code.

You'll notice that the `` has been dropped.

**Analysis**

My best guess from developer experience (without looking at the source code) is that some of the many layers of conversions and transformations is trying to render `` as HTML embedded in Markdown, which gets rendered as nothing. Just a rough guess; I'm sure there are many other possibilities.

title: Extension API to programmatically delete chat sessions
document:
Extension API to programmatically delete chat sessions

## Description

There is currently no extension API to programmatically delete chat sessions. The internal `IChatService.removeHistoryEntry()` handles this correctly (deleting JSONL files, updating `state.cache`, `model.cache`, and `ChatSessionStore.index`), but it is not exposed to extensions.

## Problem

When building an extension that needs to manage chat session lifecycle (e.g., bulk cleanup of old sessions), the only option is to delete the JSONL files directly from `workspaceStorage/*/chatSessions/`. However, this causes several issues:

1. **Sidebar ghosts**: Deleted JSONL files leave orphan entries in `agentSessions.model.cache` and `chat.ChatSessionStore.index` in `state.vscdb`. These appear in the sidebar but cannot be opened.

2. **`state.vscdb` cannot be modified externally**: VS Code caches `state.vscdb` in memory at startup and writes it back on shutdown, overwriting any external changes. This means extensions (or external tools) cannot clean up the orphan entries.

3. **No self-healing**: VS Code does not verify that JSONL files exist for entries in `model.cache` or `ChatSessionStore.index` on startup. Orphan entries persist indefinitely.

4. **50-session hard limit in `ChatSessionStore.index`**: The `trimEntries()` method caps the index at 50 entries (hardcoded `nRo=50`). Orphan entries from deleted JSONL files consume slots in this limit, pushing out active sessions.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a VS Code extension that deletes `chatSessions/*.jsonl` files via `fs.unlink()`
2. Observe that deleted sessions still appear in the chat sidebar
3. Attempt to modify `state.vscdb` using `sql.js` or Python `sqlite3` — changes are overwritten when VS Code shuts down
4. Right-clicking the ghost entry and selecting "Delete" works correctly (uses internal API), but this cannot be done programmatically from an extension

## Expected Behavior

A public extension API such as:

This would allow extensions to:

- Delete the JSONL file
- Remove entries from `state.cache`, `model.cache`, and `ChatSessionStore.index`
- Update the sidebar immediately

## Additional Suggestions

1. **Startup self-healing**: On startup, verify that JSONL files exist for all `model.cache` / `ChatSessionStore.index` entries. Remove orphans automatically.
2. **Configurable index limit**: Allow users to configure the `ChatSessionStore.index` entry limit (currently hardcoded at 50).

## Environment

- VS Code version: 1.122.x (Insiders)
- OS: Windows 11

title: Claude Agent fails with `tools: Tool names must be unique` when memory tools conflict
document:
Claude Agent fails with `tools: Tool names must be unique` when memory tools conflict

## Bug: Claude Agent fails with `tools: Tool names must be unique` when memory tools conflict

### Summary

When using **Claude Sonnet Agent** through **GitHub Copilot in VS Code**, any request fails with a `400` error:

After troubleshooting, the issue appears to be caused by a conflict between the built-in VS Code memory tool and the `digitarald.agent-memory` extension. Both seem to register a memory-related tool with the same or overlapping tool name, causing the tool list sent to Claude to contain duplicates.

### Error message

### Environment

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open VS Code.
2. Enable GitHub Copilot Agent mode.
3. Select Claude Sonnet as the model.
4. Keep both memory tools enabled:

 * Built-in `vscode:memory` tool
 * `digitarald.agent-memory` extension
5. Send any request to Claude Agent.

### Expected behavior

Claude Agent should process the request normally.

### Actual behavior

Claude Agent fails immediately with:

### Confirmed cause / observation

The conflict appears to happen when both of these are enabled at the same time:

The `digitarald.agent-memory` extension provides its own memory-related tool, such as:

When the built-in `vscode:memory` tool is also enabled, Claude Agent appears to receive duplicate or overlapping tool definitions and rejects the request.

### Confirmed workarounds

Either of the following fixes the issue:

1. Disable the built-in `vscode:memory` tool from the Copilot Agent tools list, and rely on the `digitarald.agent-memory` extension instead.

2. Disable the `digitarald.agent-memory` extension, and use only the built-in VS Code memory tool.

### Additional troubleshooting already tried

The issue does not appear to be caused by MCP configuration.

I checked or tried the following:

* MCP discovery entries are disabled.
* `mcp.json` is blank.
* No custom MCP server is actively running.
* Disabling unrelated extensions did not fix the issue.
* The issue only happens with Claude Agent.
* Other models, such as Codex, work normally with the same VS Code setup.

### Notes

This seems to be a tool registration / tool naming conflict that specifically affects Claude Agent because Claude rejects duplicate tool names strictly.

It would be helpful if VS Code/Copilot could either:

* prevent duplicate tool names from being registered,
* namespace extension-provided tools more clearly,
* show a clearer warning when two tools conflict,
* or automatically disable/rename conflicting tools before sending the tool list to the model.

title: Copilot Chat blocks the extension host ~60–135s at startup
document:
Copilot Chat blocks the extension host ~60–135s at startup

- Copilot Chat Extension Version: 0.50.1
- VS Code Version: 1.122.1
- OS Version: Windows 11 (10.0.26200), Snapdragon X / ARM64 — reproduces on local Windows folders AND in Remote-WSL (Ubuntu 24.04, arm64)
- Feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): Extension activation / Copilot CLI session integration (Agent Sessions view). Occurs before any chat mode is used.
- Selected model (e.g. GPT 4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet): N/A — the hang is during activation, before a model is selected. Seat: Copilot for Business (`copilot_for_business_seat_multi_quota`).
- Logs:

 **Startup Performance — Copilot Chat ENABLED** (Finish Activate, ms):
 
 All unrelated eager extensions cluster at ~67–72s — they finish only when the host unblocks.

 **GitHub Copilot Chat output channel:**
 

 **Startup Performance — Copilot Chat DISABLED** (same machine/network/reload):
 

 **Each component is fast in isolation** (so it is not a single slow request — it is an internal Copilot CLI IDE-mode init stall):
 

## Description

On every window reload, `GitHub.copilot-chat` blocks for ~60–135s during activation while the Copilot CLI session integration (`CopilotCLIChatSessionContentProvider` → `listSessions`, gated behind the `onChatSession:*` activation) cold-starts its MCP server. The extension host is starved during this window, so every other eager extension's `activate()` is dragged out to the same ~60–70s and the window shows "activating extensions" for over a minute. Disabling GitHub Copilot Chat returns startup to ~2s and the other extensions to <2s. Reproduces on both local Windows and Remote-WSL, so it is not remote/WSL-specific.

Workarounds that did NOT help (only fully disabling Copilot Chat works): `http.proxySupport: off`; `github.copilot.chat.cli.showExternalSessions: false` + `github.copilot.chat.cli.mcp.enabled: false`; `chat.agentSessionsViewLocation: disabled` (the CLI session provider still spawns); GitHub sign-out; `--disable-extensions`.

1. Sign in to GitHub Copilot (Business seat) in VS Code 1.122.1 with GitHub Copilot Chat 0.50.1 enabled.
2. Open a workspace (local Windows folder or Remote-WSL) and reload the window (Developer: Reload Window).
3. Observe the window stays on "activating extensions" ~60–135s; run Developer: Startup Performance and see `GitHub.copilot-chat` Finish Activate ~60–130s with `[CopilotCLIChatSessionContentProvider] listSessions took ~60000–100000 ms`, and unrelated eager extensions (terraform, github-actions) dragged to the same time.
4. Disable GitHub Copilot Chat, reload — startup drops to ~2s and the other extensions to <2s.

title: Custom agent .agent.md cannot reference custom endpoint models (non-copilot vendor)
document:
Custom agent .agent.md cannot reference custom endpoint models (non-copilot vendor)

**VS Code Version:** All versions with custom agent support
**Extension:** GitHub Copilot Chat

## Problem

The `model` field in custom agent `.agent.md` frontmatter only resolves models from the `copilot` vendor. Custom endpoint models registered via `chatLanguageModels.json` use a different vendor (e.g., `customendpoint`) and cannot be referenced by agents.

### How custom endpoints are configured

Users can register custom language models through the `chatLanguageModels.json` configuration file. Each model has a `vendor` field — native Copilot models use `vendor: copilot`, while custom endpoints use `vendor: customendpoint`.

Example `chatLanguageModels.json` entry:



### What happens

When a custom agent `.agent.md` specifies a `model` field referencing a custom endpoint model, Copilot emits:



The model is silently ignored and the agent falls back to a default, which breaks deterministic behavior in orchestrated workflows where the orchestrator expects each phase agent to use a specific model.

### What was tried

The following formats were tested without success:

- `model: qwen36-msc1` (model id)
- `model: Qwen 3.6 MSC1` (display name)
- `model: qwen36-msc1@customendpoint` (vendor suffix)
- `model: [Qwen 3.6 MSC1, qwen36-msc1]` (array of options)

None of these are resolved by the agent system.

### Expected behavior

The `model` field in `.agent.md` should resolve models from **all registered vendors**, not just `copilot`. Possible approaches:

1. Resolve by model `id` across all vendors
2. Support a vendor-qualified format like `vendor/model-id`
3. Resolve by display `name` across all vendors

### Why this matters

This is critical for **orchestrator-based workflows** where:

- An orchestrator agent delegates work to phase agents (apply, design, verify, etc.)
- Each phase agent must use a **deterministic, pre-configured model**
- If the model is ignored, the orchestrator loses control over which model runs each phase
- This breaks reproducibility and can cause failures when phase agents require specific model capabilities (tool calling, context window size, etc.)

### Related

- `chatLanguageModels.json` configuration schema
- Custom agent `.agent.md` frontmatter schema
- Language Model Chat Provider API

title: GitHub Copilot Chat in WSL experiences 40-80 second startup delays due to CopilotCLIChatSessionContentProvider.listSessions
document:
GitHub Copilot Chat in WSL experiences 40-80 second startup delays due to CopilotCLIChatSessionContentProvider.listSessions

## Environment

* VS Code: 1.122.1
* GitHub Copilot Chat: 0.50.1
* WSL: Ubuntu
* OS Host: Windows 11
* Copilot account: Educational quota
* Remote Development: WSL

## Description

GitHub Copilot Chat takes approximately 40-80 seconds to become available when running inside WSL.

The delay is reproducible across VS Code restarts and occurs even after:

* Clearing Copilot state
* Clearing workspace storage
* Reinstalling Copilot Chat
* Disabling local indexing
* Reducing workspace index size
* Disabling memory features
* Disabling MCP features
* Optimizing Git settings
* Verifying filesystem permissions

The extension itself activates quickly, but startup appears blocked by `CopilotCLIChatSessionContentProvider.listSessions`.

## Observed Logs

Subsequent calls are fast:

The large delay occurs only once during startup.

## What Has Been Verified

### Extension activation is not the problem

VS Code reports:

### Network is not the problem

Result:

### Session database is tiny

### Session state is tiny

Result:

### Workspace indexing does not affect the issue

Tested:

No measurable improvement.

### Git is not the problem

Git operations complete in milliseconds and repository scanning is fast.

## Additional Observation

Copilot reports:

However, Copilot successfully creates:

which suggests the failing database may be a different internal store.

## Expected

Copilot Chat should become available within a few seconds, similar to Codex and other AI extensions running in the same WSL environment.

## Actual

Copilot Chat consistently requires 40-80 seconds before becoming usable due to an extended `listSessions` operation during startup.

## Reproducibility

100% reproducible across restarts.

title: Copilot Chat: Editing model on historical message is ignored when regenerating — always falls back to bottom selector model
document:
Copilot Chat: Editing model on historical message is ignored when regenerating — always falls back to bottom selector model

VS Code Stable: 1.122.1 works
VS Code Insiders: 1.124.0-insider / 8d1fba1 ... fails

In Copilot Chat, the bottom model selector is set to model A (e.g., gpt-5.5)
Send a message — it uses model A as expected
Edit the historical (already sent) message and change its model to model B (e.g., claude-4-sonnet)
Click send/regenerate on the edited historical message
Observe: The request still uses model A, ignoring the model change made on the historical message
Expected Behavior:

When a user edits the model on a historical message and regenerates, the regenerated response should use the model selected on that message (model B)
Actual Behavior:

The model override on the historical message is silently ignored
The request always falls back to whatever model is currently selected in the bottom model selector (model A)
Workaround:

First switch the bottom model selector to model B
Then edit the historical message's model to model B
Regenerate — it now uses model B
Additional Context:

title: Show process name in Call Stack when only one debug session is active
document:
Show process name in Call Stack when only one debug session is active

**Problem**
When only one debug process is running, VS Code shows a flat list 
of Thread #XXXX in the Call Stack without showing the process name. 
The process name only appears when two or more processes are running.

**Why this is a problem**
Some processes take a long time to start. During that time, if I 
forget which configuration I launched, there is no way to check — 
the name is simply not shown.

**Expected behavior**
Always show the process name as a top-level item in the Call Stack, 
regardless of how many processes are running.

**Suggested solution**
Add a setting like debug.alwaysShowProcessName: true to force 
the process name to always be visible in the Call Stack.

title: Allow the integrated browser to open URLs set in launch.json serverReadyAction.
document:
Allow the integrated browser to open URLs set in launch.json serverReadyAction.

Currently serverReadyAction in launch.json will always open in an external browser even when `workbench.browser.openLocalhostLinks` is set. 

This happens because "extensions\debug-server-ready\src\extension.ts" calls `vscode.env.openExternal` which takes only the URI as a parameter, and so the internal call to extHostWindow.openUri` defaults to having `options.allowContributedOpeners` set to undefined, so the `LocalhostLinkOpenerContribution` is never executed.

I'm going to create a pull request for this, and I considered three options when doing so. 

1. Adding the parameter `options?: { allowContributedOpeners?: boolean | string }` to `vscode.env.openExternal`
2. Changing the default of `allowContributedOpeners` in `vscode.env.openExternal` to true.
3. Add a new `serverReadyAction.type` enum option `openIntegratedBrowser`.

I created branches for both, but ultimately went with the third option for a couple of reasons:
* I decided against (1) because `vscode.env.openExternal` is a public API it would need to have a clearer option than `allowContributedOpeners`. Adding a new option something along the lines of `allowIntegratedBrowser` might work, but felt like a messy solution.
* I decided against (2) because extension developers might be relying on `vscode.env.openExternal` always being external, and changing it underneath them seems unfair and risky,
* I choose option (3) because even though it only fixes the issue for my narrow situation, it's entirely opt-in and therefor relatively low risk. This option means any URL, even non-localhost ones, can be opened in the internal browser.
